author: Ibn ʿAṭāʾ Allāh al-Iskandarī
title_fr: Kitāb al-Ḥikam — Le Livre des sagesses
title_en: Kitāb al-Ḥikam — The Book of Aphorisms

records.section_title: Chapter 1: Trust in God and the nature of action
records.french_translation: Losing hope when a slippage occurs is a sign of relying too much on (one’s own) deeds.
records.english_text_draft: Losing hope when a slippage occurs is a sign of relying too much on (one’s own) deeds.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Whoever loses hope after a fault reveals that he was attaching excessive importance to his own actions. The ordinary servant bases his salvation upon his works, whereas the gnostic is preoccupied with God Himself, not with the worth of his deeds.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Ibn 'Abbād al-Rundī explains that hope and fear must be balanced: to despair of divine mercy after a fault is as blameworthy as self-satisfaction after an act of obedience.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: This maxim invites us to shift our psychological centre of gravity from self-evaluation towards trust in God. A mistake is no catastrophe if one leans upon the divine grace.

records.section_title: Chapter 1: Trust in God and the nature of action
records.french_translation: Your aspiring for isolation when Allah puts you among the causes (of everyday living) is a covert ambition; and your aspiring for the causes (of everyday living) when Allah prefers isolation for you is a collapse from exalted steadfastness.
records.english_text_draft: Your aspiring for isolation when Allah puts you among the causes (of everyday living) is a covert ambition; and your aspiring for the causes (of everyday living) when Allah prefers isolation for you is a collapse from exalted steadfastness.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Each person has a unique role assigned by God. To wish to change one's situation (to seek seclusion when one is in the world, or the reverse) is a hidden form of ambition and a lack of trust in the divine wisdom. Moses could not live as Khiḍr did, and vice versa.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Aḥmad Zarrūq stresses that 'ambition is the bane of the initiate'. True servitude consists in flourishing within one's present situation.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: A powerful lesson against chronic dissatisfaction. Spiritual growth is not bound to a change of circumstances, but to the quality of presence one brings to one's present situation.

records.section_title: Chapter 1: Trust in God and the nature of action
records.french_translation: Intentions cannot intrude the walls of preordained things.
records.english_text_draft: Intentions cannot intrude the walls of preordained things.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: The human will is a means through which the divine decree unfolds. Man has free will, yet he cannot defy what has been foreordained. Success must not be interpreted as a personal victory over God.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Ibn 'Ajībah's commentary specifies that intention is a 'light' illuminating the act, yet it cannot alter the essence of the decree.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: This idea resolves the paradox of free will and predestination. Intention and effort are necessary, but the outcome belongs to God. This frees one from the anxiety of performance.

records.section_title: Chapter 1: Trust in God and the nature of action
records.french_translation: Don’t bother to think (about your worldly fortunes). Why do you undertake to do what someone else has already done on your behalf?
records.english_text_draft: Don’t bother to think (about your worldly fortunes). Why do you undertake to do what someone else has already done on your behalf?

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Man is too preoccupied with his material sustenance, when God has already made the necessary provision. Such anxiety betrays a lack of trust in God and an ignorance of one's mission upon the earth.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Al-Sharqāwī says: 'He who worries about his sustenance though it is guaranteed is like an employee redoing his master's work.'
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: A radical critique of the modern anxiety bound up with money and career. Trust in God allows one to be freed from worry so as to devote oneself to what is essential.

records.section_title: Chapter 1: Trust in God and the nature of action
records.french_translation: Don’t let the delay in getting the gift (answer), despite your persistent appeal, drive you to despair. For He has offered to reply to (your appeals) in a way He chooses for you rather than in a way you choose for yourself, and at a time He desires rather than at a time you desire.
records.english_text_draft: Don’t let the delay in getting the gift (answer), despite your persistent appeal, drive you to despair. For He has offered to reply to (your appeals) in a way He chooses for you rather than in a way you choose for yourself, and at a time He desires rather than at a time you desire.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Insistence upon an immediate answer to one's prayers reveals impatience and an inability to grasp the divine wisdom. God sometimes delays the answer in order to grant it in the best manner and at the best moment.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Ibn 'Abbād al-Rundī likens this impatience to that of a child demanding a fruit before it has ripened.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: A powerful antidote to the culture of immediacy. Prayer is not a machine that dispenses rewards, but a dialogue in which God remains master of the timing and the form.

records.section_title: Chapter 1: Trust in God and the nature of action
records.french_translation: Don’t let the non-fulfilment of a promise, even if the time of its fulfilment has been specified, make you suspicious of the promise.
records.english_text_draft: Don’t let the non-fulfilment of a promise, even if the time of its fulfilment has been specified, make you suspicious of the promise.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: A delay in the fulfilment of a divine promise does not mean that it will never be kept. Doubt in this regard weakens one's hope and one's inner light.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Aḥmad Zarrūq specifies that 'the divine promise is like the dawn: it always comes, but rarely at the hour we wish'.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Trust in God's promises (mercy, succour, recompense) must not be made conditional upon our timetables. It is an unconditional faith.

records.section_title: Chapter 1: Trust in God and the nature of action
records.french_translation: When Allah unfolds a realm of knowledge for you, get rid of the feeling that you are not qualified for that in terms of deeds.
records.english_text_draft: When Allah unfolds a realm of knowledge for you, get rid of the feeling that you are not qualified for that in terms of deeds.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: If God grants a knowledge of Himself, it is needless to fret over one's own works. Nothing can equal this divine gift. Knowledge of God is more powerful than actions in defining the spiritual life.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Ibn 'Ajībah says: 'Knowledge is a pure gift, not a reward. He who scorns it through false humility commits an injustice towards God.'
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: To feel oneself unworthy of a spiritual illumination can be a disguised form of pride. To accept God's gift with gratitude is humbler than to refuse it through self-deprecation.

records.section_title: Chapter 1: Trust in God and the nature of action
records.french_translation: Actions differ according to the differences in the circumstances of inspiration.
records.english_text_draft: Actions differ according to the differences in the circumstances of inspiration.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Actions are the outward manifestations of an inner process, of an interior inspiration. Each action is unique, for it is born of a specific spiritual context.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: The classical commentary insists that 'inspiration' (wārid) is the true source of all upright action.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: A profound vision of the psychology of action. Our acts are not isolated 'things', but expressions of our inner state in the moment.

records.section_title: Chapter 1: Trust in God and the nature of action
records.french_translation: Actions are but motionless forms, and their spirit (and sustenance) lies in the presence of a committed heart within.
records.english_text_draft: Actions are but motionless forms, and their spirit (and sustenance) lies in the presence of a committed heart within.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Without sincere devotion (ikhlāṣ), actions are dead forms. Sincerity gives life to the act. For the saint, this reaches as far as the extinction of the self.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Al-Sharqāwī compares action without sincerity to a body without a soul, a statue without life.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: At the heart of Islamic ethics: the quality of inner presence takes precedence over the quantity of acts. A small act done with a present heart is worth more than a thousand mechanical ones.

records.section_title: Chapter 1: Trust in God and the nature of action
records.french_translation: Bury your existence in the ground of insignificance because what sprouts out of an unburied seed will not yield good results.
records.english_text_draft: Bury your existence in the ground of insignificance because what sprouts out of an unburied seed will not yield good results.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Success attained without humility is fleeting. The seed must bury itself in the earth in order to become a tree. In the same way, man must bury his inclination towards reputation.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Ibn 'Abbād al-Rundī sees in this image a universal law: 'Lowliness is the path of elevation, humility the path of greatness'.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: A powerful metaphor against ambition and the seeking of recognition. True spiritual growth passes through a phase of humility and invisibility.

records.section_title: Chapter 1: Trust in God and the nature of action
records.french_translation: Nothing will be as beneficial to the heart as seclusion wherein it enters the domain of meditation.
records.english_text_draft: Nothing will be as beneficial to the heart as seclusion wherein it enters the domain of meditation.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Solitude creates a climate favourable to meditation. For thought to rise to the presence of God, it must be independent of worldly preoccupations.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: The Sufis distinguish 'outward solitude' (khalwa) from 'inward solitude' (uzla). Both are conditions necessary for meditation.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: In a hyperconnected world, the call to solitude is not a rejection of the social but a condition for recovering an interiority capable of reflection and wonder.

records.section_title: Chapter 1: Trust in God and the nature of action
records.french_translation: How can a heart be illuminated when its mirror reflects worldly figures only?
records.english_text_draft: How can a heart be illuminated when its mirror reflects worldly figures only?

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: The heart loses its transparency if it reflects nothing but worldly figures. The passions create obstacles upon the path towards God. Only a vigilant heart can advance.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Aḥmad Zarrūq says: 'The heart is a mirror: turned downward, it reflects the earth; turned upward, it reflects the heavens'.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: The metaphor of the mirror is most useful for understanding attention. That to which we give our attention 'colours' our heart. An exclusively worldly attention darkens spiritual perception.

records.section_title: Chapter 1: Trust in God and the nature of action
records.french_translation: The whole universe would have been enveloped in darkness had it not been lighted up by His manifestation.
records.english_text_draft: The whole universe would have been enveloped in darkness had it not been lighted up by His manifestation.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: It is the presence of God that illumines the whole universe. Whoever does not perceive this divine light lives in darkness. The 'clouds' of worldly things hide the sun of knowledge.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Ibn 'Ajībah explains that 'the divine manifestation (tajallī) is the sun of the soul; without it, all is darkness'.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: A panentheistic vision: God is not only the cause of the universe, He is its inner light. To perceive this light transforms one's gaze upon the world.

records.section_title: Chapter 1: Trust in God and the nature of action
records.french_translation: That He veils you from Himself with what has no real existence alongside Him is an indication of His omnipotence.
records.english_text_draft: That He veils you from Himself with what has no real existence alongside Him is an indication of His omnipotence.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Everything that seems to veil God has no true reality. Nothing can really hide God, for everything depends upon Him in order to exist.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Zarrūq specifies: 'The veil is not a thing, it is an absence of perception. God is nearer than anything, but we are distracted'.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: This maxim deconstructs the idea of a real 'distance' between man and God. The problem is not ontological, but perceptual. God is there, but we do not see Him.

records.section_title: Chapter 2: Time, predestination, the way
records.french_translation: It’s sheer stupidity to seek to introduce in a given time what Allah has not manifested in it.
records.english_text_draft: It’s sheer stupidity to seek to introduce in a given time what Allah has not manifested in it.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: To seek a moment other than the one God has chosen is an illusion. The servant must make the best of the present situation rather than wishing for another.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Ibn 'Abbād al-Rundī calls this 'ignorance of the present moment' (jahl al-waqt).
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: A powerful invitation to acceptance and to full awareness of the present moment. Regret for the past and anxiety about the future are forms of spiritual foolishness.

records.section_title: Chapter 2: Time, predestination, the way
records.french_translation: To postpone deeds until leisure time comes is one of the frivolities of the mind.
records.english_text_draft: To postpone deeds until leisure time comes is one of the frivolities of the mind.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: To postpone one's duties, awaiting an imagined 'free moment', is folly. Each instant has its own duties.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Al-Sharqāwī says: 'He who waits for free time will never have free time. Free time is an illusion'.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: A radical critique of procrastination, seen as a lack of spiritual lucidity. The moment for action is always now.

records.section_title: Chapter 2: Time, predestination, the way
records.french_translation: Do not ask Him to take you out of a situation so that you will be employed in a different situation.
records.english_text_draft: Do not ask Him to take you out of a situation so that you will be employed in a different situation.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: To ask God to change our situation amounts to doubting His choice. The sincere servant does not call into question the destiny that God has assigned him.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Zarrūq says: 'Discontent is the steed of pride. Trust is the steed of peace'.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: This maxim does not condemn legitimate action to improve one's condition. It condemns chronic inner dissatisfaction, complaint, and the inability to see the worth of one's present situation.

records.section_title: Chapter 2: Time, predestination, the way
records.french_translation: No sooner did a determined initiate want to stop at a particular revelation than an inner voice of reality called out to him: 'What you are looking for is still ahead of you.'
records.english_text_draft: No sooner did a determined initiate want to stop at a particular revelation than an inner voice of reality called out to him: 'What you are looking for is still ahead of you.'

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: The determined aspirant halts at no temporary station. An inner voice calls him back to order. The inner realities protect him from the illusions of the world.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: The Sufis speak of the 'hātif', that mysterious inner voice which guides the initiate upon the path.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: A magnificent image of the spiritual process: there is no 'final goal' to be reached once and for all. Each stage crossed reveals a new stage beyond it.

records.section_title: Chapter 2: Time, predestination, the way
records.french_translation: If you request Him it means you are suspicious about Him; if you seek Him it means you are absent from Him. If you seek someone else it means you are unabashed by (what you are doing to) Him; if you request to someone else it means you are distant from Him.
records.english_text_draft: If you request Him it means you are suspicious about Him; if you seek Him it means you are absent from Him. If you seek someone else it means you are unabashed by (what you are doing to) Him; if you request to someone else it means you are distant from Him.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Four degrees of straying: to ask of God, to seek God, to seek another, to ask of another. The further the servant moves away, the more he manifests his ignorance and his distance.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Ibn 'Ajībah specifies that these degrees are not absolute judgements but indicators of nearness to God.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: A ladder of spiritual presence. The prayer of petition is not condemned, but it reveals a certain level. The higher degrees are those of pure love, of silent contemplation.

records.section_title: Chapter 2: Time, predestination, the way
records.french_translation: There is not a single exhalation by you that is not predestined by Him.
records.english_text_draft: There is not a single exhalation by you that is not predestined by Him.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Every action, every breath, is foreordained by God. Man cannot ignore the worth of each second for his eternal salvation.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: A constant reminder in Sufism: predestination does not abolish responsibility, but roots everything in the divine will.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: An acute awareness of the worth of every instant. Nothing is 'insignificant' or 'lost' from the divine point of view. Each breath is an occasion for obedience or for neglect.

records.section_title: Chapter 2: Time, predestination, the way
records.french_translation: Do not wait to be free of others, because it may hamper your attentiveness to Him in that state He has assigned to you.
records.english_text_draft: Do not wait to be free of others, because it may hamper your attentiveness to Him in that state He has assigned to you.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: To await a moment free of otherness before acting is a culpable procrastination. Man must make the best of his present situation, however difficult.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Zarrūq compares this waiting to a farmer who would wait for perfect soil: 'He will never plant'.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: The call to cease awaiting ideal conditions. The spiritual life is lived amid imperfection, constraint, and otherness.

records.section_title: Chapter 2: Time, predestination, the way
records.french_translation: Do not consider the existence of grief strange as long as you live in this world.
records.english_text_draft: Do not consider the existence of grief strange as long as you live in this world.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Sadness is part of life in this world. The aspirant must not regard it as an insurmountable obstacle. He must be ready to sacrifice worldly gains.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: The Prophet said: 'This world is the prison of the believer and the paradise of the disbeliever'.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: To normalise suffering and sadness, without falling into pessimism, but accepting them as ordinary components of earthly existence.

records.section_title: Chapter 2: Time, predestination, the way
records.french_translation: No pursuit you make with the help of your Lord will stagnate, while all pursuits you make on your own will not be that easy.
records.english_text_draft: No pursuit you make with the help of your Lord will stagnate, while all pursuits you make on your own will not be that easy.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Without the help of God, no effort truly succeeds. The divine guidance frees the aspirant from his worries and his anguish.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Al-Sharqāwī says: 'With God, the mountain is a feather; without Him, the feather is a mountain'.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: A distinction between 'egotic' action (centred on the self) and 'theocentric' action (trusting in God). The latter alone bears lasting fruit.

records.section_title: Chapter 2: Time, predestination, the way
records.french_translation: Resorting to Allah in the very beginning is a symbol of ultimate success.
records.english_text_draft: Resorting to Allah in the very beginning is a symbol of ultimate success.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Every undertaking oriented towards God and rooted in Him from the outset is promised spiritual success.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Ibn 'Abbād sees here the key to success in this world and the next: 'The first intention determines the final outcome'.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: The importance of the foundational intention (niyya) that gives its direction to the whole of life. A life that begins with God will end with Him.

records.section_title: Chapter 2: Time, predestination, the way
records.french_translation: If one’s beginning was illuminated, one’s end will also be illuminated.
records.english_text_draft: If one’s beginning was illuminated, one’s end will also be illuminated.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: The greater the effort at the beginning of the spiritual journey, the more luminous and successful will be its end.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: A constant principle in the Sufi tradition: the first steps are crucial. The barakah of the beginning unfolds at the end.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: The spiritual law of initial effort. In all learning, the quality of the foundations determines the solidity of the edifice.

records.section_title: Chapter 2: Time, predestination, the way
records.french_translation: Whatever is reserved in the secret interiors (of one’s heart) will be visible through (one’s) external appearance.
records.english_text_draft: Whatever is reserved in the secret interiors (of one’s heart) will be visible through (one’s) external appearance.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: The inner light of the heart always ends by manifesting itself outwardly. The inner state can be read upon the face and in one's conduct.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Hadith: 'Piety is here (in the heart), but its effects are visible'.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: The impossibility of 'pretence' over the long term. What dwells in the heart ends by inscribing itself in the body, the gaze, and one's words.

records.section_title: Chapter 2: Time, predestination, the way
records.french_translation: What a difference between those who begin their inference from Allah as evidence and those who proceed to Allah from other evidences.
records.english_text_draft: What a difference between those who begin their inference from Allah as evidence and those who proceed to Allah from other evidences.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: To begin with God as the absolute proof yields certainties. To begin with creatures in order to prove God leads to uncertainty.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: The classical commentary contrasts the 'method of faith' (imān) with the 'method of proof' (istidlāl).
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: A spiritual epistemology: faith is not a conclusion, but a point of departure. To know God does not proceed from a syllogism, but from an inner light.

records.section_title: Chapter 2: Time, predestination, the way
records.french_translation: Those who have already reached Him belong to the 'let him who has abundance spend out of his abundance' group, while those who are still on a journey to Him belong to the 'whoever has his means of subsistence straitened to him' group.
records.english_text_draft: Those who have already reached Him belong to the 'let him who has abundance spend out of his abundance' group, while those who are still on a journey to Him belong to the 'whoever has his means of subsistence straitened to him' group.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Those who have reached God are in spiritual abundance (verse 65:7). Those still on the way always lack the means. A powerful metaphor.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Zarrūq explains that the abundance here is spiritual, not material. Those who have God have need of nothing else.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: A radical distinction between two kinds of spiritual life: the precariousness of the search and the fullness of the discovery.

records.section_title: Chapter 2: Time, predestination, the way
records.french_translation: Those who journey to Him are guided by the lights in their directions whereas those who have already reached Him are in front of the lights.
records.english_text_draft: Those who journey to Him are guided by the lights in their directions whereas those who have already reached Him are in front of the lights.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Those who travel follow the lights. Those who have arrived stand before the lights; they no longer have need of them. The light belongs to them; they do not belong to the light.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Ibn 'Ajībah says: 'The seeker uses the light; the one found is light himself'.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: The metaphor of light shows the transformation of being. First illumined, then becoming light. The culmination is no longer a quest, but a presence.

records.section_title: Chapter 3: Self-knowledge and the divine attributes
records.french_translation: To be on the lookout for the deficiencies inherent in your self is better than bothering about transcendental things.
records.english_text_draft: To be on the lookout for the deficiencies inherent in your self is better than bothering about transcendental things.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: To seek out and correct one's own faults is more important than to speculate upon the transcendent realities. The one is a duty of servanthood, the other a personal curiosity.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: A fundamental principle of Sufism: 'He who knows himself knows his Lord'.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: A spiritual pragmatism: before seeking to pierce the divine mysteries, attend to your own faults. Ethical introspection is the true path towards the knowledge of God.

records.section_title: Chapter 3: Self-knowledge and the divine attributes
records.french_translation: It is not the Real who is veiled from you but rather you who are veiled from seeing Him.
records.english_text_draft: It is not the Real who is veiled from you but rather you who are veiled from seeing Him.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: The problem is not a veil on God's side, but a veil within our own perception. God can be hidden by nothing; only our limitations prevent us from seeing Him.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: The Quran: 'Vision perceives Him not, but He perceives all vision' (6:103).
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: A radical reversal. Do not seek to 'lift the veil' on God's side, but to heal your own blindness. The divine light is omnipresent; it is our gaze that is darkened.

records.section_title: Chapter 3: Self-knowledge and the divine attributes
records.french_translation: Divest yourself of all human attributes that are not in harmony with your servanthood so that you can be responsive to the call of Allah and closer to His presence.
records.english_text_draft: Divest yourself of all human attributes that are not in harmony with your servanthood so that you can be responsive to the call of Allah and closer to His presence.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: To attain the perfection of servanthood, one must eliminate the human traits incompatible with it (pride, dishonesty, and the like).
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: The Sufis speak of 'stripping away' (tajrīd) as an indispensable condition of the spiritual journey.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Divestment is not a negation of humanity, but a purification. It is a matter of casting off the attributes that distance us from God, not of ceasing to be human.

records.section_title: Chapter 3: Self-knowledge and the divine attributes
records.french_translation: All types of disobedience and negligence can be traced to self-satisfaction, while all sorts of obedience, vigilance and righteousness stem from self dissatisfaction.
records.english_text_draft: All types of disobedience and negligence can be traced to self-satisfaction, while all sorts of obedience, vigilance and righteousness stem from self dissatisfaction.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Self-satisfaction is the source of all negligence. Dissatisfaction with oneself is the source of all vigilance. Better an unsatisfied ignorant than a self-satisfied scholar.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Zarrūq says: 'Self-satisfaction is the sleep of the heart; discontent is its awakening'.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Self-satisfaction blocks growth. Dissatisfaction with oneself, rightly understood (without morbid anxiety), is the engine of all spiritual and moral progress.

records.section_title: Chapter 3: Self-knowledge and the divine attributes
records.french_translation: The ray of intellect helps you experience His proximity to you. The eye of intellect helps you experience your non-existence because of His existence. The truth of intellect helps you experience His existence only, irrespective of your existence or non-existence.
records.english_text_draft: The ray of intellect helps you experience His proximity to you. The eye of intellect helps you experience your non-existence because of His existence. The truth of intellect helps you experience His existence only, irrespective of your existence or non-existence.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Three stages: 1) the ray of the intellect: the experience of divine proximity. 2) the eye of the intellect: the experience of one's own non-existence before the existence of God. 3) the truth of the intellect: the experience of the existence of God alone, regardless of one's own.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Ibn 'Abbād al-Rundī sees in these three stages the degrees of the spiritual intellect (al-'aql al-munawwar).
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: A progression in self-decentring. First, I sense God near. Then, I efface myself before Him. Finally, I see none but Him. The self becomes transparent.

records.section_title: Chapter 3: Self-knowledge and the divine attributes
records.french_translation: Allah was, and there was nothing with Him, and He is now as He was.
records.english_text_draft: Allah was, and there was nothing with Him, and He is now as He was.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: The existence of God precedes all things and has not changed. All else is insignificant before this eternal reality.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: A classical formula (kāna Allāh wa lā shay'a ma'ah) reaffirming the absolute divine transcendence.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: The awareness that the universe is an 'accident' within the divine eternity. Nothing adds to nor takes from God. This radically relativises every worldly preoccupation.

records.section_title: Chapter 4: Centring on God
records.french_translation: Let not the focus of your aspiration deviate from Him, because no hope can outstrip the Generous.
records.english_text_draft: Let not the focus of your aspiration deviate from Him, because no hope can outstrip the Generous.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Never turn one's attention away from God. He alone is the refuge of hope. To hope in anything other than Him is an illusion.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: The divine name 'al-Karīm' (the Generous) is here invoked: His generosity surpasses every other hope.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Hope is not a vague emotion, but an orientation of the whole life. To centre one's hope upon God is to cease expecting from creatures what they cannot give.

records.section_title: Chapter 4: Centring on God
records.french_translation: Appeal to none but Him to dispose of a problem that He Himself has brought about.
records.english_text_draft: Appeal to none but Him to dispose of a problem that He Himself has brought about.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: It is God who creates the trials. He alone can relieve them. To ask another, who is himself powerless, is absurd.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: A constant reminder of tawhīd: oneness in the creation of causes and effects.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: An implacable logic: if God is the sole Agent, to whom else should one turn? Prayer to God alone is not one option among others; it is the only coherence.

records.section_title: Chapter 4: Centring on God
records.french_translation: If His good attributes are not sufficient for you to improve your impression of Him, let the way He treats you improve your impression of Him.
records.english_text_draft: If His good attributes are not sufficient for you to improve your impression of Him, let the way He treats you improve your impression of Him.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: If the attributes of God do not suffice to forge a good opinion of Him, then look upon His concrete blessings. He has granted nothing but good.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Al-Sharqāwī says: 'God makes Himself known through His names and His acts. The names speak of His nature, the acts of His goodness'.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: The personal experience of the divine blessings is a proof accessible to all. Even without learned theology, each person can observe the goodness of God in his life.

records.section_title: Chapter 4: Centring on God
records.french_translation: It is all the more surprising to see one flee from where there is no escape and search for what is ephemeral.
records.english_text_draft: It is all the more surprising to see one flee from where there is no escape and search for what is ephemeral.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: None can flee from God. To seek to flee Him is vain. To pursue the fleeting gains of the world at the expense of the eternal is an absurdity.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: The Quran: 'Wherever you turn, there is the Face of God' (2:115).
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: The existential absurdity of the man who agitates himself for things doomed to vanish, when he cannot escape God. An invitation to cease fleeing.

records.section_title: Chapter 4: Centring on God
records.french_translation: Do not travel from creature to creature as if you were a donkey in the mill, its destination always the starting point itself. You had better travel from creatures to the Creator.
records.english_text_draft: Do not travel from creature to creature as if you were a donkey in the mill, its destination always the starting point itself. You had better travel from creatures to the Creator.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Do not go round in circles from one creature to another, like a mill-donkey. One must journey from the creatures to the Creator. Thought must rise from the world to God.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: The metaphor of the donkey is famous in Sufism. The initiate then cites the hadith of the hijra (migration): 'he whose hijra is for Allah...'
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Static thought goes round in circles. Dynamic thought rises. The aim is not to accumulate knowledge about creatures, but to rise through them towards the Creator.

records.section_title: Chapter 5: Friendship, the spirit and the dhikr
records.french_translation: Do not associate with anyone whose circumstances do not awaken you and whose words do not guide you to Allah.
records.english_text_draft: Do not associate with anyone whose circumstances do not awaken you and whose words do not guide you to Allah.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: The choice of companions is crucial for the spiritual life. One must associate with those who awaken and guide towards God, not with those who distract.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: A universal ethical principle in Islam: 'A man follows the religion of his friend' (hadith).
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: The responsibility of choosing one's environment (friends, readings, media) with discernment. What surrounds us forms us. One must cultivate relationships that elevate.

records.section_title: Chapter 5: Friendship, the spirit and the dhikr
records.french_translation: If you are bad, your association with one who is worse will show you your own virtues (in good light).
records.english_text_draft: If you are bad, your association with one who is worse will show you your own virtues (in good light).

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Compared with one worse than himself, the bad man can become aware of his (relatively) good qualities. This can help him to recognise and to cultivate them.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: A form of teaching by contrast. The Prophet said: 'Do not compare your sins to those of others'.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: A very real psychological effect. Beware, however, of using this comparison to reassure oneself within one's mediocrity. The aim is progress, not complacency.

records.section_title: Chapter 5: Friendship, the spirit and the dhikr
records.french_translation: Hardly scarce are deeds emanating from an ascetic mind and hardly abundant are deeds originating from an avaricious mind.
records.english_text_draft: Hardly scarce are deeds emanating from an ascetic mind and hardly abundant are deeds originating from an avaricious mind.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: The deeds of the ascetic (zāhid), even when few, are more precious than the many deeds of the covetous (ḥirṣ). The ascetic acts with sincerity, the covetous with calculation.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Ibn 'Abbād distinguishes the 'quality' (kayfiyya) from the 'quantity' (kamiyya) of acts.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: The true worth of an act lies in the state of mind that produces it. A small act done with detachment and presence is worth more than a great accumulation of mechanical acts.

records.section_title: Chapter 5: Friendship, the spirit and the dhikr
records.french_translation: Good works are the fruits of good circumstances; and good circumstances are achieved through access to the realms of spiritual realisation.
records.english_text_draft: Good works are the fruits of good circumstances; and good circumstances are achieved through access to the realms of spiritual realisation.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Good actions are the fruit of good inner circumstances, which themselves arise from an openness to the spiritual realities (futūḥ).
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Imam Ghazālī: 'Right knowledge yields the right state, the right state yields right action'.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: One cannot mechanically produce good actions without a prior work upon one's inner state. Spiritual transformation is a condition of authentic action.

records.section_title: Chapter 5: Friendship, the spirit and the dhikr
records.french_translation: Do not give up Allah’s remembrance (dhikr) even though your mind is not in harmony with Allah. Forgetting His remembrance is worse than forgetting during His remembrance.
records.english_text_draft: Do not give up Allah’s remembrance (dhikr) even though your mind is not in harmony with Allah. Forgetting His remembrance is worse than forgetting during His remembrance.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: One must persevere in dhikr, even with a distracted mind. Better a distracted dhikr than no dhikr at all. Progress is made by stages.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: The four stages: heedless dhikr → vigilant dhikr → dhikr with presence → dhikr with absence of all save the One remembered.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: A progressive pedagogy. Constancy takes precedence over intensity. The mere act of repeating the name of God, even amid distraction, prepares the heart for a deeper presence.

records.section_title: Chapter 6: The death of the heart and hope
records.french_translation: If you neither feel regret about the opportunities of obedience you lost nor feel remorse for the mistakes you committed, it signifies the demise of your heart.
records.english_text_draft: If you neither feel regret about the opportunities of obedience you lost nor feel remorse for the mistakes you committed, it signifies the demise of your heart.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: The living heart regrets the missed occasions of obedience and the faults committed. Indifference is a sign of spiritual death.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: The Prophet: 'The believer is he who rejoices in his good deeds and grieves over his bad ones'.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Moral sensitivity is the vital sign of the soul. To lose this sensitivity is to cease being fully human.

records.section_title: Chapter 6: The death of the heart and hope
records.french_translation: Let no sin generate such gravity in your mind that it deprives you of your good opinion about Allah. He who knows his Lord truly, belittles his sin in front of His generosity.
records.english_text_draft: Let no sin generate such gravity in your mind that it deprives you of your good opinion about Allah. He who knows his Lord truly, belittles his sin in front of His generosity.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Do not despair of the divine mercy on account of a sin. The true knower of God relativises his sin before the generosity of his Lord.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: A subtle balance between fear (khawf) and hope (rajā'). Despair is as blameworthy as self-satisfaction.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: The gravity of sin must never eclipse the greatness of mercy. Humility consists in acknowledging one's fault, but trust consists in not despairing of forgiveness.

records.section_title: Chapter 6: The death of the heart and hope
records.french_translation: None of your sins will be trivial in front of His justice, and none of your sins will be severe in front of His grace.
records.english_text_draft: None of your sins will be trivial in front of His justice, and none of your sins will be severe in front of His grace.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Before the divine justice, every sin is grave. Before the divine grace, no sin is beyond pardon. Both perspectives are true.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Zarrūq says: 'Look upon your sin with the eye of fear, but look upon your Lord with the eye of hope'.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Do not absolutise either the gravity of sin (to the point of despair) or its lightness (to the point of complacency). Hold the tension between justice and grace.

records.section_title: Chapter 6: The death of the heart and hope
records.french_translation: Deeds which you are not aware of and which you consider trivial are more likely to be accepted (by Allah).
records.english_text_draft: Deeds which you are not aware of and which you consider trivial are more likely to be accepted (by Allah).

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: The acts one considers insignificant, done without boasting, are more likely to be accepted than those of which one is proud. Pride spoils the action.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Humility (tawāḍu') is the condition of acceptance. 'God loves the discreet act'.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: The attention given to evaluating one's own acts can be a trap. The accepted act is often the one we have forgotten, the one we did not 'calculate'.

records.section_title: Chapter 6: The death of the heart and hope
records.french_translation: He only brought you an inspiration so that you can approach Him.
records.english_text_draft: He only brought you an inspiration so that you can approach Him.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: The divine inspiration (wārid) has as its purpose the purification of the servant's heart, that he may draw near to God.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: The Sufis distinguish 'inspiration' (wārid) from 'state' (hāl). Inspiration is the first awakening.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Inspiration is not an end in itself, but a means. It aims to set us in motion towards God.

records.section_title: Chapter 6: The death of the heart and hope
records.french_translation: He brought you an inspiration so as to save you from the grip of vicissitudes and from the bondage of worldly things.
records.english_text_draft: He brought you an inspiration so as to save you from the grip of vicissitudes and from the bondage of worldly things.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Divine inspiration frees the spirit from worldly preoccupations and from the vicissitudes that hold it captive.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: The world (dunyā) is a prison for the believer. Inspiration is the key.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Our spirit is often chained to cares, projects, anxieties. Spiritual inspiration is a breach, a liberation.

records.section_title: Chapter 6: The death of the heart and hope
records.french_translation: He brought you an inspiration so as to liberate you from the prison of your existence to the space of contemplation.
records.english_text_draft: He brought you an inspiration so as to liberate you from the prison of your existence to the space of contemplation.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Inspiration carries one from ordinary egoic existence into the open space of contemplation (mushāhada).
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Ibn 'Ajībah: 'The ego is a cell; contemplation is the garden'.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: A powerful image of liberation. The ordinary state is a prison. Spiritual awakening is the opening of the door.

records.section_title: Chapter 6: The death of the heart and hope
records.french_translation: (Divine) lights are the horsepower of hearts and innermost secrets.
records.english_text_draft: (Divine) lights are the horsepower of hearts and innermost secrets.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: The divine light is the moving force that allows the heart and the inner secrets to rise towards God.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: A classical metaphor: the lights (anwār) are the mounts of souls.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Without this spiritual energy, the heart stays motionless. Asceticism and prayer are means of receiving this 'energy'.

records.section_title: Chapter 6: The death of the heart and hope
records.french_translation: Light is the army of the heart, while darkness is the army of the self. So when Allah wants to help His servant, He will extend to him the forces of light and will cut him off from the forces of darkness and vicissitudes.
records.english_text_draft: Light is the army of the heart, while darkness is the army of the self. So when Allah wants to help His servant, He will extend to him the forces of light and will cut him off from the forces of darkness and vicissitudes.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Two forces contend within man: light (the heart) and darkness (the ego). God aids His servant through the light.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Sufism speaks of the 'struggle against the soul' (jihād al-nafs). The forces of light are the divine aids (madad).
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: A dramatic vision of the inner life as a battlefield. Divine help is decisive. Without Him, the ego triumphs.

records.section_title: Chapter 6: The death of the heart and hope
records.french_translation: Light generates illumination, and insight generates wisdom. It is up to the heart to decide on progression or regression.
records.english_text_draft: Light generates illumination, and insight generates wisdom. It is up to the heart to decide on progression or regression.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: The light illumines, the intellect judges, the heart decides. The free will of the heart is central.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: The three faculties: nūr (light), 'aql (intellect), qalb (heart). The heart is the master.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: It is not enough to 'know' (intellect) or to 'feel' (light). One must 'decide' (heart). Spiritual freedom is a responsibility.

records.section_title: Chapter 6: The death of the heart and hope
records.french_translation: Let your obedience please you not because it has stemmed from you but because it has originated from Allah to you.
records.english_text_draft: Let your obedience please you not because it has stemmed from you but because it has originated from Allah to you.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Two delights may follow a good deed: rejoicing that God has chosen you (good), or rejoicing in yourself (bad).
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Verse 10:58: 'Let them rejoice in the grace and mercy of God'.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: The subtle distinction between gratitude (shukr) and self-satisfaction ('ujb). The believer rejoices in God's gift, not in his own merit.

records.section_title: Chapter 6: The death of the heart and hope
records.french_translation: He prevents both those who are still journeying to Him and those who have already reached Him from seeing their deeds and witnessing their circumstances.
records.english_text_draft: He prevents both those who are still journeying to Him and those who have already reached Him from seeing their deeds and witnessing their circumstances.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: God keeps both the seekers (through their dissatisfaction) and the gnostics (through their contemplation) from clinging to their works.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Seekers doubt their acts; the gnostics are absorbed in God. In both cases, the ego does not seize hold of the act.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: God protects them both from a common trap: pride. By two different paths (doubt and contemplation), He keeps them humble.

records.section_title: Chapter 7: Ambitions, disappointments and trials
records.french_translation: Had it not been for the seeds of ambition, the boughs of disgrace could not have grown that lofty.
records.english_text_draft: Had it not been for the seeds of ambition, the boughs of disgrace could not have grown that lofty.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Ambition is the root of every disgrace. The ambitious man does not hesitate to commit blameworthy acts in order to reach his worldly ends. The greater the ambition, the steeper the fall.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Zarrūq: 'Ambition (himma) is double-edged: good if it is for God, evil if it is for the world. Worldly ambition builds its own gallows.'
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Boundless ambition builds the ladder of its own downfall. Humility and moderation guard against disgrace. What climbs too high ends by crashing down.

records.section_title: Chapter 7: Ambitions, disappointments and trials
records.french_translation: Nothing has misled you as your delusions.
records.english_text_draft: Nothing has misled you as your delusions.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Man is led astray by his illusions and his suspicions. His failure to know himself, his life and his ends has turned him aside. The worst veil is not outer but inner.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Ibn 'Abbād: 'Illusion (wahm) is the thickest veil between man and the truth. It turns the false into the true and the true into the false.'
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Our mental projections, our false beliefs about ourselves and the world, are the greatest obstacles to truth. We are never so deceived as by ourselves.

records.section_title: Chapter 7: Ambitions, disappointments and trials
records.french_translation: You are free from what you have despaired of, while you are slave to what you crave for.
records.english_text_draft: You are free from what you have despaired of, while you are slave to what you crave for.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Man is the prisoner of his ambitions and his desires. He is free of whatever he has despaired of (for he no longer thinks of it), but a slave to whatever he still pursues. Desire is a chain.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: A universal wisdom: desire is a chain, detachment a liberation. The Prophet said: 'Look to those beneath you, do not look to those above you.'
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Our freedom is inversely proportional to the intensity of our unfulfilled desires. Let us detach ourselves from what we cannot control, and so recover our freedom.

records.section_title: Chapter 7: Ambitions, disappointments and trials
records.french_translation: Those who are not drawn to Allah through His affectionate caress will be led to Him shackled with adversities.
records.english_text_draft: Those who are not drawn to Allah through His affectionate caress will be led to Him shackled with adversities.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Some are drawn to God by His favours (tender caresses, eases granted). Others, who do not answer that gentle call, are dragged by trials and adversities. God uses two pedagogies: love or pain.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Al-Sharqāwī: 'God is so eager to draw us to Him that He uses two methods: the gentle call of well-being or the lash of hardship. What matters is to arrive.'
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: The hardships of life are not always punishments. Sometimes they are pressing invitations, somewhat rough reminders, to turn us towards God when gentleness has not sufficed.

records.section_title: Chapter 7: Ambitions, disappointments and trials
records.french_translation: He who is not grateful for the graces (of Allah) runs the risk of losing them, while he who is grateful for them fastens them with their own ropes.
records.english_text_draft: He who is not grateful for the graces (of Allah) runs the risk of losing them, while he who is grateful for them fastens them with their own ropes.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Ingratitude risks losing the favours bestowed. Gratitude binds them fast, as with a cord, so that they cannot slip away. Gratitude is a protection and an increase.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: The Quran: 'If you are grateful, I shall give you more' (14:7). Gratitude draws increase; ingratitude draws loss.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Gratitude is not merely a moral virtue, it is a strategy of preservation and increase. A favour received with gratitude takes root and multiplies.

records.section_title: Chapter 7: Ambitions, disappointments and trials
records.french_translation: Beware of His bounties offered to you while you are still on bad terms with Him, gradually leading you to a nemesis. 'Those who reject Our signs, We shall gradually visit with punishment, in ways they perceive not'.
records.english_text_draft: Beware of His bounties offered to you while you are still on bad terms with Him, gradually leading you to a nemesis. 'Those who reject Our signs, We shall gradually visit with punishment, in ways they perceive not'.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Beware of favours granted while one is on bad terms with God. They may be a lure, a gradual punishment (istidrāj). God gives the sinner what he desires so as to sink him deeper into his straying.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: The concept of istidrāj: God grants favours to one who persists in sin, not out of generosity, but to blind him and lead him gradually to ruin.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Material success is not always a sign of divine favour. It may be a test, even a punishment in disguise. Do not confuse prosperity with blessing.

records.section_title: Chapter 7: Ambitions, disappointments and trials
records.french_translation: It's foolish for a novice to say, if the punishment for his misdemeanour got delayed, that if it was a misdemeanour, He would have cut off help and forced isolation on him. Help could be taken away from him in a way he does not understand, maybe through denying further increase in it. And he could be kept isolated in a way he does not know, maybe through being given freedom to do what he pleases.
records.english_text_draft: It's foolish for a novice to say, if the punishment for his misdemeanour got delayed, that if it was a misdemeanour, He would have cut off help and forced isolation on him. Help could be taken away from him in a way he does not understand, maybe through denying further increase in it. And he could be kept isolated in a way he does not know, maybe through being given freedom to do what he pleases.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: A delay in punishment does not mean the absence of sanction. God may punish by withholding increase (spiritual stagnation) or by leaving the passions unbridled (isolation through abandonment). The worst chastisement is sometimes to be left to one's own freedom.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Zarrūq warns against the illusion of believing that the absence of immediate chastisement is approval. 'The greatest chastisement is to be ignored by God'.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Do not read the absence of visible consequences as a validation. Divine punishments are sometimes subtle: stagnation, indifference, a deceptive freedom.

records.section_title: Chapter 7: Ambitions, disappointments and trials
records.french_translation: If you see a servant whom Allah has made remain reciting the litanies and extended His help therein, do not disregard what his Lord has bestowed on him because you could not notice in him signs of gnostics or the delight of lovers. For had it not been for an inspiration, there would have been no litany.
records.english_text_draft: If you see a servant whom Allah has made remain reciting the litanies and extended His help therein, do not disregard what his Lord has bestowed on him because you could not notice in him signs of gnostics or the delight of lovers. For had it not been for an inspiration, there would have been no litany.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Do not look down on one who recites his litanies with constancy, even if you do not see in him the outward signs of the gnostics (miracles, ecstasies). The litany itself is already a divine inspiration. Constancy in practice is an immense favour.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: A call not to judge others by external criteria. Divine grace is hidden. Ibn 'Ajībah: 'Despise no worshipper, for his litany may be more accepted than your exalted states.'
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Constancy in practice, even without visible 'ecstasy', is already an immense grace. Do not belittle 'ordinary' practices. Faithfulness to the litany is a sign of election.

records.section_title: Chapter 7: Ambitions, disappointments and trials
records.french_translation: Allah selects some people for His service and He reserves some others for loving Him. 'Of the bounties of Your Lord, We bestow freely on all—these as well as those: The bounties of Your Lord are not closed (to anyone)'.
records.english_text_draft: Allah selects some people for His service and He reserves some others for loving Him. 'Of the bounties of Your Lord, We bestow freely on all—these as well as those: The bounties of Your Lord are not closed (to anyone)'.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: God chooses some for His service ('ibāda, obedience) and others for His love (maḥabba, intimacy). Both are gifts. One must not set them in a hierarchy. Verse 17:20 underlines that God's gifts are for all, without exclusion.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: There are different paths to God: devoted service (the worshipper, the ascetic) or intense love (the lover, the gnostic). Neither is superior to the other in itself. Each receives according to his nature.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Do not compare your path with that of others. Some serve, others love. God gives to each. Hierarchical judgement is an illusion. What matters is to be within the relationship.

records.section_title: Chapter 8: Inspiration, wisdom, reward
records.french_translation: It is rare that divine inspiration comes, except suddenly, so that servants cannot claim that it comes by virtue of their preparations.
records.english_text_draft: It is rare that divine inspiration comes, except suddenly, so that servants cannot claim that it comes by virtue of their preparations.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Divine inspiration (wārid) comes suddenly, without warning, so that none may ascribe it to his own preparations or merits. It is pure grace, not a reward. Man can neither provoke it nor control it.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Sufism insists on the gratuitousness of inspiration. Zarrūq: 'The wārid is not a harvest, it is a gift. You may prepare the field, but the rain remains a bestowal.'
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: The seeker's humility lies in knowing that even his efforts are gifts. Inspiration cannot be 'scheduled'. It cannot be earned. One can only be ready, without claiming credit for its arrival.

records.section_title: Chapter 8: Inspiration, wisdom, reward
records.french_translation: If you see someone answering whatever he is asked, speaking of whatever he witnesses and talking about whatever he knows, it betokens his ignorance.
records.english_text_draft: If you see someone answering whatever he is asked, speaking of whatever he witnesses and talking about whatever he knows, it betokens his ignorance.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: He who claims to know everything, to answer everything, to speak of all he sees and knows, is in truth an ignorant man. The wise man knows his limits. He knows when to be silent. Apparent omniscience is a mask for ignorance.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Universal wisdom: Socrates said 'All I know is that I know nothing'. In the Islamic tradition, Abū Bakr said 'What heaven would shelter me, what earth would bear me, were I to say what I do not know'.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: True knowledge includes awareness of one's own ignorance. Apparent omniscience is a sign of foolishness. Silence is often wiser than speech.

records.section_title: Chapter 8: Inspiration, wisdom, reward
records.french_translation: He chose the hereafter as a place to reward His believing servants because this world is not wide enough to contain what He wants to confer on them and because He deemed their status too great to be rewarded in a world which is not eternal.
records.english_text_draft: He chose the hereafter as a place to reward His believing servants because this world is not wide enough to contain what He wants to confer on them and because He deemed their status too great to be rewarded in a world which is not eternal.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: This world is too narrow (too limited) and too fleeting to contain the reward of the believers. The hereafter is the space fitted to the grandeur of the divine recompense. Earthly rewards would be no more than an affront.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: The Quran: 'No soul knows what comfort of the eyes has been kept hidden for them, as a reward for what they used to do' (32:17). Ibn 'Abbād: 'The world is too small for the justice of God.'
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Do not expect a full recompense here below. The modesty of earthly rewards befits the smallness of the world. The hereafter is the space of true justice, of full reward.

records.section_title: Chapter 8: Inspiration, wisdom, reward
records.french_translation: If one gets the fruits of one's deeds shortly (in this world), it is a confirmation of their acceptance in the long run (hereafter).
records.english_text_draft: If one gets the fruits of one's deeds shortly (in this world), it is a confirmation of their acceptance in the long run (hereafter).

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: The first fruits of good deeds in this world (inner peace, ease, sweetness) are a foretaste and a confirmation of their acceptance in the hereafter. They are instalments on the final reward.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: A divine promise: 'Whoever does a good deed, We shall return it to him better.' The Sufis call these fruits 'the anticipated paradise' (jannah mu'ajjala).
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: The small joys, the inner satisfactions that follow a good deed are forerunning signs. They are not the whole reward, but they confirm that the deed is accepted. This is a source of hope.

records.section_title: Chapter 8: Inspiration, wisdom, reward
records.french_translation: If you want to know your status with Him, take a look at where He made you stay.
records.english_text_draft: If you want to know your status with Him, take a look at where He made you stay.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Your condition in this world (the opportunities, eases or difficulties God has given you) is an indicator of your standing with Him. Not in terms of wealth, but in terms of spiritual nearness.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Ibn 'Abbād: 'God places each according to his nearness to Him. Look not at possessions, look at the state of the heart.' This is not a quantitative measure, but a qualitative one.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Our surroundings, our spiritual eases or difficulties are mirrors of our relationship with God. If God keeps you in obedience, that is a sign. If you are in heedlessness, that too is a sign.

records.section_title: Chapter 8: Inspiration, wisdom, reward
records.french_translation: Whenever He bestows on you obedience, making you not concerned about it because of Him, then feel sure that He has conferred on you His blessings both outwardly and inwardly.
records.english_text_draft: Whenever He bestows on you obedience, making you not concerned about it because of Him, then feel sure that He has conferred on you His blessings both outwardly and inwardly.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: If God grants you obedience while you remain unaware of it (you do not think of it, you take no pride in it) through absorption in Him, this is the sign of an outer blessing (obedience itself) and an inner one (the presence that makes you forget your act).
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: The perfection of servitude is to be obedient without even thinking of it, through love and presence. Zarrūq: 'The transparent act is the perfect act, the one not even seen.'
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: The highest state is the one in which obedience becomes natural, transparent, without self-congratulation. One obeys as one breathes, without noticing. This is the sign of a twofold blessing.

records.section_title: Chapter 9: Repentance, hope, contraction/expansion
records.french_translation: The best you can solicit from Him is what He seeks from you.
records.english_text_draft: The best you can solicit from Him is what He seeks from you.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: The best thing to ask of God is not what you desire (wealth, health, paradise), but what He awaits of you: obedience, sincerity, presence, love. To align your request with the divine desire is to enter perfect harmony.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Zarrūq: 'The servant who asks for what God wills is in perfect harmony. He no longer asks for his ego, but for his Lord.'
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Change the content of your prayer: do not ask for 'what I want' but for 'what You want of me'. This is the passage from self-centredness to God-centredness.

records.section_title: Chapter 9: Repentance, hope, contraction/expansion
records.french_translation: It is a travesty of penitence to regret the loss of opportunities of obedience without attempting to salvage the situation.
records.english_text_draft: It is a travesty of penitence to regret the loss of opportunities of obedience without attempting to salvage the situation.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Repentance without action to make amends is vain, a sham. It must be joined to a sincere resolve and to concrete efforts to recover, to compensate, to change. Tears without deeds are barren.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Repentance (tawba) in Islam has three conditions: to cease the sin, to feel regret, and to resolve not to return to it. Ibn 'Ajībah adds: 'And to make amends, if possible.'
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Barren regret is not repentance. To weep over one's faults while changing nothing is mere theatre. True penitence is shown in a visible change of conduct.

records.section_title: Chapter 9: Repentance, hope, contraction/expansion
records.french_translation: A true gnostic (the enlightened servant) is not the one who, when he alludes (to one of the secrets of Allah), finds Allah closer to him than the allusion. But a gnostic is the one who has no allusion at all because of his extinction in His being and his absorption in contemplating Him.
records.english_text_draft: A true gnostic (the enlightened servant) is not the one who, when he alludes (to one of the secrets of Allah), finds Allah closer to him than the allusion. But a gnostic is the one who has no allusion at all because of his extinction in His being and his absorption in contemplating Him.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: The true gnostic needs no allusions, symbols or words to reach God. He who says 'God is nearer than my allusion' is still within language. The gnostic is beyond it: he is absorbed, extinguished, he has no more allusion. He lives the reality, not the sign.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Ibn 'Ajībah: 'The allusion is a bridge; the gnostic has crossed the bridge, he is in the house. The bridge is useful before the crossing, useless after.'
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Words, symbols, allusions are tools for the seeker. The one who has found needs them no longer. True gnosis is silent, beyond all representations.

records.section_title: Chapter 9: Repentance, hope, contraction/expansion
records.french_translation: Hope is supposed to be accompanied by deeds. Otherwise, it will become mere desire.
records.english_text_draft: Hope is supposed to be accompanied by deeds. Otherwise, it will become mere desire.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Hope (rajā') is not a mere wish. It is dynamic: it drives one to act. Hope without deeds is only a dream, an illusion. Authentic hope is expressed in concrete actions.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: A constant principle in Islam: faith without works is dead. So too is hope. Zarrūq: 'Hope that begets no effort is a lie to oneself.'
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Do not confuse the hope that spurs one to act with the dream that lulls one to sleep. Spiritual hope is dynamic, active, fruitful. Otherwise it is only laziness in disguise.

records.section_title: Chapter 9: Repentance, hope, contraction/expansion
records.french_translation: The things a gnostic seeks from Allah are genuineness in servanthood and (the ability) to carry out privileges of the lordship.
records.english_text_draft: The things a gnostic seeks from Allah are genuineness in servanthood and (the ability) to carry out privileges of the lordship.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: The gnostic asks for nothing for his ego, neither goods, nor paradise, nor reward. He asks for sincerity in servanthood ('ubūdiyya) and the honouring of the rights of Lordship (rubūbiyya). His request is God-centred, not man-centred.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: A lofty degree where one no longer asks for oneself, but to fulfil what God expects. Al-Sharqāwī: 'The prayer of the gnostic is pure praise, a pure request to strengthen his servitude.'
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Disinterested prayer: to ask no longer for oneself, but for God. 'Make me a better servant' rather than 'give me this'. It is a revolution in prayer.

records.section_title: Chapter 9: Repentance, hope, contraction/expansion
records.french_translation: He spread you out so that you will not be remained contracted; and He contracted you so that you will not be left expanded. Then He removed you from both so that you will cease to belong to anything except Him.
records.english_text_draft: He spread you out so that you will not be remained contracted; and He contracted you so that you will not be left expanded. Then He removed you from both so that you will cease to belong to anything except Him.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Two initial states: contraction (al-qabḍ: tightening, sorrow, difficulty) and expansion (al-basṭ: opening, joy, ease). God grants both so that man may settle in neither. Then He raises him beyond the two, to belong to Him alone.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: The classical stations of Sufism. Zarrūq: 'Contraction without expansion brings despair; expansion without contraction intoxicates. Balance lies beyond both.'
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Do not identify with your states (sorrow or joy). Pass through them, do not fix yourself in them. The goal lies beyond moods: a stable presence in God, independent of states.

records.section_title: Chapter 9: Repentance, hope, contraction/expansion
records.french_translation: Gnostics are more afraid of being expanded than of being contracted, because, while expanded, only a few can stay within the limit of propriety.
records.english_text_draft: Gnostics are more afraid of being expanded than of being contracted, because, while expanded, only a few can stay within the limit of propriety.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Expansion (joy, opening, spiritual gifts) is dangerous, for the ego may seize upon the joy, grow proud, fall into illusion. Contraction (sorrow, dryness) is safer, for it humbles and holds the ego in check.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Zarrūq: 'Expansion is a trial, contraction a protection. The gnostics dread the intoxication of expansion more than the sobriety of contraction.'
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Be wary of states of ecstasy and spiritual joy. They are more dangerous than dryness. Joy can feed pride; sorrow keeps one humble.

records.section_title: Chapter 9: Repentance, hope, contraction/expansion
records.french_translation: While expanded, the self will get its share through happiness; but while contracted, there is no share for the self.
records.english_text_draft: While expanded, the self will get its share through happiness; but while contracted, there is no share for the self.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: In expansion the ego takes its share: pleasure, satisfaction, a subtle pride. Contraction starves the ego, diminishes it, gives it no foothold. The ego does not love contraction, yet it is there that it is most tamed.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Contraction is a fast for the ego. Expansion a feast at which the ego may grow fat. The Sufis therefore counsel more contraction than expansion.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: If you wish to weaken your ego, seek out states of contraction (difficulty, austerity). Avoid clinging to spiritual joys that might strengthen it.

records.section_title: Chapter 9: Repentance, hope, contraction/expansion
records.french_translation: Sometimes He gives you (material gains) by depriving you (of closeness to Him), and sometimes He will deprive you (of material gains) by giving you (closeness to Him).
records.english_text_draft: Sometimes He gives you (material gains) by depriving you (of closeness to Him), and sometimes He will deprive you (of material gains) by giving you (closeness to Him).

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: God plays with His gifts: sometimes He grants material goods yet withholds nearness (which is in truth a loss). Sometimes He withholds goods so as to grant nearness (which is an immense gain). One must learn to read His gifts.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: A divine play: what seems a gift may be a veil, what seems a deprivation may be a gift. Do not err in your appraisal.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Do not gauge your spiritual condition by your material goods. True wealth is nearness to God. A 'no' from God may be a 'yes' in disguise.

records.section_title: Chapter 9: Repentance, hope, contraction/expansion
records.french_translation: If He opens for you the door of cognisance while being deprived, the deprivation turns out to be a gift.
records.english_text_draft: If He opens for you the door of cognisance while being deprived, the deprivation turns out to be a gift.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: If God opens to you the door of knowledge (ma'rifa) in the very heart of deprivation (want, poverty), then that deprivation becomes a gift, a grace. Material lack is transformed into spiritual wealth.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: The poor man who knows God is richer than the rich man who is heedless of God. Ibn 'Ajībah: 'Poverty with God is a wealth; wealth without God is a poverty.'
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: The believer's alchemy: to turn adversity into opportunity. The deprivation that opens onto God is not a loss but a gift in disguise. Cultivate this way of seeing.

records.section_title: Chapter 9: Repentance, hope, contraction/expansion
records.french_translation: The whole universe is an illusion in its outward manifestations, while inwardly it is an admonition. The self is fascinated by its illusive exterior, while the heart is drawn by its admonitory interior.
records.english_text_draft: The whole universe is an illusion in its outward manifestations, while inwardly it is an admonition. The self is fascinated by its illusive exterior, while the heart is drawn by its admonitory interior.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: The universe is double-edged: outwardly an illusion (alluring to the ego), inwardly a warning (for the heart). The ego stops at the lure; the heart sees the reminder. The same world may be a trap or a lesson.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: The world (dunyā) is a snare, yet also a book of signs (āyāt) for one who can read. Zarrūq: 'Look at the world with the eye of the ego, you are lost; with the eye of the heart, you are saved.'
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: One's gaze determines the nature of the world. Do not stop at the outer screen. Seek the inner warning beneath the surface illusion.

records.section_title: Chapter 9: Repentance, hope, contraction/expansion
records.french_translation: If you are seeking eternal glory, do not revel in a glory that will not last.
records.english_text_draft: If you are seeking eternal glory, do not revel in a glory that will not last.
