commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Do not rejoice in a fleeting glory (riches, power, renown) if you seek the eternal glory (with God). The world does not deserve to be clung to. Its glory is illusory and temporary.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: The Prophet: 'The glory of the believer lies in his nightly prayer, his honour in his independence from people.' Worldly goods are no marks of divine glory.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Choose between the fleeting approval of men and lasting dignity with God. The two are incompatible. If you seek the eternal, do not stop at the temporal.

records.section_title: Chapter 9: Repentance, hope, contraction/expansion
records.french_translation: The real journey is to cross the precincts of this world in order that the hereafter will become closer to you than yourself.
records.english_text_draft: The real journey is to cross the precincts of this world in order that the hereafter will become closer to you than yourself.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: The true spiritual journey is not geographical. It is the crossing of this world (its allurements, its illusions) so that the hereafter may become more real, nearer than oneself. It is a radical decentring.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: The spiritual path is a decentring, an inner migration. Ibn 'Abbād: 'The traveller towards God is one for whom the hereafter becomes more present than his own shadow.'
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: This world below is not a dwelling but a passage. The goal is that the hereafter become your principal horizon, nearer than your own ego.

records.section_title: Chapter 9: Repentance, hope, contraction/expansion
records.french_translation: A gift from creatures is (really) deprivation, while deprivation from Allah is (really) munificence.
records.english_text_draft: A gift from creatures is (really) deprivation, while deprivation from Allah is (really) munificence.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: A gift from a creature, if it turns you away from God, is in truth a deprivation (even if it is material). A deprivation coming from God, if it turns you towards Him, is true munificence (even if it is painful).
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Zarrūq: 'What comes from the creature is veil, what comes from the Creator is light. Trust not the appearance, but the effect.'
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Radically re-evaluate what a 'gift' is. What draws us from God is a loss, even if it is materially a gain. What draws us near to Him is a gain, even if it is a material loss.

records.section_title: Chapter 10: Reward, privation as a blessing
records.french_translation: Far be it from our Lord to reward the servant with credit while he deals with Him in cash.
records.english_text_draft: Far be it from our Lord to reward the servant with credit while he deals with Him in cash.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: God does not defer the reward (on credit) while the servant renders Him his obedience here below (in cash). He grants a foretaste, a sweetness, from this world already. The very delight of obeying is an immediate reward.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: The Sufis speak of the 'sweetness of worship' (ḥalāwat al-'ibāda) as an anticipated reward. Zarrūq: 'God does not deal on credit with His servants; He gives them the down payment here below.'
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: If you feel no delight in obedience, question yourself. The reward begins here below: the peace of the heart, the sweetness of prayer. These are ready coin in hand.

records.section_title: Chapter 10: Reward, privation as a blessing
records.french_translation: His recognising you as worthy of obedience will suffice as a reward for your obedience.
records.english_text_draft: His recognising you as worthy of obedience will suffice as a reward for your obedience.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: The very fact that God deems you worthy to obey Him is the greatest reward. To be chosen, elected (ijtabā) to worship Him, while others are deprived of it, is a priceless gift. It surpasses every other reward.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Al-Sharqāwī: 'Election (ijtibā') is the highest distinction. The gift of obedience is superior to the reward of obedience.'
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Seek no reward beyond the grace of being in relationship with God. That He has called you, that He has given you faith, is already the essential thing. All the rest is superfluous.

records.section_title: Chapter 10: Reward, privation as a blessing
records.french_translation: It will suffice as a reward for servants (those who do good deeds) to have something that inspires their hearts to obedience and brings to them amusement.
records.english_text_draft: It will suffice as a reward for servants (those who do good deeds) to have something that inspires their hearts to obedience and brings to them amusement.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: The true reward here below is to find pleasure, delight, joy in obedience itself. The worshipper is rewarded by the transformation of worship into spiritual delight, into passion.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: The Prophet said: 'The delight of my eyes is in prayer.' Prayer was not a chore, but a pleasure, a delight in the noble sense.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: When worship becomes a delight, a pleasure, not a heavy routine, it is the sign of a great divine nearness. It is the supreme reward here below.

records.section_title: Chapter 10: Reward, privation as a blessing
records.french_translation: Those who worship Him, aspiring something from Him or to fend off an impending chastisement, do not attach to His attributes their due value.
records.english_text_draft: Those who worship Him, aspiring something from Him or to fend off an impending chastisement, do not attach to His attributes their due value.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: To worship God in order to obtain something (paradise, riches) or to avoid a chastisement (hell) is a degraded form of worship. One does not grant God His true worth. One treats Him as a means, not as the End.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Al-Sharqāwī: 'Mercenary worship is the worship of a merchant. Filial worship is the worship of a lover. God deserves disinterested worship.'
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: God is not a means but the End. To worship Him for anything other than Himself is a form of idolatry in disguise. The purest form is gratuitous worship, expecting no return.

records.section_title: Chapter 10: Reward, privation as a blessing
records.french_translation: When He gives, He makes you witness His benevolence, and when He denies you (something), He makes you witness His supremacy. And in both situations, He is making Himself discernible to you and approaching you with His gentleness.
records.english_text_draft: When He gives, He makes you witness His benevolence, and when He denies you (something), He makes you witness His supremacy. And in both situations, He is making Himself discernible to you and approaching you with His gentleness.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: In giving, God shows you His kindness (luṭf). In withholding, He shows you His supremacy (qahriyya). In both cases He makes Himself known, with gentleness He draws near to you. There is no situation in which God is absent.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Ibn 'Abbād: 'God is present in the yes as in the no. The yes unveils His generosity, the no unveils His wisdom. Both are manifestations of His mercy.'
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Accept that God's 'no' is also a manifestation of Him, at times more precious than His 'yes' (for it teaches us humility). In every situation, God shows Himself.

records.section_title: Chapter 10: Reward, privation as a blessing
records.french_translation: Deprivation hurts you because you fail to understand Allah in it.
records.english_text_draft: Deprivation hurts you because you fail to understand Allah in it.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Deprivation is painful only because one does not see God within it. He who grasps the divine presence at the heart of deprivation does not suffer from it. The pain comes from the absence of vision, not from the deprivation itself.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Zarrūq: 'The pain of deprivation is ignorance of the divine presence at the heart of deprivation. The gnostic does not suffer, for he sees God even in want.'
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Transform your perception: suffering in the face of a lack is not a fatality. It can be transcended by the awareness that God is there too, present within the absence.

records.section_title: Chapter 10: Reward, privation as a blessing
records.french_translation: Sometimes He opens the door of obedience for you but without opening the door of acceptance; or sometimes He will condemn you to sin, which may turn out to be a cause of reaching Him.
records.english_text_draft: Sometimes He opens the door of obedience for you but without opening the door of acceptance; or sometimes He will condemn you to sin, which may turn out to be a cause of reaching Him.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Sometimes God grants obedience (the act) without acceptance (the approval). Sometimes He permits sin, yet that sin, followed by repentance and humility, becomes a cause of drawing near. Do not judge by appearances.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: A humble sin may bring one nearer than a proud act of obedience. Zarrūq: 'How many obediences are veils, and how many sins are keys!'
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Do not rejoice too quickly in your obedience (it may not be accepted), nor despair over a sin (it may be a doorway). God overturns situations.

records.section_title: Chapter 10: Reward, privation as a blessing
records.french_translation: A sin that leads to humiliation and neediness is better than obedience that leads to pride and arrogance.
records.english_text_draft: A sin that leads to humiliation and neediness is better than obedience that leads to pride and arrogance.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: A sin that humbles and impoverishes (spiritually) is better than an obedience that breeds pride. The moral effect of an act outweighs its label. Humility is more precious than formal obedience.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: The Prophet said: 'None shall enter Paradise who has in his heart an atom's weight of pride.' A humble sinner is nearer to God than a proud Pharisee.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: What counts is not the act in itself but its fruit. A 'good' act that breeds pride is toxic; a 'bad' act that humbles may be saving. Do not absolutise the categories.

records.section_title: Chapter 10: Reward, privation as a blessing
records.french_translation: Two divine bounties that no being can be separated from and are unavoidable for every creature are existence and sustenance.
records.english_text_draft: Two divine bounties that no being can be separated from and are unavoidable for every creature are existence and sustenance.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Two universal graces that every living being receives, whatever its merit: existence (being created) and subsistence (sustenance, life). None escapes them. This is the basis of the most minimal gratitude.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: The Quran: 'There is no creature on earth whose sustenance does not rest upon God' (11:6). Even the disbeliever receives these two benefits.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Even in our forgetfulness, God upholds us by these two fundamental graces. They are the universal basis of gratitude, accessible to all, believers or not.

records.section_title: Chapter 10: Reward, privation as a blessing
records.french_translation: He conferred on you, initially, the grace of existence, which was followed by the grace of sustenance.
records.english_text_draft: He conferred on you, initially, the grace of existence, which was followed by the grace of sustenance.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: God created you from nothingness (existence), then He gave you the means to live (subsistence). A twofold dependence, a twofold gift prior to any merit. Nothing you have comes from you.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Man has nothing to lay claim to: neither his existence nor his survival comes from himself. Al-Sharqāwī: 'Before you even existed, you were already indebted.'
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Our whole life is a loan from God. Each breath is a gift, each meal a favour. Gratitude ought to be our permanent state.

records.section_title: Chapter 10: Reward, privation as a blessing
records.french_translation: So your destitution essentially belongs to you. The trials in this world are but reminders of the aspects of your destitution that are hidden from you. No emergency can alter the essential destitution.
records.english_text_draft: So your destitution essentially belongs to you. The trials in this world are but reminders of the aspects of your destitution that are hidden from you. No emergency can alter the essential destitution.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Poverty (need) is essential in man, constitutive of him. Trials only recall this forgotten poverty. Nothing (riches, success) changes this fundamental condition. Man remains poor before God whatever may befall.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Man is 'faqīr' (poor) by essence before God 'al-Ghanī' (the Rich). Zarrūq: 'Poverty is not accidental, it is ontological.'
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Difficulties are awakenings: they remind us that we are not autonomous but fundamentally dependent. Wealth changes nothing of this condition.

records.section_title: Chapter 10: Reward, privation as a blessing
records.french_translation: The best of your moments are those wherein you witness the existence of your destitution and thereby the existence of your submissiveness.
records.english_text_draft: The best of your moments are those wherein you witness the existence of your destitution and thereby the existence of your submissiveness.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: The best moment is the one in which you become aware of your radical need of God (faqr), and thus of your authentic submission ('ubūdiyya). It is not a moment of power but of accepted vulnerability.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Awareness of poverty (faqr) is the gate of divine nearness. The Prophet said: 'Poverty is my pride.' This is not economic poverty, but spiritual.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Celebrate your weakness, for it is the place of encounter with the divine Strength. Self-sufficiency is a veil; awareness of one's poverty is an opening.

records.section_title: Chapter 10: Reward, privation as a blessing
records.french_translation: When He isolates you from His creatures, it means He wants to open for you the door of amusement with Him.
records.english_text_draft: When He isolates you from His creatures, it means He wants to open for you the door of amusement with Him.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Isolation (solitude, withdrawal from people) is not a punishment. It is an opening, an invitation to find one's delight, one's joy, one's companionship in God alone. Solitude is a gift, not a curse.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Solitude (khalwa) is a school of the divine presence. Zarrūq: 'He who does not love solitude will never love the company of God.'
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: See isolation not as a loss but as a unique opportunity. If God draws you away from people, it is to draw you near to Him. Make use of it to cultivate delight in His company.

records.section_title: Chapter 10: Reward, privation as a blessing
records.french_translation: When He makes you utter a request, it means He wants to give you something.
records.english_text_draft: When He makes you utter a request, it means He wants to give you something.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: The very desire to ask, the impulse of prayer, does not come from you. It is God who inspires it. And if He inspires you to ask, it is because He wishes to give. The request is already the beginning of the answer.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Zarrūq: 'The servant's prayer is the Lord's response. The very aspiration to ask is a prior gift.'
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Do not see the request as a one-sided effort. It is the echo of a divine initiative. When you feel the longing to pray, thank God: He is opening to you the door of the gift.

records.section_title: Chapter 10: Reward, privation as a blessing
records.french_translation: Needs will never desert the gnostic. And he will anchor himself only to Allah.
records.english_text_draft: Needs will never desert the gnostic. And he will anchor himself only to Allah.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: The gnostic is always in need of God. He does not seek independence (which is impossible), but rather a joyful dependence. He anchors himself in God alone, for every other anchorage is illusory. His need is permanent, fully owned, and therein lies his strength.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Spiritual poverty is a permanent station among the gnostics. They seek not to 'become rich' before God; they embrace their indigence.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: The need for God is not a lack to be filled, but a state to be cultivated. It is the opposite of self-sufficiency. The gnostic is glad to be in need of God.

records.section_title: Chapter 10: Reward, privation as a blessing
records.french_translation: He illuminated the exteriors with the light of His creatures; and He illuminated the interiors (innermost hearts) with the light of His attributes. Therefore, when the light of the exteriors set, the light of the interiors and innermost hearts will not set. That is why it is said: 'Verily, the sun of the day sets in the night; but the sun of the heart will never set.'
records.english_text_draft: He illuminated the exteriors with the light of His creatures; and He illuminated the interiors (innermost hearts) with the light of His attributes. Therefore, when the light of the exteriors set, the light of the interiors and innermost hearts will not set. That is why it is said: 'Verily, the sun of the day sets in the night; but the sun of the heart will never set.'

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: The outer world (the body, nature) receives a created, fleeting light, that of creatures. The inner heart receives the light of the divine attributes, which is eternal. The light of the world sets, like the sun, but the light of the heart never sets. It is permanent.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: A powerful metaphor. Ibn 'Abbād: 'Seek not the light without, it is fleeting. Seek it within your heart, it does not die.'
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Cultivate the inner light that depends on no outward condition. Neither day nor night, neither joy nor sorrow, neither wealth nor poverty can extinguish it. It is an inexhaustible source.

records.section_title: Chapter 11: All comes from God, passions and duality
records.french_translation: In order to assuage the sufferings of tribulations, He has taught you that it is He who tests you. The One who made you confront His predestination is the same one who has accustomed you to good choices.
records.english_text_draft: In order to assuage the sufferings of tribulations, He has taught you that it is He who tests you. The One who made you confront His predestination is the same one who has accustomed you to good choices.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: The consolation within a trial is to know that it is the very same God who tests you and who has accustomed you to good. This is no hostile fate; it is a coherent pedagogy. The test comes from the One who loves you.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: God is the sole Agent. The trial comes from Him as does the benefit. Zarrūq: 'He who taught you to walk does not let you fall out of malice.'
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: To accept the trial as coming from God is to strip it of its absurd or hostile character. It is a teaching gesture from the very same Hand that has guided you.

records.section_title: Chapter 11: All comes from God, passions and duality
records.french_translation: Those who imagine that His kindness is detached from His predestination does so out of short-sightedness.
records.english_text_draft: Those who imagine that His kindness is detached from His predestination does so out of short-sightedness.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: To distinguish the divine kindness from His decree (fate, at times harsh) is an error of vision. The decree, even what appears evil, is kind at its core. There is not, on one side, the goodness of God, and on the other, His merciless decree.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Ibn 'Ajībah: 'Every divine decree is good, even if it seems evil to our limited perception. Apparent good and evil are enveloped in divine benevolence.'
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Do not separate God's goodness from His action. What befalls you, even when painful, is steeped in His goodness. To affirm this is an act of faith.

records.section_title: Chapter 11: All comes from God, passions and duality
records.french_translation: Do not fear that the way (to Allah) may be puzzling to you; but feel afraid of passion overpowering you.
records.english_text_draft: Do not fear that the way (to Allah) may be puzzling to you; but feel afraid of passion overpowering you.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: The path may be obscure and intricate; that is secondary. The true danger is not intellectual darkness, but the inner passion (hawā) that leads astray and blinds. Cognitive difficulty is less grave than moral corruption.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: The Quran: 'Have you seen him who has taken his own desire as his god?' (45:23). Passion is an inner idol, more dangerous than the outer idol.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Do not worry about failing to understand everything. Worry about your disordered desires. Intelligence matters less than the purity of the heart.

records.section_title: Chapter 11: All comes from God, passions and duality
records.french_translation: Praise be to Him who has covered the exclusive secrets with the exteriors of human nature and who showed the sublimity of lordship through manifesting servanthood (to the servants).
records.english_text_draft: Praise be to Him who has covered the exclusive secrets with the exteriors of human nature and who showed the sublimity of lordship through manifesting servanthood (to the servants).

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Praise be to God, who conceals the secrets of the saints (their nearness) beneath an ordinary human appearance, and who manifests the grandeur of His Lordship through the servanthood of the servants who worship Him. The humility of appearances and the hidden grandeur.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Hadith: 'My saints are beneath My domes, none other knows them.' The saints are 'hidden jewels'. The Prophet himself lived simply.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Do not judge by appearances. Spiritual grandeur often hides beneath ordinary features. Despise no one, for you do not know what God conceals.

records.section_title: Chapter 11: All comes from God, passions and duality
records.french_translation: Do not complain to your Lord about a delayed reply to your request; but you should rather complain about yourself for your delayed (misplaced) manners.
records.english_text_draft: Do not complain to your Lord about a delayed reply to your request; but you should rather complain about yourself for your delayed (misplaced) manners.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Do not complain of God's delay. Complain rather of your own delays and breaches of courtesy (ādāb) towards Him. The fault lies with you (a want of preparation, of sincerity), not with Him. Prayer is no machine for swift replies.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Zarrūq: 'If God delays, it is so that you may hasten to purify yourself. The delay comes from your impurity, not from any want in Him.'
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Change perspective: instead of growing impatient with God, examine your own shortcomings. The unanswered prayer is an invitation to self-examination.

records.section_title: Chapter 11: All comes from God, passions and duality
records.french_translation: Whenever He makes you obedient to His command openly and makes you acknowledge His power in secret, it means He has bestowed on you a great favour.
records.english_text_draft: Whenever He makes you obedient to His command openly and makes you acknowledge His power in secret, it means He has bestowed on you a great favour.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: The twofold grace: to obey outwardly (to perform the acts) AND to acknowledge inwardly the divine power (to claim no merit for oneself). This is the sign of a great divine favour. The balance between the act and humility is perfection.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Perfect balance: outward action without inner pride. Al-Sharqāwī: 'Many obey, few are humble in obedience. The twofold grace is rare.'
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Do not be content merely to obey. Inspect the state of the heart: is there pride there, or gratitude? The true favour is to obey without growing proud.

records.section_title: Chapter 11: All comes from God, passions and duality
records.french_translation: Not all those whose exclusiveness was confirmed could perfect their liberation.
records.english_text_draft: Not all those whose exclusiveness was confirmed could perfect their liberation.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: To possess exceptional gifts (miracles, a sense of being 'set apart') does not guarantee perfection. True perfection is constancy (istiqāma), uprightness, fidelity. Without it, even miracles fall short. One may be 'distinguished' without being 'freed'.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Al-Sharqāwī: 'Miracles (karāmāt) are not the end; uprightness (istiqāma) is the end. How many miracle-workers have fallen!'
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Do not let yourself be impressed by the extraordinary. True grandeur is discreet: regularity, fidelity, daily uprightness. A humble and steady man is greater than an unstable worker of miracles.

records.section_title: Chapter 12: The litanies and the obligatory prayers
records.french_translation: Only the ignorant ones could underestimate the litany (wird). While the litany will disappear along with this world, the inspiration drawn from it (wārid) will be intact till the hereafter. It is advisable to engage in something for which there is no alternative. The litany is what He seeks from you, while the inspiration is what you seek from Him. But where is what He asks from you compared to what you seek from Him?
records.english_text_draft: Only the ignorant ones could underestimate the litany (wird). While the litany will disappear along with this world, the inspiration drawn from it (wārid) will be intact till the hereafter. It is advisable to engage in something for which there is no alternative. The litany is what He seeks from you, while the inspiration is what you seek from Him. But where is what He asks from you compared to what you seek from Him?

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Do not underrate the litany (wird, the regular practice). It will pass away with this world, but the inspiration (wārid) it produces will remain in the hereafter. The litany is what God asks of you; the inspiration, what you await from Him. The first is your duty, the second His gift. Do not compare your duty (modest) with His gift (immense).
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Zarrūq: 'The litany is the means, inspiration is the goal. But without means, no goal. The ignorant despises the means, the wise man honours it.'
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Do not neglect the regular practice on the pretext of aiming at illumination. The means is humble, but without it there is no result. Honour your litany.

records.section_title: Chapter 12: The litanies and the obligatory prayers
records.french_translation: The arrival of sustenance (from Allah) depends on the preparations (of the servant), while the dawning of light is in accordance with innermost purity.
records.english_text_draft: The arrival of sustenance (from Allah) depends on the preparations (of the servant), while the dawning of light is in accordance with innermost purity.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Two conditions: spiritual sustenance (attraction, openings) arrives in proportion to the preparations (actions, obedience). The divine light arrives in proportion to inner purity (a purified heart). Outward effort (preparation) and inner purity are both necessary.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Effort (juhd) and purification (taṭhīr) are two distinct conditions. Zarrūq: 'Prepare the ground and purify the vessel. The divine rain will come.'
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Neglect neither the action (preparation) nor the purification of the heart (sincerity, detachment). Both are necessary in order to receive.

records.section_title: Chapter 12: The litanies and the obligatory prayers
records.french_translation: An ignorant man gets up thinking about what he is going to do, while an intelligent man reflects on what Allah is doing with him.
records.english_text_draft: An ignorant man gets up thinking about what he is going to do, while an intelligent man reflects on what Allah is doing with him.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: The ignorant man is preoccupied with his own actions (his projects). The intelligent man is preoccupied with what God does with him (the decree, fate, the teaching). The first is active, the second receptive. Spiritual intelligence consists in taking more interest in God's action than in one's own.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Zarrūq: 'Attention to the divine decree (taqdīr) is higher than attention to one's own acting. The one is self-centred, the other God-centred.'
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Shift the centre of your attention: ask not 'what shall I do?' but 'what is God doing with me, with my life?'. The answer matters more.

records.section_title: Chapter 12: The litanies and the obligatory prayers
records.french_translation: Both devotees and ascetics seek to distance themselves from everything because for them Allah is absent from everything. If they witnessed Allah in everything, they would not have sought detachment from anything.
records.english_text_draft: Both devotees and ascetics seek to distance themselves from everything because for them Allah is absent from everything. If they witnessed Allah in everything, they would not have sought detachment from anything.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: The devout and the ascetics flee the world because they do not see God within it. Were they to see God in all things, they would have no need to flee. Detachment is a makeshift for those who do not see God in things.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Ibn 'Abbād: 'Flight is for those who do not see. Presence is for those who see. Detachment is a remedy, contemplation is the cure.'
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: If you flee the world, it is because you do not see God within it. The highest degree is not flight, but contemplation within presence. Remain in the world, yet see God in all things.

records.section_title: Chapter 12: The litanies and the obligatory prayers
records.french_translation: He asked you in this world to reflect on His creatures; but in the hereafter, He will reveal to you His perfect entity.
records.english_text_draft: He asked you in this world to reflect on His creatures; but in the hereafter, He will reveal to you His perfect entity.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: In this world, God asks us to reflect upon His creatures (signs, traces). In the hereafter, He will reveal Himself directly, without intermediary. The world is a preparatory school; the hereafter is direct vision.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: The Quran: 'We shall show them Our signs in the horizons and within themselves' (41:53). Reflection upon creatures leads to God. The hereafter surpasses reflection.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: The world is not an end. It is a book of signs. The contemplation of creatures is the preparatory school. Do not halt at the signs, but aim at the Signified.

records.section_title: Chapter 12: The litanies and the obligatory prayers
records.french_translation: Since He knows that you could not endure without (seeing) Him, He made you see what is perceptible from Him (His creatures).
records.english_text_draft: Since He knows that you could not endure without (seeing) Him, He made you see what is perceptible from Him (His creatures).

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: God renders Himself imperceptible (we cannot see Him directly), yet renders His creatures perceptible. It is an accommodation to our weakness. He gives us to behold what comes from Him, since we cannot behold Him as He is.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: A gesture of mercy. Al-Sharqāwī: 'God does not leave us without vision. He grants us the vision of His works while we await the vision of His essence.'
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Nature is an accessible theophany. It is not God, but it leads to Him. It is a gift made for our weakness.

records.section_title: Chapter 12: The litanies and the obligatory prayers
records.french_translation: Since Allah knows that you would be exhausted, He conferred on you different means of obedience. And since Allah knew your impulsiveness, He restricted it to certain times so that you will be concerned more with performing devotional prayers than with the existence of the prayer itself. For not all those who pray perform it (well).
records.english_text_draft: Since Allah knows that you would be exhausted, He conferred on you different means of obedience. And since Allah knew your impulsiveness, He restricted it to certain times so that you will be concerned more with performing devotional prayers than with the existence of the prayer itself. For not all those who pray perform it (well).

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: God has diversified the acts of obedience (prayer, fasting, almsgiving, pilgrimage, and the rest) so as to avoid weariness. He has set fixed times for the prayers so as to avoid procrastination (one cannot defer them indefinitely) and so that one may concentrate on performing the prayer rather than on its mere existence.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: The Law (sharī'a) is a divine accommodation to human capacities. Zarrūq: 'God knows your sloth, which is why He set you fixed hours.'
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: The diversity of practices and their fixed times are not arbitrary constraints, but aids to constancy. Welcome them as acts of mercy.

records.section_title: Chapter 12: The litanies and the obligatory prayers
records.french_translation: Devotional prayers purify hearts from dirt and open up the door to the domain of secrets.
records.english_text_draft: Devotional prayers purify hearts from dirt and open up the door to the domain of secrets.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: The canonical prayers (ṣalāt) have two functions: to purify the heart (of sins, of stains) and to open access to the spiritual secrets (inspirations, lights). Prayer is not merely a rite; it is an instrument of transformation.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Prayer (ṣalāt) is the 'key to success' (miftāḥ al-falāḥ), after the title of a book by Ibn 'Aṭā'illāh himself. It purifies and opens.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Do not see prayer as a heavy obligation, but as a means of purification and opening. It cleanses the heart and unlocks the inner doors.

records.section_title: Chapter 12: The litanies and the obligatory prayers
records.french_translation: Devotional prayers are the place of conversation (between the servant and his Lord) and reciprocal gestures wherein the domains of the secrets will be widened and the gleams of light will crack open. He knows your weakness, so that He reduced the number of devotional prayers. And He knows that you want His grace, so that He multiplied their productivity.
records.english_text_draft: Devotional prayers are the place of conversation (between the servant and his Lord) and reciprocal gestures wherein the domains of the secrets will be widened and the gleams of light will crack open. He knows your weakness, so that He reduced the number of devotional prayers. And He knows that you want His grace, so that He multiplied their productivity.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Prayer is a dialogue, an exchange, a place where secrets expand and lights come into bloom. God has reduced their number (five prayers a day) in mercy to human weakness, yet multiplied their worth (reward, spiritual fruitfulness). Little effort, immense result.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Prayer as the 'ascension of the believer' (mi'rāj al-mu'min). Brief in time, yet an immense journey. Zarrūq: 'Five minutes, but the infinite.'
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Do not complain of the number. Five prayers is little. But their fruitfulness is immense. This is the grace of the canonical prayers: little effort, much light.

records.section_title: Chapter 12: The litanies and the obligatory prayers
records.french_translation: When you seek reward in exchange for a deed, you will be questioned about the sincerity in it. For the insincere, safety from chastisement will be enough (as a reward).
records.english_text_draft: When you seek reward in exchange for a deed, you will be questioned about the sincerity in it. For the insincere, safety from chastisement will be enough (as a reward).

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: To seek a reward for one's act exposes one to being questioned about sincerity (did you truly intend to please God, or were you seeking something else?). For the insincere, the mere fact of being saved from chastisement is already a favour, more than he deserves.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Zarrūq: 'The reward is for the sincere; for the insincere, the absence of punishment is already an unfathomable divine favour.'
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Do not rely on your acts to obtain anything whatever. Sincerity is the only valid currency. Without it, even paradise would be too much to ask.

records.section_title: Chapter 12: The litanies and the obligatory prayers
records.french_translation: Do not seek reward for a deed which you did not actually do. That He accepts it will be enough as reward for you.
records.english_text_draft: Do not seek reward for a deed which you did not actually do. That He accepts it will be enough as reward for you.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: You are not the true author of your good deeds. God is their source (He created in you the will, the capacity, the occasion). So do not seek a reward for what you did not truly do on your own. That He accepts your act (despite its imperfection) is reward enough.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: The doctrine of the creation of acts (khalq al-af'āl). Man acquires the act, but God creates it. Zarrūq: 'Your act is a gift; how can you ask a wage for a gift?'
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: The ultimate humility: to acknowledge that God is the Author. Do not claim wages for His work. His acceptance is already more than you deserve.

records.section_title: Chapter 12: The litanies and the obligatory prayers
records.french_translation: When He wants to reveal His grace to you, He creates (good) deeds (in you) and attributes them to you.
records.english_text_draft: When He wants to reveal His grace to you, He creates (good) deeds (in you) and attributes them to you.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: When God wishes to show you His grace, He creates good deeds within you and attributes them to you. A twofold gift: the deed (grace) and the credit for the deed (generosity). He grants you the obedience and, beyond that, ascribes it to you as though it were your own.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: God attributes to man what He creates in him, out of generosity, not necessity. Al-Sharqāwī: 'It is as if you lent your pen and were praised for the fine handwriting.'
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: God makes us the gift of obedience and grants us the credit for it. It is an overflowing generosity. Never claim the merit for yourself.

records.section_title: Chapter 12: The litanies and the obligatory prayers
records.french_translation: If He made you resort to yourself, there will be no end to your desolation; and if He confers His munificence on you, there will be no end to your fortunes.
records.english_text_draft: If He made you resort to yourself, there will be no end to your desolation; and if He confers His munificence on you, there will be no end to your fortunes.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: If God entrusts you to yourself (to your own will, your own wisdom), you are lost beyond recourse. If, on the contrary, He overwhelms you with His munificence (His help, His guidance), you are saved and fulfilled without end. Everything depends on Him, not on you.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Radical dependence: there is no refuge save in God. Zarrūq: 'God never entrusts His servant to himself; it is man who deludes himself with such entrusting.'
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Our salvation or our ruin hangs solely on the orientation God gives us. Humility consists in knowing that we can do nothing of ourselves, and in asking Him not to abandon us to ourselves.

records.section_title: Chapter 13: Human and divine attributes
records.french_translation: Associate yourself with the attributes of His Lordship to make you aware about attributes of your servanthood.
records.english_text_draft: Associate yourself with the attributes of His Lordship to make you aware about attributes of your servanthood.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Contemplate the divine attributes (power, eternity, riches) the better to measure your own human attributes (weakness, poverty, dependence). The comparison breeds humility. To know God is to know oneself as a servant.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: A fundamental principle: 'He who knows himself knows his Lord.' The knowledge of God is the mirror of self-knowledge.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Do not compare yourself to other human beings. Compare yourself to God. There you will see your true smallness and your true greatness, which is to be a servant.

records.section_title: Chapter 13: Human and divine attributes
records.french_translation: He has forbidden you from laying claim to what does not belong to you from the attributes of His creatures, let alone laying claim to attributes of Him who is the Lord of the universe.
records.english_text_draft: He has forbidden you from laying claim to what does not belong to you from the attributes of His creatures, let alone laying claim to attributes of Him who is the Lord of the universe.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: It is already forbidden to you to claim the attributes of others (theft, usurpation). How much more, then, is it forbidden to claim the divine attributes (power, absolute knowledge, infinite mercy). It is a grave violation of the bounds of servanthood. It is shirk (associationism).
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: The gravest sin: shirk, especially when hidden, consisting in ascribing to oneself the qualities of God or ascribing human qualities to Him.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Spiritual pride, which consists in ascribing power or holiness to oneself, is a form of idolatry. Remain humble within the bounds of your condition as a servant.

records.section_title: Chapter 13: Human and divine attributes
records.french_translation: How can extraordinary things happen to you when you have yet to rip apart the ordinariness of your life?
records.english_text_draft: How can extraordinary things happen to you when you have yet to rip apart the ordinariness of your life?

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: A rhetorical question: how can you hope for extraordinary spiritual openings (miracles, gifts) if you still live an ordinary life, without rupture, without renunciation, without asceticism? The ordinary (attachment, routine, passions) does not lead to the extraordinary.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Al-Sharqāwī: 'One does not attain the extraordinary by ordinary means. The veil of the ordinary must be torn through renunciation and vigilance.'
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Do not be astonished at having no intense spiritual experiences if your life is humdrum and without deep commitment. The gift demands a rupture, a sacrifice.

records.section_title: Chapter 13: Human and divine attributes
records.french_translation: It is not important that you beg (Allah), but the important thing is whether you conform to good conduct or not.
records.english_text_draft: It is not important that you beg (Allah), but the important thing is whether you conform to good conduct or not.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: What matters is not to supplicate (quantity, insistence), but to observe the good manners (ādāb) of servanthood. Courtesy towards God (presence, respect, humility) takes precedence over insistence. A courteous prayer is more precious than an insistent but ill-mannered one.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: The ādāb (good manners) are the condition of the acceptance of prayer. Zarrūq: 'God has no need of your insistence, He has need of your adab.'
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Far more than the quantity of prayers or the force of supplication, it is the quality of presence and respect that counts. Be courteous with God.

records.section_title: Chapter 13: Human and divine attributes
records.french_translation: Nothing is so demanded of you as extreme need; and nothing so expedites gifts as lowliness and need.
records.english_text_draft: Nothing is so demanded of you as extreme need; and nothing so expedites gifts as lowliness and need.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: What is awaited of you above all is the keen feeling of your need for God. And nothing hastens the divine gifts like lowliness and the awareness of that need. Spiritual poverty is the key to divine riches.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Poverty (faqr) is the key to divine wealth. The Prophet said: 'Poverty is my pride.' Humility draws gifts.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: God is not impressed by our strength, but moved by our humble weakness and our acknowledged dependence. To show one's need is not to belittle oneself; it is to open the door of the gift.

records.section_title: Chapter 13: Human and divine attributes
records.french_translation: If you do not hope to reach Him without the extinction of your defects and the effacement of your self-importance, you will never reach Him. When He wants you to reach Him, He will hide your attributes with His attributes and your qualities with His qualities. Thus, He makes you reach Him by virtue of what comes from Him and not by means of what comes from you to Him.
records.english_text_draft: If you do not hope to reach Him without the extinction of your defects and the effacement of your self-importance, you will never reach Him. When He wants you to reach Him, He will hide your attributes with His attributes and your qualities with His qualities. Thus, He makes you reach Him by virtue of what comes from Him and not by means of what comes from you to Him.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Do not imagine that you can reach God by eliminating your faults through your own efforts (you will never manage it). It is God who veils your attributes (your faults) with His own (perfection) when He wishes to raise you. The arrival is a gift, not a merit. God makes you arrive through what comes from Him (His grace), not through what comes from you (your imperfect efforts).
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: The doctrine of the 'substitution of attributes': God replaces yours with His own through pure grace. Zarrūq: 'It is not you who become perfect, it is He who covers your imperfection.'
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Do not believe that one 'deserves' to reach God. It is He who comes to us and clothes us in His attributes to make us capable. Effort is necessary, but the arrival is a pure gift.

records.section_title: Chapter 14: The veil of divine protection
records.french_translation: Had it not been for His beautiful veneer (of protection), no deed would be worthy of acceptance.
records.english_text_draft: Had it not been for His beautiful veneer (of protection), no deed would be worthy of acceptance.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Were it not for God's protecting veil (which covers our faults, our imperfections), no act would be acceptable. God renders our actions meritorious by covering their imperfections. It is He who approves them, not their intrinsic perfection.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Al-Sharqāwī: 'God does not accept our acts for their perfection, but accepts them through His generosity, covering their flaws. Without His veil, all would be rejected.'
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Never be proud of your acts. Without the mercy of God that covers our imperfections, they would all be rejected. Gratitude is therefore fitting, not pride.

records.section_title: Chapter 14: The veil of divine protection
records.french_translation: You need His forbearance more when you obey Him than when you disobey Him.
records.english_text_draft: You need His forbearance more when you obey Him than when you disobey Him.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: In obedience, you need the divine forbearance (ḥilm) for Him to accept your imperfect act, tainted with pride or distraction. In disobedience, you need forgiveness. But the need is greater in obedience, for it is there that pride may subtly take root. The disobedient man knows he needs forgiveness; the obedient man may delude himself.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Zarrūq: 'Pride born of obedience is more dangerous than sin, for it is invisible. The sinner knows he is a sinner; the obedient one may be heedless of his pride.'
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Beware: it is when we obey that we are most exposed to pride, and so most in need of God's forbearance. Never feel secure within obedience.

records.section_title: Chapter 14: The veil of divine protection
records.french_translation: There are two types of veneer (protection): one that protects you from disobedience and one that protects you in disobedience. The layman who is afraid of losing his status among people seeks Allah's covering in disobedience, while the privileged one who is afraid of losing his position with the Real King seeks Allah's protection from disobedience.
records.english_text_draft: There are two types of veneer (protection): one that protects you from disobedience and one that protects you in disobedience. The layman who is afraid of losing his status among people seeks Allah's covering in disobedience, while the privileged one who is afraid of losing his position with the Real King seeks Allah's protection from disobedience.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Two kinds of veil: 1) the veil that protects from sin (through the fear of God). 2) The veil that protects within sin (concealing the sin from people). The common man seeks the second (fear of people). The privileged man (the gnostic) seeks the first (fear of God). The first seeks not to sin; the second seeks to hide his sin.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: The fear of the gnostic is higher: he fears God, not men. Zarrūq: 'He who fears men more than God is a hypocrite; he who fears God more than men is a believer.'
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Which veil do you seek? The one that hides your sin from people, or the one that preserves you from sin? The answer reveals your spiritual degree.

records.section_title: Chapter 14: The veil of divine protection
records.french_translation: Those who hold you in high esteem honour the beauty of your veneer. Therefore, praise the One who veneered you and not the one who honoured and thanked you.
records.english_text_draft: Those who hold you in high esteem honour the beauty of your veneer. Therefore, praise the One who veneered you and not the one who honoured and thanked you.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: People honour you on account of the veil (the appearance) that God has placed upon you. They do not see your faults. So do not thank people for their esteem, but thank God who has veiled you. It is He who deserves the gratitude, not those deceived by your appearance.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: The Prophet: 'Do not thank the creature for what God has given you.' Zarrūq: 'The praise of people is a gift from God, not a due owed to your virtue.'
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: When you are praised, do not claim the merit. God has simply hidden from others the faults they would have seen. The gratitude goes to Him, not to you, nor even to the people.

records.section_title: Chapter 14: The veil of divine protection
records.french_translation: The real companion is the one who accompanies you despite his knowing your defects. This (companion) is none other than your Lord. The best companion is the one who chooses you without expecting any benefit from you in return.
records.english_text_draft: The real companion is the one who accompanies you despite his knowing your defects. This (companion) is none other than your Lord. The best companion is the one who chooses you without expecting any benefit from you in return.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: The true companion is God. He knows you better than anyone, sees all your faults (nothing is hidden from Him), and remains with you. Moreover, He draws no benefit from you. It is the perfect friendship, selfless and clear-sighted. Human beings, by contrast, often flee when they come to know our faults, or expect something in return.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: God is al-Walī (the Friend, the Protector). The Prophet said: 'God says: I am with My servant so long as he remembers Me.'
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Seek that companionship. Human friendships are beautiful but fragile. God is the Friend who knows the worst of you and stays, and who gives expecting nothing. He is the surest company of all.

records.section_title: Chapter 14: The veil of divine protection
records.french_translation: When the light of certitude dawns on you, you will see the hereafter so close that you need not travel towards it, and you will see the splendours of this world exposed to the eclipse of extinction.
records.english_text_draft: When the light of certitude dawns on you, you will see the hereafter so close that you need not travel towards it, and you will see the splendours of this world exposed to the eclipse of extinction.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Certainty (yaqīn) transforms perception: the hereafter is no longer distant; it becomes present, immediate ('you have no need to travel'). The world, by contrast, loses its lustre; its splendours appear as what they are: destined to extinction.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Yaqīn is a light that changes the perception of the real. Al-Sharqāwī: 'The believer sees the hereafter as though he touched it, and the world as though it were crumbling.'
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Deep faith is not a belief in distant things, but a direct vision. The hereafter becomes the here-and-now, the world becomes transparent.

records.section_title: Chapter 14: The veil of divine protection
records.french_translation: It is not the existence of something else alongside Him that veils you from Him. But what veils you from Him is your illusion that something is there alongside Him.
records.english_text_draft: It is not the existence of something else alongside Him that veils you from Him. But what veils you from Him is your illusion that something is there alongside Him.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: It is not the real existence of anything other than God that veils you (for nothing truly exists alongside Him). It is your illusion (wahm) that there is something other than Him. The veil is mental, not ontological. It is your belief in a separate reality that hides God from you.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Pure tawhīd: there is no reality save God. Zarrūq: 'The world is an illusion of separation. Dispel the illusion, and God appears.'
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: The root of every veil is mental: it is the idea that 'God and the world' are two separate realities. When that illusion falls, the veil falls. The world becomes transparent to God.

records.section_title: Chapter 14: The veil of divine protection
records.french_translation: Had it not been for His manifestation in creatures, (human) eyes would not have perceived them. If His attributes were manifested, creatures would have faded away.
records.english_text_draft: Had it not been for His manifestation in creatures, (human) eyes would not have perceived them. If His attributes were manifested, creatures would have faded away.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: It is the divine manifestation (tajallī) within creatures that renders them perceptible. Without that touch of divine light, they would be invisible (or without interest). But were the divine attributes themselves to manifest directly (unveiled), creatures could not bear it; they would vanish (annihilation).
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Modulated theophany: the world is an attenuated manifestation of God, bearable for creatures. Al-Sharqāwī: 'God shows Himself just enough for us to see Him without being annihilated.'
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: The world is a screen that shows God without exhausting Him. Were God to show Himself directly, we would be annihilated by His glory. His mercy veils His full manifestation.

records.section_title: Chapter 14: The veil of divine protection
records.french_translation: He made everything manifest because He is the Interior, and He obscured the existence of everything because He is the Exterior.
records.english_text_draft: He made everything manifest because He is the Interior, and He obscured the existence of everything because He is the Exterior.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: God is the Inward (al-Bāṭin): He is more intimate to each thing than it is to itself. He is the Outward (al-Ẓāhir): He surpasses every thing, He is beyond. Through His quality of the Outward, He manifests all. Through His quality of the Inward, He obscures the pretension of things to exist of themselves.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Verse 57:3: 'He is the First and the Last, the Outward and the Inward.' God is the horizon and the kernel.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: God is more inward than our inwardness (He knows our secrets), and more outward than every thing (nothing encloses Him). He is both at once. Contemplate this duality.

records.section_title: Chapter 14: The veil of divine protection
records.french_translation: He allows you to reflect on creatures. But He does not allow you to stay there with those creatures. With His words 'Say, Behold all that is in the heavens and on earth!', He opened up the door of understanding for you. But He does not say, 'Behold the heavens,' so as not to stop you at their corporal existence.
records.english_text_draft: He allows you to reflect on creatures. But He does not allow you to stay there with those creatures. With His words 'Say, Behold all that is in the heavens and on earth!', He opened up the door of understanding for you. But He does not say, 'Behold the heavens,' so as not to stop you at their corporal existence.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: It is permitted to reflect upon creatures, but not to halt at them (fetishism). The Quran says 'look at what is there' (the content, the signs), not 'look at the sky' (the material object), so as not to fix the gaze upon the form. One must pass through creatures towards the Creator.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: The world is a bridge, not a dwelling. It must be crossed, not settled upon. Zarrūq: 'Look through things, not at things themselves.'
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Never fetishise the creation. It is a sign, not the Signified. To contemplate it is to pass through it towards God. Do not halt at the beauty of the world without seeing the One who made it.

records.section_title: Chapter 14: The veil of divine protection
records.french_translation: The universe became stable when He made it stable, but it is effaced viewed through the unity of His being.
records.english_text_draft: The universe became stable when He made it stable, but it is effaced viewed through the unity of His being.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: The universe has a relative stability, granted by God (the laws of nature). But from the standpoint of divine unity (tawhīd), that stability is effaced. From God's standpoint, the world is like a dream that vanishes. Its stability is illusory; its reality is God.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: The vision of unity (wahda) makes multiplicity vanish. Ibn 'Arabī: 'The world is a shadow of God.'
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Physics sees a stable universe; the Sufi sees a universe that vanishes at every instant into God. Both are true at their own level. Do not absolutise the stability of the world.

records.section_title: Chapter 15: Beware the praise of people
records.french_translation: People praise you for what, according to their supposition, is yours; so you must blame yourself for what you know of those praises.
records.english_text_draft: People praise you for what, according to their supposition, is yours; so you must blame yourself for what you know of those praises.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: People praise you for what they suppose to be your merit. You yourself know that it comes from God. You must therefore blame yourself for accepting that praise (for you do not deserve it). Do not rejoice in compliments, for they bear upon gifts received, not merits acquired.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Humility before praise: not to accept it, but to refer it to God. The Prophet said: 'Do not praise me excessively as the Christians praised Jesus.'
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: When you are complimented, do not swell with pride. Remember that you are but the vessel, not the source. Blame yourself for accepting what is not your due.

records.section_title: Chapter 15: Beware the praise of people
records.french_translation: When a believer is lauded, he feels ashamed in front of Allah of being praised for an attribute that he cannot perceive in himself.
records.english_text_draft: When a believer is lauded, he feels ashamed in front of Allah of being praised for an attribute that he cannot perceive in himself.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: The believer, when praised for some quality (generosity, piety), does not recognise himself in that quality (for he sees his own faults). He therefore feels ashamed before God to accept a praise he deems undeserved. Shame (ḥayā') is a virtue.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Modesty (ḥayā') is a branch of faith. The Prophet said: 'Modesty brings only good.' The believer is ashamed to be praised for what he is not.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: The shame of receiving undeserved credit is a mark of sincerity. He who accepts without shame the praise for divine gifts is proud. Cultivate this salutary shame.

records.section_title: Chapter 15: Beware the praise of people
records.french_translation: The most ignorant one is he who forgoes his certitude for people's suppositions.
records.english_text_draft: The most ignorant one is he who forgoes his certitude for people's suppositions.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: The most ignorant man is not he who does not know, but he who forsakes his inner certainty (what he knows of himself, of God) to follow people's suppositions about him. He prefers public opinion to intimate truth. It is the height of ignorance.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Zarrūq: 'Better to follow the certainty of one's heart than the suppositions of the crowds. The crowd often errs, the sincere heart rarely.'
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Do not let public opinion dictate what you are. Your inner certainty is worth more than their outward suppositions. Be faithful to what you know of yourself and of God.

records.section_title: Chapter 15: Beware the praise of people
records.french_translation: When He makes people praise you for what you do not deserve, praise Him for what He deserves.
records.english_text_draft: When He makes people praise you for what you do not deserve, praise Him for what He deserves.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: When people praise you undeservedly, do not accept the praise for yourself. Seize that occasion to praise God, who deserves all praise. Transform the praise received into praise rendered to God. It is a spiritual technique.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Zarrūq: 'The believer receives praise as a deposit and returns it at once to God. He keeps nothing of it for himself.'
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: A practical technique: when you are complimented, answer mentally or aloud, 'Praise be to God, from Him comes all good.' In this way you neutralise pride.

records.section_title: Chapter 15: Beware the praise of people
records.french_translation: When ascetics are lauded, they are contracted because they perceive the praises hailing from creatures; but when gnostics are praised, they are expanded because they perceive it coming from the real King.
records.english_text_draft: When ascetics are lauded, they are contracted because they perceive the praises hailing from creatures; but when gnostics are praised, they are expanded because they perceive it coming from the real King.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: The ascetic (zāhid) flees creatures. When praised, he contracts (uneasy, sorrowful), for he sees the praise as coming from creatures, of whom he is wary. The gnostic ('ārif) sees God in all things. When praised, he expands (rejoices), for he sees the praise as coming from the real King through creatures.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: A difference of perception: the ascetic flees the world, the gnostic sees God through the world. Al-Sharqāwī: 'The gnostic is freed from the fear of creatures, so he can receive without dread.'
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Praise is not bad in itself. Everything depends on its perceived source: if it comes from men, it weighs heavy; if it comes from God through men, it is light. Raise your gaze.

records.section_title: Chapter 15: Beware the praise of people
records.french_translation: If something given to you expands you, and if something denied to you contracts you, it betokens your immaturity (or triviality because you are an uninvited guest among gnostics) and the insincerity of your servanthood.
records.english_text_draft: If something given to you expands you, and if something denied to you contracts you, it betokens your immaturity (or triviality because you are an uninvited guest among gnostics) and the insincerity of your servanthood.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: If a gift dilates you (excessive joy) and a refusal contracts you (excessive sorrow), it is a sign of spiritual immaturity and insincerity. The true servant does not depend on gifts for his joy. His joy is in God, constant, beyond the divine yes and no.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Zarrūq: 'The mature servant is stable in all states. His joy does not rise with gifts, his sorrow does not fall with refusals. He is anchored in God.'
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Our inner state ought not to fluctuate with outward circumstances. If you are too happy when you receive and too sorrowful when you lose, it is because your happiness depends on things, not on God.

records.section_title: Chapter 16: Be hopeful toward God
records.french_translation: When you commit a sin, let it not make you despair of being righteous with your Lord, because it might be the last sin predestined for you.
records.english_text_draft: When you commit a sin, let it not make you despair of being righteous with your Lord, because it might be the last sin predestined for you.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Do not despair after a sin. This sin you commit today might be the last God had decreed for you. After it, you will repent and will do it no more. Despair is a sin greater than the sin itself.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Zarrūq: 'Despair is the gravest of faults, for it cuts off hope in mercy. Sin is an error; despair is a revolt.'
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Never let the shame of sin shut against you the door of repentance. Hope in God's mercy is always permitted, even after the worst of faults. Despair, by contrast, is fatal.

records.section_title: Chapter 16: Be hopeful toward God
records.french_translation: If you want the door of hope opened for you, bring to mind whatever (bounties) He conferred on you. And if you want the door of fear opened for you, bring to mind whatever you did to Him.
records.english_text_draft: If you want the door of hope opened for you, bring to mind whatever (bounties) He conferred on you. And if you want the door of fear opened for you, bring to mind whatever you did to Him.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: For hope (rajā'), recall the favours of God upon you (His generosity). For fear (khawf), recall what you have done towards God (your shortcomings, your sins). Alternate between the two so as to remain in balance.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: The alternation between hope and fear is essential in Islamic spirituality. The Quran praises those who 'call upon their Lord in fear and hope'.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: To govern one's spiritual emotions: hope comes from gratitude (think of the favours), fear comes from awareness of one's faults. Both are necessary so as not to fall into illusion (too much hope) or despair (too much fear).

records.section_title: Chapter 16: Be hopeful toward God
records.french_translation: Sometimes He will teach you in the night of contraction what you have not learned in the bright day of expansion. 'You know not which of them is nearest to you in benefit'.
records.english_text_draft: Sometimes He will teach you in the night of contraction what you have not learned in the bright day of expansion. 'You know not which of them is nearest to you in benefit'.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: In the night of contraction (sorrow, difficulty), God teaches lessons that were not learnt in the luminous day of expansion (joy, ease). Adversity is at times more beneficial than prosperity. The verse (4:11) stresses that we do not know what is best for us.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Al-Sharqāwī: 'God gives contraction to teach humility, and expansion to teach gratitude. But the lessons of contraction are often the deeper.'
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Do not systematically prefer luminous expansion to dark contraction. Contraction can bring lessons that expansion does not give. Trust in the divine wisdom, even in difficult moments.

records.section_title: Chapter 16: Be hopeful toward God
records.french_translation: Hearts and innermost secrets are the places where lights dawn.
records.english_text_draft: Hearts and innermost secrets are the places where lights dawn.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: The heart and the inner secrets (the depths of the soul) are the receptacles of the divine lights. The heart of the believer is like a sky where the stars of knowledge, of wisdom, and of gnosis rise.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: A cosmic metaphor: the heart is a sky, the light is the sun. Zarrūq: 'Seek not the light without, but within your heart. It is there that it rises.'
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: The place of illumination is not outward (books, masters, holy places), but inward. It is the purified heart that becomes the receptacle of the divine light. Purify your heart that it may be a luminous sky.

records.section_title: Chapter 16: Be hopeful toward God
records.french_translation: The light deposited in hearts is nourished by the light issuing from invisible treasures—the light which unveils to you the states of His creatures and the light which unveils to you His attributes.
records.english_text_draft: The light deposited in hearts is nourished by the light issuing from invisible treasures—the light which unveils to you the states of His creatures and the light which unveils to you His attributes.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: The light laid within the heart is nourished by a light coming from the unseen treasuries (and so directly from God). This light has two functions: 1) to unveil the states of creatures (the realities of the world), 2) to unveil the divine attributes. A single light, two unveilings (kashf).
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Two kinds of unveiling (kashf): upon the world ('ālam) and upon God (Ḥaqq). Al-Sharqāwī: 'The same key opens two doors.'
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Spiritual light has two functions: it illumines the world (lets it be seen as it is) and it illumines God (lets Him be known). Cultivate this light; it will teach you everything.

records.section_title: Chapter 16: Be hopeful toward God
records.french_translation: Sometimes hearts will be stopped along with the lights just as souls will be veiled by the opacity of vicissitudes.
records.english_text_draft: Sometimes hearts will be stopped along with the lights just as souls will be veiled by the opacity of vicissitudes.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Sometimes the heart halts at the light (instead of going on to God). The light itself can become a veil, a place of arrest, just as the soul is veiled by the world's vicissitudes (its troubles). The danger: to grow attached to spiritual experiences rather than to the One who grants them.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Zarrūq: 'Do not halt at the lights, pass through them. The station is not the destination.' Ibn 'Ajībah: 'The light is a means, not an end. To halt at it is to turn the guide into an idol.'
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Be wary of spiritual experiences themselves (raptures, visions). They are means, not ends. To halt at them is to turn light into a veil. Go further.

records.section_title: Chapter 16: Be hopeful toward God
records.french_translation: In order to honour the inner lights (lights of innermost secrets), He veiled them with opaque exteriors so that they will not be degraded by the exteriors or be accused of seeking fame.
records.english_text_draft: In order to honour the inner lights (lights of innermost secrets), He veiled them with opaque exteriors so that they will not be degraded by the exteriors or be accused of seeking fame.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: To honour the inner lights of the saints, God has veiled them beneath ordinary appearances (opaque, unseeing). Thus the inner light is protected: it is not degraded by unworthy gazes, nor charged with seeking fame. The saints are ordinary in appearance.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: The hadith: 'My saints are beneath My domes, none other knows them.' Sanctity is hidden by ordinary appearance. Zarrūq: 'The display of sanctity is the loss of sanctity.'
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Be wary of those who parade their spirituality. True light conceals itself. Spiritual authenticity is discreet, ordinary, almost invisible. Display degrades it.

records.section_title: Chapter 17: The secrets of the saints
records.french_translation: Glorified be He who has not made any sign indicative of His saints except for the signs indicative of Him, and who has not led anyone to them except for the one who He wanted to lead to Him.
records.english_text_draft: Glorified be He who has not made any sign indicative of His saints except for the signs indicative of Him, and who has not led anyone to them except for the one who He wanted to lead to Him.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Glory be to God! He has set no signs pointing specifically towards His saints, but only signs that lead to Him. And He guides to the saints only those whom He wills to guide to Himself. The saints are like mirrors: they refer back to God. One does not see them directly; one sees God through them.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Hadith: 'My saints are beneath My domes, none but Me knows them.' Zarrūq: 'To seek the saints for their own sake is a dead end. To seek God through them is the path.'
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Do not seek the saints for their own sake, but for God's. They are guides, not idols. And they reveal themselves only to those who sincerely seek God.

records.section_title: Chapter 17: The secrets of the saints
records.french_translation: Sometimes He discloses to you the invisibilities of His realm but prevents you from having a glimpse of the secrets of servants.
records.english_text_draft: Sometimes He discloses to you the invisibilities of His realm but prevents you from having a glimpse of the secrets of servants.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Sometimes God unveils to you His own secrets (the unseen kingdom, malakūt) yet veils from you the secrets of His servants (the saints). It is at times easier to reach God than to understand a saint. Even Moses did not understand Khidr at first.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: The story of Moses and Khidr (Quran 18:60-82) illustrates this. Moses had an opening upon God, yet did not understand Khidr's actions. Zarrūq: 'Do not judge the servants of God, even if you have lofty openings.'
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Do not judge the servants of God. Even with great spiritual openness, one may fail to understand the saints. Their secret belongs to God. Set that judgement aside.

records.section_title: Chapter 17: The secrets of the saints
records.french_translation: He who happens to know the secrets of servants without accommodating himself to divine mercy finds his knowledge an ordeal and a precursor of the evils to come.
records.english_text_draft: He who happens to know the secrets of servants without accommodating himself to divine mercy finds his knowledge an ordeal and a precursor of the evils to come.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Whoever comes to know the secrets of the saints (their states, their apparent weaknesses) without being himself imbued with divine mercy (that is, without forgiving, without understanding) will see this knowledge turn against him: it will become a trial (fitna) and an omen of evil. Knowledge without mercy hardens the heart.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Zarrūq: 'Knowledge without mercy is a poison. It kills the one who possesses it and those who approach it.' Al-Sharqāwī: 'The dry, harsh scholar is more dangerous than an ignorant man.'
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Spiritual knowledge without grace makes one critical, arrogant, destructive. If you have knowledge of others (their faults), let it be accompanied by mercy, lest you destroy yourself.

records.section_title: Chapter 17: The secrets of the saints
records.french_translation: The body's share in disobedience is outward and apparent, while its share in obedience is inward and hidden. Treating what is hidden (of ailments) is very difficult.
