records.english_text_draft: The body's share in disobedience is outward and apparent, while its share in obedience is inward and hidden. Treating what is hidden (of ailments) is very difficult.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: The body has an apparent interest in disobedience (immediate, visible pleasure). Its interest in obedience is hidden (spiritual, invisible benefit). It is therefore easier to persuade the body to sin (since the gain is visible) than to obey (since the gain is hidden). Treating the hidden ailments of the soul is harder than tending the body.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Zarrūq: 'Sin pays in cash (pleasure), obedience pays on term (salvation). Man prefers the cash.'
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: The comfort of sin is visible; the fruits of obedience are deferred. This is why constancy is hard. It takes a faith that sees the invisible to prefer obedience.

records.section_title: Chapter 17: The secrets of the saints
records.french_translation: Sometimes ostentation invades you in a place where nobody notices you.
records.english_text_draft: Sometimes ostentation invades you in a place where nobody notices you.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Ostentation can be invisible: one performs a good deed in secret, yet secretly desires that people know of it, or one congratulates oneself. This is hidden ostentation, the 'hidden shirk' (ar-riyā' al-khafī). It is more dangerous than visible ostentation.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: The Prophet said: 'Shirk in my community is more hidden than the step of an ant upon a black rock in the dark of night.' Zarrūq: 'The worst show-off is he who admires himself in secret.'
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Even in secret, one can be a hypocrite (seeking God's approval, or one's own, in a subtle way). Purifying intention is the work of every moment, even (and above all) in privacy.

records.section_title: Chapter 17: The secrets of the saints
records.french_translation: Your craving for getting people's attention to your distinctions betrays your insincerity in servanthood.
records.english_text_draft: Your craving for getting people's attention to your distinctions betrays your insincerity in servanthood.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: The desire to show people your distinctions (qualities, spiritual gifts) is proof of insincerity. The true servant does not seek the attention of people. He seeks the attention of God. The moment we desire that people notice us, we step out of pure servanthood.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: The Prophet: 'He who seeks to be known, God will make him known then humiliated.' Humility consists in preferring the unknown, invisibility.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Examine your heart: do we secretly desire that people notice us, admire us, speak to us? That is a sign of insincerity. The sincere servant prefers obscurity, anonymity, the indifference of people.

records.section_title: Chapter 17: The secrets of the saints
records.french_translation: Let Allah's taking notice of you outshine people's taking notice of you; and contemplate His treatment of you to outshine their treatment of you.
records.english_text_draft: Let Allah's taking notice of you outshine people's taking notice of you; and contemplate His treatment of you to outshine their treatment of you.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Let the attention God gives you (His gaze, His loving watchfulness) wholly eclipse the attention of people. Contemplate God's actions towards you (His blessings, His trials), not people's actions towards you (their praise or their blame). Change your frame of reference.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: To shift one's gaze from creatures to the Creator. Zarrūq: 'God's opinion of you is infinitely more important than people's opinion. Meditate on what God does for you, not on what people say of you.'
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Practice: when you grow anxious about others' gaze, remember that God is watching you. His gaze is more important, more real, more impactful. Shift your attention.

records.section_title: Chapter 17: The secrets of the saints
records.french_translation: Those who know Allah contemplate Him in everything; those who are made extinct by Him are absent from everything; and those who love Him prefer nothing to Him.
records.english_text_draft: Those who know Allah contemplate Him in everything; those who are made extinct by Him are absent from everything; and those who love Him prefer nothing to Him.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Three spiritual degrees: 1) Those who know God (the knowers): they contemplate Him in all things (they see His traces). 2) Those who are extinguished in Him (the enraptured): they are absent to all things (they perceive only Him). 3) Those who love God (the lovers): they prefer nothing to Him (all else is secondary). Three faces of spiritual perfection.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Knowledge (ma'rifa), extinction (fanā'), love (maḥabba). Zarrūq: 'Each of these degrees is a gateway. What matters is to enter.'
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Three possible ways, three experiences. Do not rank them too strictly: some souls are more contemplative, others more loving, others more united. What matters is authenticity.

records.section_title: Chapter 17: The secrets of the saints
records.french_translation: You are veiled from Allah by His intense proximity to you.
records.english_text_draft: You are veiled from Allah by His intense proximity to you.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: A paradox: God is so near that we do not see Him, as we do not see our own hand when it is pressed against the eye, as we do not see our own eyes without a mirror. His utter nearness becomes a veil. The problem is not distance, but excessive nearness.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: The Quran: 'We are nearer to him than his jugular vein' (50:16). Zarrūq: 'God is not far, He is too near to be perceived by limited senses.'
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Do not seek God far from yourself. He is nearer than your breath. The veil is your own incapacity to perceive this intimate nearness. There is no distance to cross, but a consciousness to awaken.

records.section_title: Chapter 17: The secrets of the saints
records.french_translation: He was veiled because of His intense presence and He became unseen because of His great light.
records.english_text_draft: He was veiled because of His intense presence and He became unseen because of His great light.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: The same idea: His presence is so intense that it becomes veiling. His light is so great that it becomes blinding (like the sun, which cannot be looked at directly). It is not an absence, but a superabundance. The problem is not lack, but excess.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: The metaphor of the sun: the brighter it shines, the more it dazzles. Al-Sharqāwī: 'God is the spiritual Sun. Our earthly eyes cannot fix upon Him.'
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: God is not hidden by darkness, but by an excess of light. The problem is not absence, but superabundance. This is good news: He is not far, He is too present.

records.section_title: Chapter 18: Human prayer and the divine gifts
records.french_translation: Let not your pleading be the cause of His bounty because it will weaken your understanding of Him. Let your pleading be a means of expressing your servanthood and fulfilling (His) rights of lordship.
records.english_text_draft: Let not your pleading be the cause of His bounty because it will weaken your understanding of Him. Let your pleading be a means of expressing your servanthood and fulfilling (His) rights of lordship.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Do not regard your asking as the cause of the divine gift. That would weaken your understanding of God. Asking is an expression of servanthood, not a cause.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Zarrūq: 'Prayer is not an instrumental cause, it is an adab'.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: God gives out of pure generosity, not because we ask. Asking is our way of acknowledging our poverty, not a technique for obtaining.

records.section_title: Chapter 18: Human prayer and the divine gifts
records.french_translation: How can the latter (your pleading) be the cause of the former (His bounty)?
records.english_text_draft: How can the latter (your pleading) be the cause of the former (His bounty)?

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: A rhetorical question: how could your asking (recent, contingent) be the cause of the divine gift (prior, eternal)?
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: God knows what you need before you ask it.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Asking does not alter the divine decree. It is a way of entering the flow of the gift, not of triggering it.

records.section_title: Chapter 18: Human prayer and the divine gifts
records.french_translation: Far be it from the eternal decree to be dependent on causes.
records.english_text_draft: Far be it from the eternal decree to be dependent on causes.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: The eternal decree (al-qaḍā') depends on no cause. Causes are appearances.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Absolute determinism from the divine standpoint.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Do not make causality in the world absolute. From God's vantage, the decree is first, the causes (including your prayer) are second.

records.section_title: Chapter 18: Human prayer and the divine gifts
records.french_translation: His providence for you is not thanks to anything from you. Where were you when His providence approached you and His patronage came to you? There was neither sincerity of deeds nor (subsequent) states of mind in His eternity. There was nothing except pure grace and sublime bounties.
records.english_text_draft: His providence for you is not thanks to anything from you. Where were you when His providence approached you and His patronage came to you? There was neither sincerity of deeds nor (subsequent) states of mind in His eternity. There was nothing except pure grace and sublime bounties.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Divine providence is not a response to your acts. Before you existed, God was already providing for you in eternity. Only His pure grace counts.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Al-Sharqāwī: 'God is al-Raḥmān, the Merciful even before mercy has an object'.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: A radical reminder: nothing in you (sincerity, state) caused God's blessings. They are prior to you and freely given. Humility and gratitude take root there.

records.section_title: Chapter 18: Human prayer and the divine gifts
records.french_translation: He knew that servants would expect the appearance of the mystery of providence, so He said, 'Allah will choose for His special mercy whom He pleases'. And He knew if He left them like that, they would have abandoned deeds, depending too much on the Eternal, so He said: 'Surely the mercy of Allah is nigh to those who do good'.
records.english_text_draft: He knew that servants would expect the appearance of the mystery of providence, so He said, 'Allah will choose for His special mercy whom He pleases'. And He knew if He left them like that, they would have abandoned deeds, depending too much on the Eternal, so He said: 'Surely the mercy of Allah is nigh to those who do good'.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: God says: mercy is according to His will (to forestall presumption), yet He also says that it is near to those who do good (to forestall the abandoning of deeds). A tension between grace and responsibility.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: The dialectic between predestination and free will in the Quran.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: A subtle balance: do not believe that our deeds 'earn' mercy, yet do not abandon our deeds under the pretext of grace. One must act as if everything depended on oneself, and entrust everything to God as if all depended on God.

records.section_title: Chapter 18: Human prayer and the divine gifts
records.french_translation: Everything relies on the divine will which, in turn, depends on nothing at all.
records.english_text_draft: Everything relies on the divine will which, in turn, depends on nothing at all.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Everything depends on the divine will, and the divine will depends on nothing. It is absolute, first, without cause.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: The bedrock of tawhīd: there is no agent save God.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: The ultimate source of all reality is a free Will, unconditioned. Nothing compels it, nothing limits it.

records.section_title: Chapter 19: The importance of being in need
records.french_translation: Sometimes, good manners prompt them to keep away from asking; they are confident of His providence and too preoccupied with His invocation to ask Him.
records.english_text_draft: Sometimes, good manners prompt them to keep away from asking; they are confident of His providence and too preoccupied with His invocation to ask Him.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Out of good manners (adab), the knowers refrain from asking, trusting in divine providence and too occupied with invocation to ask.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: A lofty adab: not to ask, out of reverence and out of preoccupation.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: The prayer of petition can be a sign of absence. The state of presence is such that one need not even ask; one receives without asking.

records.section_title: Chapter 19: The importance of being in need
records.french_translation: Only those who are susceptible to forgetfulness need to be reminded; and only those who are prone to carelessness need to be cautioned.
records.english_text_draft: Only those who are susceptible to forgetfulness need to be reminded; and only those who are prone to carelessness need to be cautioned.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: To ask is to 'remind' God. But God is not subject to forgetfulness. So asking is for us, not for Him.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Prayer purifies us, it does not inform God.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Do not see prayer as a 'claim' or a 'reminder'. God does not forget. Prayer places us in a state of receptivity.

records.section_title: Chapter 19: The importance of being in need
records.french_translation: The hour of need is the festive period for novices.
records.english_text_draft: The hour of need is the festive period for novices.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: The moment of need is a festival for the initiate (the novice). For it is then that he turns towards God and experiences His providence.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Need (iḍṭirār) is a school of tawakkul.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Do not flee moments of distress. They are spiritual festivals: they set us before our own poverty and open the space of trust.

records.section_title: Chapter 19: The importance of being in need
records.french_translation: Sometimes you may get more benefit from the hour of need than from fasting and ritual prayers.
records.english_text_draft: Sometimes you may get more benefit from the hour of need than from fasting and ritual prayers.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Need can be more beneficial than fasting and prayer, for it breaks pride and forces surrender.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Zarrūq: 'One hour of humbled poverty is better than years of tranquil worship'.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Value hardships. A trial can produce more spiritual purification than routine acts of worship.

records.section_title: Chapter 19: The importance of being in need
records.french_translation: The hour of need is a carpet full of gifts.
records.english_text_draft: The hour of need is a carpet full of gifts.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: The time of need is a carpet strewn with gifts. It is a metaphor: in distress, the divine gifts abound.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: God is nearer to one who is in need (hadith: 'I am with those whose hearts are broken').
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Do not flee vulnerability. It is upon this carpet of distress that God lays down His gifts.

records.section_title: Chapter 19: The importance of being in need
records.french_translation: If you want to get gifts, you have to meet the criteria of (spiritual) poverty and deprivation—'Alms are only for the poor'.
records.english_text_draft: If you want to get gifts, you have to meet the criteria of (spiritual) poverty and deprivation—'Alms are only for the poor'.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: To receive the divine gifts, one must acknowledge oneself poor and destitute (spiritually). Alms are for the poor.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: To feel oneself 'faqīr' (poor towards God) is the condition for receiving.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Self-sufficiency blocks the gifts. Acknowledging one's destitution opens the floodgates. To be poor before God is to be rich in Him.

records.section_title: Chapter 19: The importance of being in need
records.french_translation: If you realise your attributes, He will help you with His attributes. If you realise your humility, He will help you with His glory. If you realise your incapacity, He will help you with His capability. If you realise your weakness, He will help you with His might and power.
records.english_text_draft: If you realise your attributes, He will help you with His attributes. If you realise your humility, He will help you with His glory. If you realise your incapacity, He will help you with His capability. If you realise your weakness, He will help you with His might and power.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: To acknowledge your own attributes (weakness, incapacity) allows God to aid you with His own (strength, power). Awareness of poverty draws down divine richness.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: The spiritual law: to each human lack there corresponds a divine attribute that comes to fill it.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Do not hide your weakness, but acknowledge it. It is the call that brings down strength from on high.

records.section_title: Chapter 20: Miracles are not a criterion
records.french_translation: Sometimes miracles are conferred on those whose righteousness is not up to the mark.
records.english_text_draft: Sometimes miracles are conferred on those whose righteousness is not up to the mark.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Even people of little virtue can perform miracles. Uprightness (istiqāma) is higher than miracles.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Zarrūq: 'Judge not a man's sanctity by his miracles, but by his conformity to the Law'.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Do not be impressed by the extraordinary. A humble, upright man who prays five times a day is greater than an arrogant worker of miracles.

records.section_title: Chapter 20: Miracles are not a criterion
records.french_translation: The proof of Allah’s putting you in a certain state is that He keeps you in it while it is fruitful.
records.english_text_draft: The proof of Allah’s putting you in a certain state is that He keeps you in it while it is fruitful.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: The proof that God has placed you in a certain state is that He keeps you in it by making it fruitful (spiritually).
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Fruitful constancy (thabāt) is the sign of divine acceptance.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Do not seek to change your situation if the present one, however difficult, is spiritually fruitful. Fruitful stability is worth more than barren instability.

records.section_title: Chapter 20: Miracles are not a criterion
records.french_translation: He who speaks under the auspices of his own virtues will be silenced by his wrongdoing to Allah, while he who speaks under the auspices of Allah’s beneficence to him will not be silenced when he misbehaves.
records.english_text_draft: He who speaks under the auspices of his own virtues will be silenced by his wrongdoing to Allah, while he who speaks under the auspices of Allah’s beneficence to him will not be silenced when he misbehaves.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Whoever speaks loudly of his virtues will be silenced by his faults. Whoever speaks in the name of divine beneficence will not be silenced by his lapses (for he repents).
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Humility protects from contradiction.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Do not boast. Our faults belie our claims. Speaking of God's gifts (not of our virtues) keeps us humble and spares us contradiction.

records.section_title: Chapter 20: Miracles are not a criterion
records.french_translation: The light of sages precedes their words so that their illumination is always followed by expression.
records.english_text_draft: The light of sages precedes their words so that their illumination is always followed by expression.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: The light of the sages precedes their words. Inner illumination is followed by outer expression. The sage speaks only after he has been enlightened.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: The opposite of the chatterer who speaks in the dark.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Authentic speech flows from an inner light, not from empty rhetoric. Keep silent until you have received the light.

records.section_title: Chapter 20: Miracles are not a criterion
records.french_translation: Every utterance appears clad in the attire of the heart that it issues from.
records.english_text_draft: Every utterance appears clad in the attire of the heart that it issues from.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Each word wears the garment of the heart from which it issues. An illumined heart produces luminous words.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: The hadith: 'Truly, there is in the body a morsel of flesh; if it is sound, the whole body is sound; if it is corrupt, the whole body is corrupt. It is the heart'.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Work upon your heart, for it is the heart that clothes our words. A pure heart produces pure and effective words.

records.section_title: Chapter 20: Miracles are not a criterion
records.french_translation: (Of the gnostics), he who is allowed to speak out is understood by people and his allusions are deciphered by them.
records.english_text_draft: (Of the gnostics), he who is allowed to speak out is understood by people and his allusions are deciphered by them.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: That knower who is permitted to speak is understood by people. His allusions are deciphered, for God upholds his communication.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: The gift of expression (bayān) granted to the saints.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: The effectiveness of spiritual speech comes not from oratorical talent, but from divine authorisation. If God permits, even the most obscure is understood.

records.section_title: Chapter 20: Miracles are not a criterion
records.french_translation: Sometimes, inner realities, transcendental knowledge, will make an obscured appearance if you are not allowed to express them.
records.english_text_draft: Sometimes, inner realities, transcendental knowledge, will make an obscured appearance if you are not allowed to express them.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: If one lacks the authorisation to express oneself, the inner realities manifest in a veiled, incomprehensible manner.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: The importance of permission (idhn) to speak of spiritual secrets.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: To speak without divine permission is to obscure what one would illumine. Silence is then preferable.

records.section_title: Chapter 20: Miracles are not a criterion
records.french_translation: Their expression is either due to an ecstatic overflow or for guiding a disciple. The former happens in the case of beginners, while the latter happens in the case of those who have acquired strength and are cognisant.
records.english_text_draft: Their expression is either due to an ecstatic overflow or for guiding a disciple. The former happens in the case of beginners, while the latter happens in the case of those who have acquired strength and are cognisant.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Two types of expression: ecstatic overflowing (the beginners) or deliberate guidance (the masters).
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: The distinction between speech under the sway of a state (ghalabah) and controlled speech for the purpose of teaching.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Do not confuse the ecstasy that speaks (sometimes confused) with the wisdom that teaches (clarifying). Both have their place, but the second is higher.

records.section_title: Chapter 20: Miracles are not a criterion
records.french_translation: An expression is nutritious to the needy listeners, and what you get from it is only what you are able to eat thereof.
records.english_text_draft: An expression is nutritious to the needy listeners, and what you get from it is only what you are able to eat thereof.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Spiritual speech is nourishment for thirsting listeners. Each takes from it what he can digest.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: The metaphor of spiritual nourishment: each receives according to his capacity.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Do not be surprised if the same words enlighten more or less according to the listeners. The word is one, but spiritual stomachs differ.

records.section_title: Chapter 20: Miracles are not a criterion
records.french_translation: Sometimes he who is very close to an exalted position expresses himself about it, and sometimes he who has already attained it expresses himself about it. It becomes very confusing except to those who are insightful.
records.english_text_draft: Sometimes he who is very close to an exalted position expresses himself about it, and sometimes he who has already attained it expresses himself about it. It becomes very confusing except to those who are insightful.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Sometimes one who is near a high rank speaks of that rank; sometimes one who has attained it speaks of it. The words can be confusing for those who lack discernment.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Only the man of inner core (lubb) distinguishes the speech of the aspirant from that of the realised one.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Do not judge by words. Two people may say the same thing, but one speaks from the near shore, the other from the far shore. Their words are identical, but their weight differs.

records.section_title: Chapter 20: Miracles are not a criterion
records.french_translation: Those who are still on the way should not give vent to their inspiration because it will demean their deeds in their hearts and prevent them from attaining sincerity with their Lord.
records.english_text_draft: Those who are still on the way should not give vent to their inspiration because it will demean their deeds in their hearts and prevent them from attaining sincerity with their Lord.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Those still on the way should not express their inspirations, for it might make them proud and corrupt their sincerity.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: The secret of the beginner: to keep silent about his states so as not to lose them.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Discretion protects the purity of spiritual experience. To speak too soon is to squander what has been received.

records.section_title: Chapter 20: Miracles are not a criterion
records.french_translation: Do not stretch out your hand to take something from creatures if you do not understand that the (real) donor among them is your Lord. If you do so, you can accept what knowledge suits you best.
records.english_text_draft: Do not stretch out your hand to take something from creatures if you do not understand that the (real) donor among them is your Lord. If you do so, you can accept what knowledge suits you best.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Take nothing from a creature unless you see that the true giver is God. If you see this, you may accept what suits you.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: To take from creatures without seeing God is shirk. To take while seeing God is gnosis.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: To accept a gift is licit, provided you know where it truly comes from. Do not thank the creature as though it were the source.

records.section_title: Chapter 20: Miracles are not a criterion
records.french_translation: If the gnostic is reluctant to solicit his Lord for his needs, because he is satisfied with His will, then why is he not reluctant to solicit His creatures for his needs?
records.english_text_draft: If the gnostic is reluctant to solicit his Lord for his needs, because he is satisfied with His will, then why is he not reluctant to solicit His creatures for his needs?

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: If the knower hesitates to ask of God (out of adab), all the more should he hesitate to ask of creatures. The incoherence of one who dares not ask God yet asks of men.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Zarrūq: 'To ask of men is a humiliation; to ask of God is an honour'.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: If out of reverence you are ashamed to ask of God, how are you not ashamed to ask of men? Consistency would have you refrain from asking anyone, or ask first of God.

records.section_title: Chapter 21: Choosing the hard, and the obligatory acts
records.french_translation: If you are confronted with two alternatives, consider what is more difficult to you and choose it because nothing weighs on you except what is true.
records.english_text_draft: If you are confronted with two alternatives, consider what is more difficult to you and choose it because nothing weighs on you except what is true.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Between two options, choose the one harder for the ego. For what is heavy for the ego is often what is true and beneficial for the spirit.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: The principle of the inversion of values: the comfort of the ego is often the path of ruin; hardship is the path of elevation.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: The criterion: what resists the ego is probably good for the soul. Do not flee difficulty, but face it.

records.section_title: Chapter 21: Choosing the hard, and the obligatory acts
records.french_translation: A sign of one’s adherence to passion is enthusiasm to carry out supererogatory deeds and laziness in doing obligatory deeds.
records.english_text_draft: A sign of one’s adherence to passion is enthusiasm to carry out supererogatory deeds and laziness in doing obligatory deeds.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: A sign of attachment to passion: being eager for supererogatory works yet slothful towards the obligatory ones. The ego prefers what it chooses (the supererogatory) over what it owes (the obligatory).
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Al-Sharqāwī: 'He who neglects the fard (obligatory) for the nāfil (supererogatory) is one gone astray'.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Priorities: first the obligations, then the surplus. Regularity in the obligatory is worth more than intermittent enthusiasm for the supererogatory.

records.section_title: Chapter 21: Choosing the hard, and the obligatory acts
records.french_translation: He made (obligatory) deeds limited to specific times so that procrastination does not deflect you from them; and He made the time ample enough so that you have your share of choice.
records.english_text_draft: He made (obligatory) deeds limited to specific times so that procrastination does not deflect you from them; and He made the time ample enough so that you have your share of choice.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: God has fixed set times for the obligations (e.g. the prayers) to forestall procrastination, yet He has given a window of time wide enough that you retain a choice (no absolute constraint).
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: The wisdom of the prayer times: neither too rigid (no leeway), nor too lax (the risk of negligence).
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Between constraint and laxity, the Law (sharī'a) offers a balanced frame: a fixed time for obligation, yet with room to manoeuvre.

records.section_title: Chapter 21: Choosing the hard, and the obligatory acts
records.french_translation: He knows about the lethargy of servants in dealing with Him so he made obedience to Him obligatory for them, herding them to obedience fettered by obligation. Your Lord is surprised to see people being herded to Paradise in fetters.
records.english_text_draft: He knows about the lethargy of servants in dealing with Him so he made obedience to Him obligatory for them, herding them to obedience fettered by obligation. Your Lord is surprised to see people being herded to Paradise in fetters.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: God knows our sloth. He has made obedience obligatory, driving us towards Paradise in fetters (of obligation). It is astonishing to see people dragged to Paradise in chains (of obligation).
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Hadith: 'Allah marvels at a people dragged to Paradise in chains' (after having been prisoners of war).
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Legal obligation (fard) is a mercy: it drags us towards the good in spite of our inertia. Even without enthusiasm, obligation saves us.

records.section_title: Chapter 21: Choosing the hard, and the obligatory acts
records.french_translation: He made it obligatory for you to serve Him. It means He made it obligatory for you to enter His paradise.
records.english_text_draft: He made it obligatory for you to serve Him. It means He made it obligatory for you to enter His paradise.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: By making obedience obligatory, God has made your entry into Paradise obligatory (if you obey). This is a mercy.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: The fard (obligation) is a compelled path towards felicity.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Do not see obligation as a constraint, but as a safety net. God obliges us to worship Him so as to oblige us to Paradise.

records.section_title: Chapter 21: Choosing the hard, and the obligatory acts
records.french_translation: Those who consider it amazing that Allah saves them from passion and takes them out of their forgetfulness actually deem divine faculties feeble. And it is (only) Allah who prevails over all things.
records.english_text_draft: Those who consider it amazing that Allah saves them from passion and takes them out of their forgetfulness actually deem divine faculties feeble. And it is (only) Allah who prevails over all things.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: To be astonished that God could save you from passion and heedlessness is to doubt His power. God prevails over all things.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: The closing verse (18:45) recalls that nothing is impossible for God.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Do not limit God by our limited experience. To save man from his passions is not difficult for God. Have faith in this possibility.

records.section_title: Chapter 21: Choosing the hard, and the obligatory acts
records.french_translation: Sometimes darkness may envelop you in order that He make you aware of the importance of His graces He conferred on you.
records.english_text_draft: Sometimes darkness may envelop you in order that He make you aware of the importance of His graces He conferred on you.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Sometimes God wraps you in darkness to make you aware of the worth of His blessings, which you had taken for granted.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Absence reveals presence. Loss reveals worth.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: The temporary deprivation of a blessing is a divine pedagogy meant to awaken gratitude.

records.section_title: Chapter 21: Choosing the hard, and the obligatory acts
records.french_translation: He who does not know the importance of (divine) graces in their presence does know it in their absence.
records.english_text_draft: He who does not know the importance of (divine) graces in their presence does know it in their absence.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Whoever does not value blessings while he has them, values them when he loses them. So is man made.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: A proverb: 'One appreciates health only when one is ill'.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Learn to be grateful before the loss. Do not wait for absence to value presence.

records.section_title: Chapter 21: Choosing the hard, and the obligatory acts
records.french_translation: Do not let the abundance of graces dazzle you, so that they keep you away from your obligation of gratitude, because it will make your rank plummet.
records.english_text_draft: Do not let the abundance of graces dazzle you, so that they keep you away from your obligation of gratitude, because it will make your rank plummet.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: The abundance of blessings should not so dazzle you as to draw you away from gratitude. Otherwise your spiritual station will fall.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Pride and ingratitude bring about a fall.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: The more one receives, the more one must be grateful. Abundance is a test: either it raises you through gratitude, or it casts you down through forgetfulness.

records.section_title: Chapter 21: Choosing the hard, and the obligatory acts
records.french_translation: When sweet passions reign over your heart it will result in chronic diseases.
records.english_text_draft: When sweet passions reign over your heart it will result in chronic diseases.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: When sweet passions dominate the heart, they cause chronic (incurable) diseases within it.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: A medical metaphor: illicit pleasures are 'spiritual diabetes'.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Pleasant sins are the most dangerous, for one does not wish to part with them. They corrode the heart slowly.

records.section_title: Chapter 21: Choosing the hard, and the obligatory acts
records.french_translation: Passion cannot be driven out from the heart except by an alarming fear or an impatient desire.
records.english_text_draft: Passion cannot be driven out from the heart except by an alarming fear or an impatient desire.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Passion leaves the heart only through a fear that alarms or an impatient longing (for God). Two forces: fear (khawf) or love (shawq).
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Fear and love are the two horsemen that drive out the passions.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: To overcome the passions, a stronger emotion is needed: either the fear of God, or a burning yearning for Him.

records.section_title: Chapter 21: Choosing the hard, and the obligatory acts
records.french_translation: Just as He does not like deeds tinged with hypocrisy, He does not like a heart tinged with hypocrisy. He does not accept deeds tinged with hypocrisy, while He does not come near a heart tinged with hypocrisy.
records.english_text_draft: Just as He does not like deeds tinged with hypocrisy, He does not like a heart tinged with hypocrisy. He does not accept deeds tinged with hypocrisy, while He does not come near a heart tinged with hypocrisy.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: God does not love acts tainted with hypocrisy, nor does He love the hypocritical heart. Hypocritical acts are not accepted; the hypocritical heart is rejected.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Hypocrisy (nifāq) is graver than ostentation (riyā'), for it corrupts the heart.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Hypocrisy is not only an act, it is a state of the heart. One must purify one's heart so that God may draw near to it.

records.section_title: Chapter 22: Two kinds of divine light
records.french_translation: (There are two types of lights:) Lights that are allowed to reach and lights that are allowed to enter.
records.english_text_draft: (There are two types of lights:) Lights that are allowed to reach and lights that are allowed to enter.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Two lights: the one that reaches the surface of the heart (the believer feels the presence) and the one that enters into the heart (the believer is no longer preoccupied with anything but God).
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: The distinction between 'wārid' (inspiration) and 'tamkīn' (establishment).
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: The first light grants faith and awareness. The second light transforms the entire being. Do not confuse the two.

records.section_title: Chapter 22: Two kinds of divine light
records.french_translation: Sometimes lights come to you and, finding the heart stuffed with earthly figures, go back to where they came from.
records.english_text_draft: Sometimes lights come to you and, finding the heart stuffed with earthly figures, go back to where they came from.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Sometimes the divine lights come, but they find the heart full of worldly preoccupations, and they depart.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: A cluttered heart is not receptive.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: To receive the light, one must make an emptiness. A heart filled with cares, projects and attachments drives the lights away.

records.section_title: Chapter 22: Two kinds of divine light
records.french_translation: Empty your heart of vicissitudes to fill it up with intuitions and mysteries.
records.english_text_draft: Empty your heart of vicissitudes to fill it up with intuitions and mysteries.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Empty your heart of vicissitudes (shifting preoccupations) so as to fill it with intuitions and mysteries.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Emptiness (farāgh) is the condition of fullness (mala').
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: The spiritual labour: first to empty (detachment, silence), then to fill (inspiration, knowledge).

records.section_title: Chapter 22: Two kinds of divine light
records.french_translation: Do not think His present comes tardily; but rather think your receptivity is slow.
records.english_text_draft: Do not think His present comes tardily; but rather think your receptivity is slow.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Do not think that the divine gift is delayed. Think rather that your receptivity is slow. The gift is there; you are not ready.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Zarrūq: 'The gift is ever present, but the vessel is blocked'.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: A shift of perspective: instead of complaining that God does not give, ask yourself why you do not receive. The problem lies within us.

records.section_title: Chapter 22: Two kinds of divine light
records.french_translation: It is possible to delay discharging time-bound obligations; but it is not possible to delay discharging the obligation inherent in every moment, because there is no moment without your having a new obligation or a more serious matter from Allah. How can you discharge in this moment another moment’s obligation when you have not discharged obligations from Allah in it?
records.english_text_draft: It is possible to delay discharging time-bound obligations; but it is not possible to delay discharging the obligation inherent in every moment, because there is no moment without your having a new obligation or a more serious matter from Allah. How can you discharge in this moment another moment’s obligation when you have not discharged obligations from Allah in it?

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: The fixed-hour obligations (the prayers) may be deferred. But the obligation inherent in each instant cannot be deferred: every moment has its own obligation (gratitude, patience, repentance, and so on).
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: The 'waqt' (present moment) has its rights.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: The duty of the present moment is sacred. Do not live in anticipation or in regret. Each instant has its own demand.

records.section_title: Chapter 22: Two kinds of divine light
records.french_translation: Les saints connaissent les deux nuits : celle qui précède la lumière, et celle qui la suit.

records.section_title: Chapter 22: Two kinds of divine light
records.french_translation: You have not loved anything without being a slave of it. But Allah does not want you to be a slave of someone else.
records.english_text_draft: You have not loved anything without being a slave of it. But Allah does not want you to be a slave of someone else.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: When you love something, you become its slave (you wish to please it, you fear to lose it). God does not want you to be the slave of anything other than Him.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Love (ḥubb) towards other than God is a form of idolatry.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Examine your attachments. Everything you love more than God makes you a slave. The love of God alone frees you from all servanthood.

records.section_title: Chapter 22: Two kinds of divine light
records.french_translation: Neither does your obedience benefit Him nor does your disobedience harm Him. It is only for your benefit that He commanded certain things and proscribed certain others.
records.english_text_draft: Neither does your obedience benefit Him nor does your disobedience harm Him. It is only for your benefit that He commanded certain things and proscribed certain others.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Your obedience does not profit Him, your disobedience does not harm Him. The commands and prohibitions are for your good, not for His interest.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: God is al-Ghanī (the Self-Sufficient). Man is al-faqīr (the poor one).
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: God has no need of our worship. It is we who need to worship Him, for our own equilibrium and our salvation.

records.section_title: Chapter 22: Two kinds of divine light
records.french_translation: If someone draws closer to Him, it does not add to His power; and if someone withdraws from Him it does not diminish His power.
records.english_text_draft: If someone draws closer to Him, it does not add to His power; and if someone withdraws from Him it does not diminish His power.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: The nearness of a servant adds nothing to the divine power; his remoteness takes nothing from it. God is absolute, unchanged.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: God neither gains nor loses by human actions.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Our faith or our unbelief does not affect God. It is we who are affected. God remains perfectly Himself.

records.section_title: Chapter 23: Reaching God through contemplation
records.french_translation: Your reaching Allah is your reaching knowledge about Him. For our Lord is beyond being connected to something or something being connected to Him.
records.english_text_draft: Your reaching Allah is your reaching knowledge about Him. For our Lord is beyond being connected to something or something being connected to Him.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: To attain God is to know Him through knowledge (maʿrifa). He is beyond every physical or ontological connection. The union is intellectual and spiritual, not physical.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: God unites with nothing and nothing unites with Him. 'Union' (waṣl) is a metaphor for intense knowledge.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Do not seek a 'physical' fusion with God (it is impossible). Seek the intimate knowledge (maʿrifa) that transforms the gaze.

records.section_title: Chapter 23: Reaching God through contemplation
records.french_translation: Your proximity to Him is that you can contemplate His proximity (to you). Otherwise, how on earth can you attain His proximity?
records.english_text_draft: Your proximity to Him is that you can contemplate His proximity (to you). Otherwise, how on earth can you attain His proximity?

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Your nearness to God is to contemplate His nearness to you. You cannot 'draw near' physically; the drawing near is mental, contemplative.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: God is 'nearer than your jugular vein' (Quran 50:16). Awareness of this nearness is the nearness.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Do not try to 'climb' towards God. Realise that He is already there, nearer than yourself. To contemplate this nearness is to be near.

records.section_title: Chapter 23: Reaching God through contemplation
records.french_translation: In the time of illumination, the truths come altogether, and their explanations come only after retention. 'So when We promulgate it, follow thou its recital. Nay more, it is for Us to explain it (and make it clear)'.
records.english_text_draft: In the time of illumination, the truths come altogether, and their explanations come only after retention. 'So when We promulgate it, follow thou its recital. Nay more, it is for Us to explain it (and make it clear)'.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: At the moment of illumination (tajallī), the truths come all at once, not in detail. Their explanation (tafsīr) comes afterwards, with retention (repetition, memorisation). The Quran says: God then explains.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Revelation (tanzīl) is followed by explanation (tafsīr). Illumination is first global, then detailed.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Do not try to understand everything instantly. Illumination gives the overall vision; the labour of study and repetition gives the details.

records.section_title: Chapter 23: Reaching God through contemplation
records.french_translation: When divine inspiration (comes), do away with your routine. 'Kings, when they enter a country, despoil it'.
records.english_text_draft: When divine inspiration (comes), do away with your routine. 'Kings, when they enter a country, despoil it'.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: When the divine inspiration (wārid) comes, leave aside your routines (your ordinary habits). For the royal inspiration 'dispossesses' the heart of its habits in order to install its presence.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: The Quranic metaphor (27:34): the entry of the king (inspiration) empties the land (the heart) of its occupants (ordinary thoughts).
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Authentic inspiration overturns, disturbs. If it changes nothing, then it is not real. Let it 'dispossess' your routines.

records.section_title: Chapter 23: Reaching God through contemplation
records.french_translation: Since inspiration comes from the presence of the Omnipotent, nothing can resist it without being torn apart. 'Nay, We hurl the truth against falsehood, and it knocks out its brain, and behold, falsehood perishes!'
records.english_text_draft: Since inspiration comes from the presence of the Omnipotent, nothing can resist it without being torn apart. 'Nay, We hurl the truth against falsehood, and it knocks out its brain, and behold, falsehood perishes!'

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: The inspiration comes from the All-Powerful. Nothing resists it without being broken. It pulverises the false (passions, illusions). The truth triumphs brutally.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Verse 21:18 illustrates the violent victory of truth over falsehood.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Divine inspiration is not gentle and comfortable. It is a force that breaks resistances. Accept this salutary violence.

records.section_title: Chapter 23: Reaching God through contemplation
records.french_translation: How can Allah be concealed by something if He is apparent and visible in what He has been veiled?
records.english_text_draft: How can Allah be concealed by something if He is apparent and visible in what He has been veiled?

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: A rhetorical question: how could God be hidden by anything whatsoever, when He is manifest in the very thing that hides Him? The manifestation is stronger than the veil.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: God shows Himself in the veil itself. The veil is transparent for one who can see.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Nothing is so opaque as to hide God. The world, sin, ignorance themselves bear the mark of God. It is our gaze alone that is opaque.

records.section_title: Chapter 23: Reaching God through contemplation
records.french_translation: Do not despair if your deed, wherein you could not perceive a divine presence, is accepted. Sometimes, He will accept your deed the reward of which you cannot perceive right away.
records.english_text_draft: Do not despair if your deed, wherein you could not perceive a divine presence, is accepted. Sometimes, He will accept your deed the reward of which you cannot perceive right away.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Do not despair if your act (performed without any felt presence) is accepted. God sometimes accepts acts whose reward is not perceived immediately.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: The absence of feeling (lacking presence, lacking sweetness) is no sign of rejection. The reward may be deferred.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Do not appraise your acts by the pleasure felt. A dry but accepted act is worth more than a sweet but hypocritical one.

records.section_title: Chapter 23: Reaching God through contemplation
records.french_translation: Do not attach credibility to an inspiration whose reward you do not know. The real purpose of clouds is not to bring rain but to yield fruits.
records.english_text_draft: Do not attach credibility to an inspiration whose reward you do not know. The real purpose of clouds is not to bring rain but to yield fruits.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Do not trust an inspiration whose fruit (its concrete result) you do not know. The true aim of the clouds is not the rain, but the fruits (which come afterwards). The inspiration must bear fruit (change, virtues).
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Zarrūq: 'True inspiration is known by its fruits'.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Appraise a spiritual experience by its moral and behavioural consequences, not by its intensity. An inspiration without transformation is suspect.

records.section_title: Chapter 23: Reaching God through contemplation
records.french_translation: Do not seek the persistence of (divine) inspirations after their lights have dawned on you and their mysteries deposited in you, because in Allah you have one who enables you to dispense with everything, but nothing enables you to dispense with Allah.
records.english_text_draft: Do not seek the persistence of (divine) inspirations after their lights have dawned on you and their mysteries deposited in you, because in Allah you have one who enables you to dispense with everything, but nothing enables you to dispense with Allah.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Do not seek the persistence of inspirations after they have done their work. For God is sufficient unto Himself and lets you do without everything (inspirations included). Nothing lets you do without God.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: God is the only necessity. Inspirations are means, not ends.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Do not become attached to spiritual experiences (ecstasies, visions). They pass. God remains. Rest in Him, not in the gifts.

records.section_title: Chapter 23: Reaching God through contemplation
records.french_translation: Your seeking the persistence of something other than Him is proof that you have not found Him. Your alienation because of losing something other than Him is proof that you have not reached Him.
records.english_text_draft: Your seeking the persistence of something other than Him is proof that you have not found Him. Your alienation because of losing something other than Him is proof that you have not reached Him.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: To seek the persistence of anything other than God proves that you have not found Him. To be grieved at the loss of anything other than Him proves that you have not reached Him.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: The criterion of realisation: indifference to the presence or absence of what is not God.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Examine your attachments and your sorrows. If you suffer from the loss of a worldly good (or even of a spiritual experience), it is because God is not yet everything to you.

records.section_title: Chapter 24: The divine presence is primordial
records.french_translation: Divine grace, however diverse its manifestations may be, is attained through contemplating and drawing near to Him; Suffering, however diverse its manifestations may be, is due to His veil. The veil is the cause of the suffering, while the perfection of grace is achieved through looking at the countenance of Allah, the Generous.
records.english_text_draft: Divine grace, however diverse its manifestations may be, is attained through contemplating and drawing near to Him; Suffering, however diverse its manifestations may be, is due to His veil. The veil is the cause of the suffering, while the perfection of grace is achieved through looking at the countenance of Allah, the Generous.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Every grace is subordinate to the contemplation of God. Every suffering is owing to the veil (which hides God). The perfection of grace is to see Allah.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: The vision of God (ru'ya) is the summit of paradise. The veil is the summit of hell.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: A radical hierarchy: even in paradise, the material delights are secondary. The essential thing is to see God. Here below, the worst suffering is not illness or poverty, but the felt absence of God.

records.section_title: Chapter 24: The divine presence is primordial
records.french_translation: What prevents you from attaining insight causes the worries and grief in your heart.
records.english_text_draft: What prevents you from attaining insight causes the worries and grief in your heart.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: What prevents you from attaining inner vision (baṣīra) is the cause of your cares and your sorrows. Darkness engenders anguish.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: The absence of inner light makes the heart restless. Faith illumines and pacifies.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Our modern anxiety is not merely psychological: it is spiritual. The lack of inner vision (of meaning, of presence) renders us vulnerable to every disquiet.

records.section_title: Chapter 24: The divine presence is primordial
records.french_translation: His providing you with what is sufficient, and denying you what makes you exceed the bounds, means that He has perfected His grace on you.
records.english_text_draft: His providing you with what is sufficient, and denying you what makes you exceed the bounds, means that He has perfected His grace on you.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: God perfects His grace upon you when He gives you what is necessary (sufficiency) and withholds from you the superfluous (which would make you exceed the bounds).
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Excess is a source of pride and transgression. Contentment (qinā'a) is a great wealth.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Do not regard as a deprivation the fact of not having too much. It is a protection. What is necessary suffices; excess is a risk.

records.section_title: Chapter 24: The divine presence is primordial
records.french_translation: Let your happiness about something be less, so that your grief over it will be less.
records.english_text_draft: Let your happiness about something be less, so that your grief over it will be less.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Lessen your joy in things so as to lessen your sorrow in losing them. Moderate attachment avoids excessive suffering.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: A universal rule of wisdom: the stronger the attachment, the more painful the loss.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Do not forbid yourself joy, but temper it. Know that every good is lent, not given. Moderate joy is a more serene joy.

records.section_title: Chapter 24: The divine presence is primordial
records.french_translation: If you do not want to be isolated, do not take up the reins of a post that is not lasting.
records.english_text_draft: If you do not want to be isolated, do not take up the reins of a post that is not lasting.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: If you do not wish to know isolation (after the fall), do not take up the reins of an ephemeral position (power, prestige).
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Lofty worldly positions isolate and bring about a fall. Better humility and obscurity.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Do not covet positions of power or renown. They afford an illusion of company, but end in the bitter solitude of loss.

records.section_title: Chapter 24: The divine presence is primordial
records.french_translation: If the beginning made you desirous, the end will make you isolated. If the exteriors draw you near to it, the interiors prevent you from it.
records.english_text_draft: If the beginning made you desirous, the end will make you isolated. If the exteriors draw you near to it, the interiors prevent you from it.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: The beginning (of power) attracts; the end isolates. The outward (prestige) seduces; the inward (the reality) repels. Power is deceptive.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: The world (dunyā) is a fair promise and a sorry end.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Do not let yourself be seduced by brilliant beginnings. Contemplate the end. The outward is a veil; the inward is a warning.

records.section_title: Chapter 24: The divine presence is primordial
records.french_translation: He made the world a place of vicissitudes and a mine of dirtiness to make you repugnant to it.
records.english_text_draft: He made the world a place of vicissitudes and a mine of dirtiness to make you repugnant to it.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: God has made the world a place of vicissitudes (changes) and a mine of impurities so as to render it repugnant to you. The trials are there to detach you from the world.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Life's hardships are not accidents, but pedagogies of detachment.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Instead of cursing the world's troubles, give thanks for them: they remind you that this world is not your dwelling.

records.section_title: Chapter 24: The divine presence is primordial
records.french_translation: Since He knows that you will not accept mere advice, He makes you sample the tastes of this world so as to ease your separation from it.
records.english_text_draft: Since He knows that you will not accept mere advice, He makes you sample the tastes of this world so as to ease your separation from it.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: God knows that counsels alone do not suffice. He therefore makes you taste the bitterness of the world in order to ease your separation from it.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: The experience of suffering is more effective than the sermon.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Trials are forced 'detoxifications'. God makes us suffer the world so that we may wish to leave it.

records.section_title: Chapter 24: The divine presence is primordial
records.french_translation: Useful knowledge is knowledge whose rays dawn on your heart and take your heart out of its covering.
records.english_text_draft: Useful knowledge is knowledge whose rays dawn on your heart and take your heart out of its covering.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Useful knowledge is that whose rays illumine the heart and bring it forth from its husk (ignorance, attachments).
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Knowledge that does not illumine the heart is useless, however abundant it may be.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Appraise your learning not by its volume, but by its effect: does it make you more present, more free, more luminous?

records.section_title: Chapter 24: The divine presence is primordial
records.french_translation: The best knowledge is knowledge which is accompanied by fear (of Allah).
records.english_text_draft: The best knowledge is knowledge which is accompanied by fear (of Allah).

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: The best knowledge is that which is accompanied by the fear of God. Fear is the fruit and the sign of authentic knowledge.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: The Quran: 'Of His servants, only the learned fear Allah' (35:28).
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: A knowledge that does not engender the reverent fear of God is barren, even dangerous. Humility and fear are the safeguards of learning.

records.section_title: Chapter 24: The divine presence is primordial
records.french_translation: Knowledge, if it’s coupled with fear (of Allah), is in your favour; if not, it is against you.
records.english_text_draft: Knowledge, if it’s coupled with fear (of Allah), is in your favour; if not, it is against you.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Knowledge with fear is in your favour. Without fear, it is against you (a source of pride, of judgement, of ruin).
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Knowledge is a sword: in the hands of a humble man, it heals; in the hands of a proud man, it kills.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Do not accumulate learning without purifying your heart. Knowledge without fear swells the ego and removes you from God.

records.section_title: Chapter 24: The divine presence is primordial
records.french_translation: If it pains you to see people not coming to you or mistreating you, take recourse to your knowledge of Allah. If knowledge of Him does not satisfy you, then your suffering from dissatisfaction with knowledge of Him is greater than your suffering from pain caused by people.
records.english_text_draft: If it pains you to see people not coming to you or mistreating you, take recourse to your knowledge of Allah. If knowledge of Him does not satisfy you, then your suffering from dissatisfaction with knowledge of Him is greater than your suffering from pain caused by people.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: If people forsake you or mistreat you, take refuge in your knowledge of God. If that knowledge does not satisfy you, your misfortune is greater than the one caused by people.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: The knowledge of God is the greatest consolation. He who is not content with it is more to be pitied than one rejected by men.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: The true problem is not that people ignore you, it is that God does not suffice you. If God suffices you, the regard of people becomes insignificant.

records.section_title: Chapter 24: The divine presence is primordial
records.french_translation: He made you suffer at the hands of people so that you will not depend on them for repose. He wanted you to be troubled by everything so that nothing will alienate you from Him.
records.english_text_draft: He made you suffer at the hands of people so that you will not depend on them for repose. He wanted you to be troubled by everything so that nothing will alienate you from Him.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: God makes you suffer through people so that you do not seek repose with them. He wills that everything trouble you, so that nothing may remove you from Him.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: A radical pedagogy: discontent in all save God.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Do not curse relational difficulties. They are there to turn you away from creatures and towards the Creator. Perfect human relations would be a snare.

records.section_title: Chapter 24: The divine presence is primordial
records.french_translation: If you know that the Devil will not forget you, do not forget the one who holds your forelock.
records.english_text_draft: If you know that the Devil will not forget you, do not forget the one who holds your forelock.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: The Devil never forgets you. Do not, then, forget the One who holds the forelock of your hair (God, your absolute Master).
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: The metaphor of the 'forelock': God has hold of you. Fear not Satan, remember your Lord.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: If the enemy is ever vigilant, what then of the Friend? God is nearer and more attentive than Satan. Turn towards Him with the same constancy.

records.section_title: Chapter 24: The divine presence is primordial
records.french_translation: He made the Devil your enemy to take you to Him through him; and he stirred up your soul against you so as to make you constantly submit to Him.
records.english_text_draft: He made the Devil your enemy to take you to Him through him; and he stirred up your soul against you so as to make you constantly submit to Him.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: God has made the Devil your enemy in order to drive you to Him through him (by forcing you to seek refuge). He has stirred up your soul (nafs) against you in order to make you constantly submit to Him.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Adversities (outward and inward) are pedagogical means to make us have recourse to God.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Do not curse the Devil nor your carnal soul. They are instruments of God to keep you in humility and in constant recourse to Him. Without them, you would become autonomous and forgetful.

records.section_title: Chapter 24: The divine presence is primordial
records.french_translation: He who attaches weight to his humility is really proud, because humility emanates only from a sublime state. Therefore whenever you attach weight to your humility, you are really proud.
records.english_text_draft: He who attaches weight to his humility is really proud, because humility emanates only from a sublime state. Therefore whenever you attach weight to your humility, you are really proud.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: He who boasts of his humility is in reality proud. Humility is a quality that cannot be claimed. The moment one takes pride in it, one loses it.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Zarrūq: 'Humility is a snare: if you believe you have it, you have lost it'.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Do not measure your own humility. It is like wishing to measure your own shadow. Authentic humility is unaware of itself.

records.section_title: Chapter 24: The divine presence is primordial
records.french_translation: A humble man is not the one who thinks, when he humbles himself, that he is above what he did; on the other hand, the humble man is the one who thinks, when he humbles himself, that he is below what he did.
records.english_text_draft: A humble man is not the one who thinks, when he humbles himself, that he is above what he did; on the other hand, the humble man is the one who thinks, when he humbles himself, that he is below what he did.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: The proud man who humbles himself thinks himself above that act. The humble man, in humbling himself, thinks himself beneath it, unworthy.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: The difference lies in the intention and in the perception of self.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Humility is not a posture, it is a keen awareness of one's unworthiness. He who 'plays' at humility stands above it; he who lives it stands beneath it.

records.section_title: Chapter 24: The divine presence is primordial
records.french_translation: Real humility emanates from the contemplation of His sublimity and the enlightenment of His attributes. It is only the contemplation (of His) attributes that can take you away from (your own) attributes.
records.english_text_draft: Real humility emanates from the contemplation of His sublimity and the enlightenment of His attributes. It is only the contemplation (of His) attributes that can take you away from (your own) attributes.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: True humility comes from the contemplation of the divine sublimity. To contemplate the attributes of God makes you forget your own attributes (your pretensions).
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Practical negative theology: to empty oneself of one's attributes through the contemplation of the divine attributes.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: To be humble, do not concentrate on yourself (trying to lower your ego), but concentrate on God, on His grandeur. Humility is a by-product of contemplation.

records.section_title: Chapter 24: The divine presence is primordial
records.french_translation: The believer is too engaged with praising Allah to praise himself and is too engaged with the rights of Allah to remember his fortunes.
records.english_text_draft: The believer is too engaged with praising Allah to praise himself and is too engaged with the rights of Allah to remember his fortunes.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: The believer is too occupied with praising God to praise himself, and too occupied with the rights of God to remember his own fortunes.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Attention to the duty (wājib) eclipses attention to the good received (maqṣūd).
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Centre yourself on your duties towards God, not on your privileges. Active gratitude (praise) replaces sterile self-celebration.

records.section_title: Chapter 24: The divine presence is primordial
records.french_translation: The lover does not want a reward from his beloved, nor does he claim any objective. The true lover is the one who is generous to you and not the one you are generous to.
records.english_text_draft: The lover does not want a reward from his beloved, nor does he claim any objective. The true lover is the one who is generous to you and not the one you are generous to.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: The true lover asks for no reward. He gives, he does not wait. It is he who is generous towards the Beloved, not the reverse.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Pure love is gratuitous, disinterested, active.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Our relation to God must tend towards pure love. Not 'I love You because You give to me', but 'I love You, full stop'. It is a generous love, not a self-interested one.

records.section_title: Chapter 24: The divine presence is primordial
records.french_translation: Had it not been for the domains of the self, the journey of the wayfarers could not be successful; for there would neither have been a distance between you and Him that you traverse in your journey nor a barrier between you and Him that will be effaced by your reaching Him.
records.english_text_draft: Had it not been for the domains of the self, the journey of the wayfarers could not be successful; for there would neither have been a distance between you and Him that you traverse in your journey nor a barrier between you and Him that will be effaced by your reaching Him.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Without the domain of the ego (nafs), there would be no journey. The distance (created by the ego) is necessary for there to be a path to traverse and an obstacle to overcome.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: The ego is at once the problem and the condition of the journey. Without ego, no path, no merit.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Do not curse your ego. It is thanks to it that there is a path. The struggle against the ego is the journey. Without that struggle, no nearness is conquered, only a nearness undergone (without value).

records.section_title: Chapter 24: The divine presence is primordial
records.french_translation: He placed you in the intermediary world between His Dominion (the visible world) and His Kingdom (the invisible world) to teach you the majesty of your position among His creatures and that you are a jewel hidden in the oysters of His Creation.
records.english_text_draft: He placed you in the intermediary world between His Dominion (the visible world) and His Kingdom (the invisible world) to teach you the majesty of your position among His creatures and that you are a jewel hidden in the oysters of His Creation.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: God has placed you between the visible world (mulk) and the invisible world (malakūt) to show you your unique position: you are at once corporeal and spiritual, a 'hidden pearl'.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Man is a microcosm (ʿālam ṣaghīr) that sums up the two worlds.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: The human being is a bridge between matter and spirit. This position is a dignity and a responsibility. He may rise or he may sink.

records.section_title: Chapter 24: The divine presence is primordial
records.french_translation: The universe surrounds you only in terms of your corporeal existence, but it does not surround you in terms of your spiritual existence. He who exists in the universe is imprisoned by his surroundings and is confined in the temple of his body, so long as the domains of the invisible world are not opened to him.
records.english_text_draft: The universe surrounds you only in terms of your corporeal existence, but it does not surround you in terms of your spiritual existence. He who exists in the universe is imprisoned by his surroundings and is confined in the temple of his body, so long as the domains of the invisible world are not opened to him.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: The world surrounds you through your body. Through your spirit, you transcend it. As long as the invisible does not open, you are a prisoner of the body and of the world.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: The body is a prison; the soul is the bird that can take flight.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Our freedom is not in the body, but in the spirit. To develop the spiritual dimension is to escape from the prison of the material world.

records.section_title: Chapter 24: The divine presence is primordial
records.french_translation: You shall remain with the creatures till you contemplate the Creator; and once you contemplated Him, the creatures shall belong to you.
records.english_text_draft: You shall remain with the creatures till you contemplate the Creator; and once you contemplated Him, the creatures shall belong to you.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: As long as you do not contemplate the Creator, you remain at the level of the creatures (subject to them). Once you have contemplated God, the creatures become yours (you master them, they serve you).
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Contemplation (mushāhada) confers spiritual authority (wilāya).
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Seek God first. When you find Him, the world becomes an instrument, not a master. The order is reversed: God first, then the world (the servant).

records.section_title: Chapter 24: The divine presence is primordial
records.french_translation: Getting rid of human attributes is not a prerequisite for attaining constant sanctity. Sanctity is analogous to the light of the sun during the day. It appears in the horizon but is not part of it. Sometimes, the sun of His attributes rises on the night of your existence, while on other occasions, He takes it back from you and takes you back to yourself. So the sun is neither from nor to you; but it is something that comes on you.
records.english_text_draft: Getting rid of human attributes is not a prerequisite for attaining constant sanctity. Sanctity is analogous to the light of the sun during the day.
