It appears in the horizon but is not part of it. Sometimes, the sun of His attributes rises on the night of your existence, while on other occasions, He takes it back from you and takes you back to yourself. So the sun is neither from nor to you; but it is something that comes on you.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Sainthood (walāya) does not require divesting oneself of human attributes. The light of the sun does not belong to the horizon. It comes upon you; it is neither of you nor yours. God gives you the light and then withdraws it.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: The human attributes remain; sanctity is a superadded light, not a destruction of one's humanity.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Do not seek to 'become something other' than human. Sainthood is a light that illumines your humanity; it does not abolish it. Be humble: the light comes and departs; it does not belong to you.

records.section_title: Chapter 24: The divine presence is primordial
records.french_translation: He alludes with His creatures to the existence of His names, with His names to the existence of His attributes and with His attributes to the existence of His being, because it is impossible for attributes to stand alone. To those who have reached a state of spiritual ecstasy (jadhb), He reveals the perfection of His being; and then He takes them to the contemplation of His attributes. Afterwards, He takes them to the reflection of His names and then to the contemplation of His creatures. But as for those who are still on the way (sālikīn), the journey will be in the reverse order. Their end will be the beginning for the ecstatic, while the end for the latter will be the beginning for the former, albeit not in the strict sense of the word. Sometimes they meet on the way, one ascending and the other descending.
records.english_text_draft: He alludes with His creatures to the existence of His names, with His names to the existence of His attributes and with His attributes to the existence of His being, because it is impossible for attributes to stand alone. To those who have reached a state of spiritual ecstasy (jadhb), He reveals the perfection of His being; and then He takes them to the contemplation of His attributes. Afterwards, He takes them to the reflection of His names and then to the contemplation of His creatures. But as for those who are still on the way (sālikīn), the journey will be in the reverse order. Their end will be the beginning for the ecstatic, while the end for the latter will be the beginning for the former, albeit not in the strict sense of the word. Sometimes they meet on the way, one ascending and the other descending.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Two inverse courses: the wayfarer (sālik) sets out from the creatures → the names → the attributes → the divine Being. The enraptured one (majdhūb) sets out from the Being → the attributes → the names → the creatures. The two sometimes cross.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: The distinction between the 'traveller' (sālik) and the 'attracted one' (majdhūb). God draws some directly, others advance by stages.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: There are several ways to God. One progressive (asceticism, knowledge), the other through sudden grace (attraction). Neither is superior; they complete one another. Do not judge the other way.

records.section_title: Chapter 24: The divine presence is primordial
records.french_translation: The worth of the lights of hearts and of secrets is known only in the invisible world (the hereafter), whereas the lights of the sky do not appear only in the visible world (in this world).
records.english_text_draft: The worth of the lights of hearts and of secrets is known only in the invisible world (the hereafter), whereas the lights of the sky do not appear only in the visible world (in this world).

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: The worth of the lights of the heart is fully known only in the hereafter. The material lights (sun, stars) are visible here below.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: The visible world measures visible lights; the invisible measures spiritual lights.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Do not try to measure your spirituality by visible criteria. The true worth of your heart will be revealed later. Patience.

records.section_title: Chapter 24: The divine presence is primordial
records.french_translation: Getting the fruits of obedience in this world is glad tiding of the rewards due to its performer in the hereafter.
records.english_text_draft: Getting the fruits of obedience in this world is glad tiding of the rewards due to its performer in the hereafter.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: To receive the fruits of obedience in this world (inner peace, ease) is a good tiding (bushrā) of the reward in the hereafter.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: The sweetnesses of obedience are a foretaste of paradise.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: When you find pleasure in prayer, give thanks to God: it is a sign of what awaits you in the hereafter. It is an instalment, not the whole.

records.section_title: Chapter 24: The divine presence is primordial
records.french_translation: How can you seek reward for a deed which He donated to you? Or how can you seek reward for the sincerity which He gifted to you?
records.english_text_draft: How can you seek reward for a deed which He donated to you? Or how can you seek reward for the sincerity which He gifted to you?

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: You cannot ask for a reward for an act that God has given you (the act comes from Him), nor for the sincerity He has bestowed upon you.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: All is gift. Even sincerity is a gift. To ask a reward for a gift is unfitting.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: The utmost humility: to realise that even our good action and our good intention are gifts. Before God, we are never 'creditors'. We receive everything, we give nothing.

records.section_title: Chapter 24: The divine presence is primordial
records.french_translation: There are people whose lights precede their invocations, and some others whose invocations precede their lights. That is, there are invokers who invoke to illuminate their hearts, and invokers who became invokers because they illuminated their hearts.
records.english_text_draft: There are people whose lights precede their invocations, and some others whose invocations precede their lights. That is, there are invokers who invoke to illuminate their hearts, and invokers who became invokers because they illuminated their hearts.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Two types: 1) those whose light precedes the invocation (they are illumined first, then invoke). 2) those whose invocation precedes the light (they invoke in order to obtain the light).
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: The ecstatic (majdhūb) receives the light without effort; the initiate (sālik) acquires it through effort.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Do not envy those who receive without effort. Each way is fitting. What matters is to invoke and to receive the light, whatever the order.

records.section_title: Chapter 24: The divine presence is primordial
records.french_translation: Invocation becomes manifest through internal contemplation.
records.english_text_draft: Invocation becomes manifest through internal contemplation.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: The invocation (dhikr) outwardly manifests the inward contemplation (mushāhada). It is the expression of the state of the heart.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Vocal dhikr is the reflection of the dhikr of the heart.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: A mechanical invocation without inner contemplation is hollow. The aim is to align the tongue and the heart. Let your dhikr manifest your inner presence.

records.section_title: Chapter 24: The divine presence is primordial
records.french_translation: Before asking you to bear witness (to Him), He made you witness. The outward signs bespeak His divinity, while the heart and secrets have realised His oneness.
records.english_text_draft: Before asking you to bear witness (to Him), He made you witness. The outward signs bespeak His divinity, while the heart and secrets have realised His oneness.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Before asking you to bear witness (shahāda), God made you a witness (in the 'ālam al-arwāḥ, the world of spirits). Your outward faculties speak of His divinity; your heart and your innermost secrets have realised His oneness.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: The primordial covenant (mīthāq): all humans bore witness that God is their Lord (Quran 7:172).
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Faith is not a fresh invention. It is the recollection of an ancient testimony. You already know. Life is a remembering, not a discovery.

records.section_title: Chapter 24: The divine presence is primordial
records.french_translation: He honoured you with three gifts: 1) He made you invoke Him and had it not been for His grace, you would not have deserved the flow of invocation in you; 2) He made you remembered by Him because He confirmed His association with you; 3) He made you remembered by those with Him and confirmed His grace on you.
records.english_text_draft: He honoured you with three gifts: 1) He made you invoke Him and had it not been for His grace, you would not have deserved the flow of invocation in you; 2) He made you remembered by Him because He confirmed His association with you; 3) He made you remembered by those with Him and confirmed His grace on you.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Three gifts: 1) to move you to invoke Him (grace), 2) to make you one whom He remembers (a confirmed bond), 3) to make you one remembered by those who are near Him (the angels, the saints).
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: The hadith qudsī: 'He who remembers Me, I remember him'.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Invocation unfolds: first you invoke, then God makes you a subject of His remembrance, then the celestial beings remember you. A gradual ascent.

records.section_title: Chapter 24: The divine presence is primordial
records.french_translation: Some lives are long in terms of years but short in terms of results, while others are short in terms of years but abundant in terms of results.
records.english_text_draft: Some lives are long in terms of years but short in terms of results, while others are short in terms of years but abundant in terms of results.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: The worth of a life lies not in its length but in its spiritual fruits. A short life can be deeply fertile; a long one, barren.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: What matters is the barakah (blessing), not the quantity of time.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Do not seek to 'fill' your life with days, but to 'fill' your days with meaning. A single moment with God is worth more than a year of empty turmoil.

records.section_title: Chapter 24: The divine presence is primordial
records.french_translation: He who has been blessed in his life will get in a shorter span of time gifts from Allah neither words can express nor symbols can allude to.
records.english_text_draft: He who has been blessed in his life will get in a shorter span of time gifts from Allah neither words can express nor symbols can allude to.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: The one who is blessed receives, in a short span, gifts beyond all expression. A single blessed moment is worth more than a long ordinary life.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Barakah compresses time. One night (Laylat al-Qadr) is worth a thousand months.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Seek blessing (barakah) rather than quantity. A spell of concentrated presence yields more than a long routine.

records.section_title: Chapter 24: The divine presence is primordial
records.french_translation: It would be really disappointing if you were unable to move to Allah while free of (worldly) responsibilities, or if you were unable to travel to Allah while having few obstacles.
records.english_text_draft: It would be really disappointing if you were unable to move to Allah while free of (worldly) responsibilities, or if you were unable to travel to Allah while having few obstacles.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: It is a great disappointment not to advance towards God when one is free (with no crushing burdens) and when the obstacles are slight.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: If in ease one does not progress, what will one do in hardship?
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Do not wait for ideal conditions to turn towards God. If you do not move now (in your freedom), you will never move. Spiritual idleness is inexcusable when one has the means.

records.section_title: Chapter 24: The divine presence is primordial
records.french_translation: Thought is the journey of the heart in the realms of the vicissitudes (of creatures).
records.english_text_draft: Thought is the journey of the heart in the realms of the vicissitudes (of creatures).

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Thought (tafakkur) is the journey of the heart across the vicissitudes of created things. It is a movement, a voyage.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Meditation (fikr) is the ship of the heart.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Do not let the heart sit still. The thought that reflects upon the signs of God sends the heart travelling towards Him. Meditation is active, not passive.

records.section_title: Chapter 24: The divine presence is primordial
records.french_translation: Thought is the lamp of the heart. If it goes away, the heart will be devoid of light.
records.english_text_draft: Thought is the lamp of the heart. If it goes away, the heart will be devoid of light.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Meditative thought is the lamp of the heart. Without it, the heart remains in darkness.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: The absence of reflection is the darkness of ignorance and unconsciousness.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Cultivate daily reflection. A heart that does not think (does not meditate) falls asleep, grows numb, loses its light.

records.section_title: Chapter 24: The divine presence is primordial
records.french_translation: There are two types of thoughts: thought about belief and faith and thought about contemplation and vision. The first is the case for reflective thinkers, while the second is the case for contemplative and visionary thinkers.
records.english_text_draft: There are two types of thoughts: thought about belief and faith and thought about contemplation and vision. The first is the case for reflective thinkers, while the second is the case for contemplative and visionary thinkers.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Two kinds of thought: 1) reflection upon belief (for reflective thinkers), 2) thought arising from contemplation and vision (for the contemplatives).
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: The first thinks of God (theology), the second sees God and thinks from out of that vision.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: The highest stage is to 'think from God' (thought flowing out of contemplation), not to 'think about God' (discursive enquiry).

records.section_title: The four letters (appendices)
records.french_translation: Beginnings are the places wherein the ends are revealed. He who begins with Allah will also end up with Him. The wise one is he who is happier with what is eternal rather than with what is transient.
records.english_text_draft: Beginnings are the places wherein the ends are revealed. He who begins with Allah will also end up with Him. The wise one is he who is happier with what is eternal rather than with what is transient.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Letter 1: The beginning determines the end. To begin with God (pure intention) ensures one ends with Him. The wise prefer the eternal to the temporal.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: The importance of the initial intention (niyya).
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Examine the beginnings of your undertakings. If they are for God, their end will be beautiful, even if it appears difficult.

records.section_title: The four letters (appendices)
records.french_translation: If the eye of the heart sees Allah alone in His blessings, the law stipulates that it is necessary to thank His creatures (also). In this, people are of three classes: the absent-minded, the man of spiritual reality, and the best who drinks and becomes more sober.
records.english_text_draft: If the eye of the heart sees Allah alone in His blessings, the law stipulates that it is necessary to thank His creatures (also). In this, people are of three classes: the absent-minded, the man of spiritual reality, and the best who drinks and becomes more sober.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Letter 2: Three kinds of people before blessings: 1) the absent one (sees created things as the source), 2) the man of reality (sees God alone, absent to created things), 3) the best (sees God and thanks created things in Him, sober and present).
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Verse 37:14, 'Be grateful to Me and to your parents', shows the balance.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: The highest degree is not to deny secondary causes, but to see God through them, while honouring the law that commands gratitude towards the intermediary creatures.

records.section_title: The four letters (appendices)
records.french_translation: The eye's delight obtained through contemplation is commensurate with knowledge about the contemplated thing. No one's knowledge is like that of Allah's Messenger, and the delight of anyone's eye is not like that of Allah's Messenger.
records.english_text_draft: The eye's delight obtained through contemplation is commensurate with knowledge about the contemplated thing. No one's knowledge is like that of Allah's Messenger, and the delight of anyone's eye is not like that of Allah's Messenger.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Letter 3: The 'delight of the eyes' in prayer (hadith) depends upon knowledge of the Object contemplated. That of the Prophet is unique, beyond compare.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: The Prophet's prayer is a contemplation of the divine majesty, not a pleasure tied to prayer as a gift.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Do not compare your experience of prayer to that of the Prophet. Rather, seek to contemplate God, not to relish prayer as though it were an object.

records.section_title: The four letters (appendices)
records.french_translation: People are of three categories with respect to (their responses to) the advent of blessings: 1) rejoices in blessings (oublieux), 2) rejoices in the blessings, considering them from their Sender, 3) rejoices in Allah, distracted from blessings by the concentration on Him.
records.english_text_draft: People are of three categories with respect to (their responses to) the advent of blessings: 1) rejoices in blessings (oublieux), 2) rejoices in the blessings, considering them from their Sender, 3) rejoices in Allah, distracted from blessings by the concentration on Him.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Letter 4: Three categories before blessings: 1) one who rejoices in the goods (the materialist), 2) one who rejoices in the blessings as signs of the Giver, 3) one who rejoices in God, to the point of forgetting the blessings.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Verse 6:92, 'Say: God!': one rejoices in Him, not in His gifts.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: The highest degree is joy in God Himself, regardless of what He gives. The blessings turn transparent, and only the Presence counts.

records.section_title: Munājāt — Intimate prayers
records.french_translation: My God, if I am poor in my affluence, how should I not be poor in my poverty?
records.english_text_draft: My God, if I am poor in my affluence, how should I not be poor in my poverty?

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: If even in wealth I am poor (in need of You), how much more so in material poverty. Spiritual poverty is absolute, independent of material conditions.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Ibn 'Ajībah: 'The gnostic is poor towards God whether he be rich or poor, for his poverty is ontological, not economic.'
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Our destitution before God is not a matter of a bank balance. It is an existential condition. Wealth makes us no less poor before Him.

records.section_title: Munājāt — Intimate prayers
records.french_translation: My God, if I am ignorant in my knowledge, how should I not be the stupidest in my ignorance?
records.english_text_draft: My God, if I am ignorant in my knowledge, how should I not be the stupidest in my ignorance?

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: If even when I know I remain ignorant (since my knowledge is so limited before God), what then of my sheer ignorance? The humility of the learned is to acknowledge his relative ignorance.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Zarrūq: 'The ultimate knowledge is to know that one does not know. The scholar heedless of this is the most ignorant.'
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: The more one knows, the more one measures the extent of one's ignorance. Intellectual humility is the mark of the true scholar.

records.section_title: Munājāt — Intimate prayers
records.french_translation: My God, the differences in Your planning and the swift advent of Your preordination prevent Your gnostic servants from finding comfort in (worldly) gifts and from despairing of You during trials.
records.english_text_draft: My God, the differences in Your planning and the swift advent of Your preordination prevent Your gnostic servants from finding comfort in (worldly) gifts and from despairing of You during trials.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: The constant changes in the divine ordering and the swiftness of the decree keep the gnostics from settling into the joy of gifts (which may cease) or the despair of trials (which may also cease).
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Al-Sharqāwī: 'Nothing is stable here below. The wise man floats upon uncertainty, clinging to nothing.'
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: The instability of the world is not a flaw, it is a grace: it keeps us from clinging to what passes away.

records.section_title: Munājāt — Intimate prayers
records.french_translation: My God, I am the source of everything related to my meanness and You are the source of everything related to Your generosity.
records.english_text_draft: My God, I am the source of everything related to my meanness and You are the source of everything related to Your generosity.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: All that is base in me comes from me. All that is generous in You comes from You. A clear distinction between the human origin of evil and the divine origin of good.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: The doctrine of the creation of acts (khalq al-af'āl): the good comes from God, the evil from man.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Own your dark part without projecting it onto God. God is not the author of evil. Generosity, for its part, is His signature.

records.section_title: Munājāt — Intimate prayers
records.french_translation: My God, You made kindness and compassion to me Your attributes even before the existence of my weakness. Then why do You prevent me from them after my weakness?
records.english_text_draft: My God, You made kindness and compassion to me Your attributes even before the existence of my weakness. Then why do You prevent me from them after my weakness?

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: God made kindness and compassion His attributes eternally, before man even existed and was weak. Why then would He deprive man of these attributes after he has become weak?
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: The rhetorical question underscores that divine mercy is prior to any human condition.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: God does not wait for us to be strong in order to be good. His kindness precedes our weakness. It is a reason never to despair.

records.section_title: Munājāt — Intimate prayers
records.french_translation: My God, if virtues come from me it is because of Your grace; so it's up to You to bless me. And if vices come from me it's because of Your justice; so it's up to You to produce proof against me.
records.english_text_draft: My God, if virtues come from me it is because of Your grace; so it's up to You to bless me. And if vices come from me it's because of Your justice; so it's up to You to produce proof against me.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: If virtues come from me, it is by Your grace (for without You I could not). So it falls to You to bless me. If vices come from me, it is by Your justice (for You gave me free will). So it falls to You to bring the proof against me.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: The articulation between grace and justice. Man can neither lay claim to his virtues (grace) nor escape his vices (justice).
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Neither self-satisfaction (since the virtues come from God), nor accusation of God (since the vices are ours under His justice). A stance of humility and responsibility.

records.section_title: Munājāt — Intimate prayers
records.french_translation: My God, how can You entrust me with myself when You are in charge of me? And how can anyone do harm to me when You are my protector, or how can I grow disappointed when You are affectionate to me?
records.english_text_draft: My God, how can You entrust me with myself when You are in charge of me? And how can anyone do harm to me when You are my protector, or how can I grow disappointed when You are affectionate to me?

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: An affectionate questioning: how could God entrust me to myself (which would be my ruin) when He is my Master? How could anyone harm me when He is my protector? How could I despair when He is tender towards me?
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Zarrūq: 'God never entrusts His servant to himself. It is an illusion to believe one is left to oneself.'
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Even in the trial, we are not handed over to ourselves. God is always in charge. Divine protection is never suspended, even when it is unseen.

records.section_title: Munājāt — Intimate prayers
records.french_translation: I make my need of You a means to seek access to You. How can I seek access to You by means of something by which it is impossible to reach You?
records.english_text_draft: I make my need of You a means to seek access to You. How can I seek access to You by means of something by which it is impossible to reach You?

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: The servant makes his need of God a means by which to seek God. But how can one seek God by a means that cannot reach God? The answer: need is not a means, it is a state. God gives Himself beyond all means.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Ibn 'Abbād: 'One does not attain God by anything other than Him. Need is not a ladder, it is a posture.'
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Do not instrumentalise your weakness to 'obtain' God. Need is the place of the encounter, not a technique. God gives Himself freely, not in response to a 'good method'.

records.section_title: Munājāt — Intimate prayers
records.french_translation: My God, how compassionate You are towards me despite my great ignorance, and how merciful You are to me despite my vile deeds.
records.english_text_draft: My God, how compassionate You are towards me despite my great ignorance, and how merciful You are to me despite my vile deeds.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Wonder before the divine compassion despite human ignorance, and the mercy despite base deeds.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: The wonderment (ta'ajjub) of the believer before the goodness of God.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Gratitude is born of awareness of the contrast between our mediocrity and the divine goodness. The more one knows oneself, the more one marvels.

records.section_title: Munājāt — Intimate prayers
records.french_translation: My God, You are very close to me, while I am very distant from You.
records.english_text_draft: My God, You are very close to me, while I am very distant from You.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: A paradox: God is near by essence (nearer than our jugular vein), yet man is distant through his heedlessness, his sins, his forgetting.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: The Quran 50:16: 'We are nearer to him than his jugular vein.'
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Do not accuse God of distance. It is we who go far off. He is always there, at the same absolute nearness. The road to be travelled is within us.

records.section_title: Munājāt — Intimate prayers
records.french_translation: My God, when You are so merciful to me, what veils me from You?
records.english_text_draft: My God, when You are so merciful to me, what veils me from You?

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: A rhetorical question: if God is so merciful, what could veil me from Him? The answer: it is I, my inattention, my attachments.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Zarrūq: 'The veil is not on God's side. The veil is our gaze turned elsewhere.'
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Do not look for an outward veil. The veil is our own distraction. The moment we turn, we see Him. There is no real obstacle.

records.section_title: Munājāt — Intimate prayers
records.french_translation: My God, I know from the diversity of creatures and from the vicissitudes of life that You want me to understand You in everything so that I will not ignore You in anything.
records.english_text_draft: My God, I know from the diversity of creatures and from the vicissitudes of life that You want me to understand You in everything so that I will not ignore You in anything.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: The diversity of created things and the changes of life are signs. God wills that man understand Him through everything, so as to be ignorant of Him in nothing.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: The Quran: 'We shall show them Our signs in the horizons and within themselves' (41:53).
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: The whole world is a book of signs. Nothing is profane. Diversity is not chaos, but divine language.

records.section_title: Munājāt — Intimate prayers
records.french_translation: My God, whenever my ignobility makes me dumb, Your munificence makes me articulate. Whenever my attributes disappoint me, Your grace makes me optimistic.
records.english_text_draft: My God, whenever my ignobility makes me dumb, Your munificence makes me articulate. Whenever my attributes disappoint me, Your grace makes me optimistic.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: The lowliness of the servant reduces him to silence (shame). But the divine munificence restores his speech (praise). His own attributes dishearten him; the divine grace makes him hopeful.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Al-Sharqāwī: 'The penitent alternates between shame at his acts and hope in grace.'
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Do not stay frozen in shame. Shame is just, but it must not be the end. The divine grace is stronger and sets life going again.

records.section_title: Munājāt — Intimate prayers
records.french_translation: My God, if one's virtues are really evils, then why can one's vices not be really virtues? If one's realities are really affectations, why can one's affectations not be really affectations?
records.english_text_draft: My God, if one's virtues are really evils, then why can one's vices not be really virtues? If one's realities are really affectations, why can one's affectations not be really affectations?

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: A questioning of the nature of acts. If our 'virtues' are sometimes vices (pride), why should our vices not sometimes be virtues (humility)? Reality and pretence overlap.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Ibn 'Ajībah: 'Do not judge by appearance. An act may be virtuous in appearance yet vicious in intention, and the reverse.'
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Do not judge hastily. The moral label matters less than the intention and the effect. A humble 'sin' may be nearer to God than a proud 'obedience'.

records.section_title: Munājāt — Intimate prayers
records.french_translation: My God, Your effective verdicts and Your forceful will make the articulate speechless and the important trivial.
records.english_text_draft: My God, Your effective verdicts and Your forceful will make the articulate speechless and the important trivial.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: The divine verdicts (decrees) and the mighty will of God reduce the most eloquent to silence and render insignificant what was important (in the eyes of men).
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Before the divine decree (qadr), all human speech falls silent.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Humility before the inevitable. Before what God has decreed, our discourses, our strategies, our 'importances' collapse. It is a lesson in submission.

records.section_title: Munājāt — Intimate prayers
records.french_translation: My God, my dependence on acts of obedience I built and situations I erected was often razed by Your justice. But it was Your grace that liberated me from it.
records.english_text_draft: My God, my dependence on acts of obedience I built and situations I erected was often razed by Your justice. But it was Your grace that liberated me from it.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: The servant relied upon his acts of obedience and upon the situations he had built. But the divine justice often razed these constructions. It is grace that freed him from this false dependence.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Zarrūq: 'God destroys what man builds, to teach him that He alone is the Builder.'
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: God sometimes frees us by destroying what we relied upon. The ruin of our 'spiritual projects' can be a grace: it brings us back to Him alone.

records.section_title: Munājāt — Intimate prayers
records.french_translation: My God, You know that although my obedience has not persisted as a resolute action, it has persisted as love and firm will.
records.english_text_draft: My God, You know that although my obedience has not persisted as a resolute action, it has persisted as love and firm will.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: The servant confesses that his obedience is not constant (in act), but that love and firm resolve (niyya) are constant.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Ibn 'Abbād: 'Continuous intention is worth the action, even more, when the action falls short.'
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: What matters is not only outward performance, but the constancy of desire and love in the heart. God looks at the intention.

records.section_title: Munājāt — Intimate prayers
records.french_translation: My God, how can I be firm while You are the Omnipotent, or how can I not be firm while You are the Commander?
records.english_text_draft: My God, how can I be firm while You are the Omnipotent, or how can I not be firm while You are the Commander?

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Questions: how could I be firm (by myself) before Your almightiness? Yet how could I fail to be firm when You are my Commander (and You sustain me)?
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Firmness (thabāt) comes from God. Without Him, one wavers; with Him, one holds fast.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Do not lean on your own strength to be firm. But do not despair: with God's command, one can do everything.

records.section_title: Munājāt — Intimate prayers
records.french_translation: My God, my vacillation among creatures makes my destination farther. So connect me with You through a service that guides me to You.
records.english_text_draft: My God, my vacillation among creatures makes my destination farther. So connect me with You through a service that guides me to You.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: The servant's wavering among created things draws him away from his destination (God). He asks God to bind him to Himself through a service ('ibāda) that guides him towards Him.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Al-Sharqāwī: 'One single direction, no dispersion. Divine service recentres.'
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Dispersion (among several masters, several aims) is a waste of time and energy. One service, one direction: God.

records.section_title: Munājāt — Intimate prayers
records.french_translation: My God, how can one take as a proof of You something which depends on You for its existence? Will anything other than You manifest something that You do not have so that it becomes Your manifestation?
records.english_text_draft: My God, how can one take as a proof of You something which depends on You for its existence? Will anything other than You manifest something that You do not have so that it becomes Your manifestation?

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: How could a creature (dependent on God) prove God? Nothing other than He can manifest what He does not possess. God is His own proof.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: The proof of God is God Himself (tajallī). Theological arguments are for beginners.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: One does not prove God as one proves a theorem. He is an inner self-evidence for whoever seeks Him. Do not look for outward proofs.

records.section_title: Munājāt — Intimate prayers
records.french_translation: My God, blind is an eye that cannot see You monitoring it, and unsuccessful is the deal of a servant who has not won a share of Your love.
records.english_text_draft: My God, blind is an eye that cannot see You monitoring it, and unsuccessful is the deal of a servant who has not won a share of Your love.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: The blind one is he who does not see God watching over him. The wretched one is he who has no share in the divine love.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Inner vision (baṣīra) sees the divine presence. True poverty is the absence of love.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: The success or failure of a life is not measured by goods, but by the love of God. Without that love, all is loss.

records.section_title: Munājāt — Intimate prayers
records.french_translation: My God, You have ordered me to go back to creatures. Return me to them, clad in the cloths of lights and with the guidance of inner vision, so that I will return to You from them as I entered You from them, with my secrets being guarded from looking at them and my determination so high that it does not depend on them.
records.english_text_draft: My God, You have ordered me to go back to creatures. Return me to them, clad in the cloths of lights and with the guidance of inner vision, so that I will return to You from them as I entered You from them, with my secrets being guarded from looking at them and my determination so high that it does not depend on them.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: God commands the servant to return to created things (into the world). The request: to return clothed in light, with inner vision, so that this return may be a return to God through them, with no secret attachment, with a resolve that does not depend upon them.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: The return to the world after the spiritual retreat (khalwa) is a stage for the accomplished saint.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: The true saint is not the one who flees the world, but the one who lives in it with God. He is in the world, but not of the world. His heart remains bound to God.

records.section_title: Munājāt — Intimate prayers
records.french_translation: My God, I submit my lowliness in front of You and unveil my situation to You. And from You I seek to join You and by means of You I seek proof of You. Guide me to You with Your light and keep me with sincere servanthood in front of You.
records.english_text_draft: My God, I submit my lowliness in front of You and unveil my situation to You. And from You I seek to join You and by means of You I seek proof of You. Guide me to You with Your light and keep me with sincere servanthood in front of You.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: The servant lays his lowliness and his state before God. He seeks to reach God through God, and to prove God through God. He asks for guidance through the divine light and for constancy in sincere servanthood.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: The perfect circularity: all comes from God and returns to God. The path is in God Himself.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Do not seek God anywhere but in Him. The path, the guide, the proof, the destination: all is He.

records.section_title: Munājāt — Intimate prayers
records.french_translation: My God, teach me from the knowledge of Your treasury and safeguard me with the secret of Your guarded name.
records.english_text_draft: My God, teach me from the knowledge of Your treasury and safeguard me with the secret of Your guarded name.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: A request to be instructed by the knowledge issuing from the divine treasury, and protected by the secret of the preserved Name (ism al-a'ẓam).
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: The 'hidden treasure' (kanz makhfī) is the divine knowledge. The 'guarded Name' is the supreme name of God.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Recognise that true knowledge comes from God alone (a gift), and that protection against evil is also a gift (the Name).

records.section_title: Munājāt — Intimate prayers
records.french_translation: My God, make me discern the realities of those who are closer to You and make me tread the path of those who are in spiritual ecstasy.
records.english_text_draft: My God, make me discern the realities of those who are closer to You and make me tread the path of those who are in spiritual ecstasy.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: A request to discern the realities of the saints (those near to God) and to walk the path of the rapt (majdhūb).
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Discernment (tamyīz) is a grace; the ecstatic way is a divine attraction.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Do not be content merely to know the saints. Discern their reality. And do not be content with the gradual path, but aspire also to the divine attraction.

records.section_title: Munājāt — Intimate prayers
records.french_translation: My God, make Your planning dispense with my planning and make Your preferences for me dispense with my preferences; make me stay along with the centre of my needs.
records.english_text_draft: My God, make Your planning dispense with my planning and make Your preferences for me dispense with my preferences; make me stay along with the centre of my needs.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: May Your ordering (tadbīr) take the place of mine. May Your preferences take the place of mine. Keep me at the centre of my need (that is, in dependence).
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Zarrūq: 'The centre of need' (markaz al-faqr) is the highest station: to want nothing other than God, to expect nothing other than Him.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Total surrender: to hold no plan against the plan of God, to prefer one's own choice to none over His. To remain poor, dependent, available.

records.section_title: Munājāt — Intimate prayers
records.french_translation: My God, take me out of my self-humiliation and cleanse me of my doubts and polytheism before I join my grave. I beseech Your help, so help me. Only You I trust, so do not entrust me (to someone else). Only You I ask, so do not turn me down. Your grace I crave, so do not reject me. To You I belong, so do not banish me. At Your door I stand, so do not cast me off.
records.english_text_draft: My God, take me out of my self-humiliation and cleanse me of my doubts and polytheism before I join my grave. I beseech Your help, so help me. Only You I trust, so do not entrust me (to someone else). Only You I ask, so do not turn me down. Your grace I crave, so do not reject me. To You I belong, so do not banish me. At Your door I stand, so do not cast me off.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: A long and urgent prayer: to come out of self-abasement (which may be false humility), to be purified of doubts and of hidden polytheism (shirk khafī), before death. Manifold requests set in antithesis.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Al-Sharqāwī: 'This prayer sums up the state of the servant who implores everything of God, aware that he can do nothing of himself.'
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: The urgency before death. The purification from doubt and subtle polytheism (claiming merit for oneself, fearing other than God). This is the essential thing.

records.section_title: Munājāt — Intimate prayers
records.french_translation: My God, if Your pleasure is too holy to have any cause from You, how can it have a cause from me? If You are in person independent of any benefit coming to You, why should You not be independent of me?
records.english_text_draft: My God, if Your pleasure is too holy to have any cause from You, how can it have a cause from me? If You are in person independent of any benefit coming to You, why should You not be independent of me?

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: If the divine good pleasure (riḍwān) is too holy to have a cause in God (He is not moved by any external cause), how could it have a cause in me (my acts)? If God is self-sufficient, why would He have need of me?
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Radical gratuitousness. Nothing causes divine acceptance. Nothing is necessary to Him.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Do not instrumentalise your acts to 'earn' the good pleasure of God. He gives out of pure generosity. Good pleasure is not a reward, it is a gift.

records.section_title: Munājāt — Intimate prayers
records.french_translation: My God, fate and divine decree have triumphed over me, and desire with its strong passions hold me prisoner. Be my protector so as to help me and help (others) through me. Enrich me with Your graces, so that I will be too contented to ask You.
records.english_text_draft: My God, fate and divine decree have triumphed over me, and desire with its strong passions hold me prisoner. Be my protector so as to help me and help (others) through me. Enrich me with Your graces, so that I will be too contented to ask You.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: The decree prevails, the passions imprison. God is called upon as protector. A plea for help for oneself and to help others. A request to be so enriched by graces as no longer to need to ask (for one is sufficed).
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Zarrūq: 'The highest state is to need no longer to ask, for one receives continually.'
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Prayer aspires to its own surpassing: to ask never to have to ask again, being fulfilled. This is the paradox of the one who prays.

records.section_title: Munājāt — Intimate prayers
records.french_translation: You illuminated with lights the hearts of Your saints so that they knew You and avowed Your oneness. You kept the vicissitudes away (from them) so that only You they loved and only in You they took refuge.
records.english_text_draft: You illuminated with lights the hearts of Your saints so that they knew You and avowed Your oneness. You kept the vicissitudes away (from them) so that only You they loved and only in You they took refuge.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Praise: God illumines the hearts of the saints with His lights, which lets them know Him and proclaim His oneness. He turns the vicissitudes away from them, so that they love none but Him and take refuge in none but Him.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: The saint is 'preserved' (maḥfūẓ) by God from the distractions of the world.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Sainthood is not a self-construction. It is a gift: God illumines and protects. The saint loves without division and knows no other refuge.

records.section_title: Munājāt — Intimate prayers
records.french_translation: What did he who has lost track of You manage to get? And what is there to lose for one who has found You? Truly he went astray who has considered someone else a substitute for You; and truly he is lost who seeks to stray from You.
records.english_text_draft: What did he who has lost track of You manage to get? And what is there to lose for one who has found You? Truly he went astray who has considered someone else a substitute for You; and truly he is lost who seeks to stray from You.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: He who has lost God, what has he truly gained? He who has found God, what can he lose? To go astray is to seek a substitute for God. To be lost is to wish to turn away from Him.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: The self-evidence: God is the All. The rest is nothing.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Assess your life: if you have lost God, then even with every good, you have gained nothing. If you have found God, then even stripped bare, you have lost nothing. All the rest is incidental.

records.section_title: Munājāt — Intimate prayers
records.french_translation: My God, how can one pin his hope on someone other than You when You have not cut off Your benevolence? How can one beseech someone other than You when You have not changed the habit of bestowing bounties?
records.english_text_draft: My God, how can one pin his hope on someone other than You when You have not cut off Your benevolence? How can one beseech someone other than You when You have not changed the habit of bestowing bounties?

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: A rhetorical question: how can one hope in another than God, when God has never ceased His benevolence? How can one beseech another, when God has not changed His habits as a giver?
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: God is al-Wahhāb (the perpetual Bestower). Others give only through Him.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Spiritual unfaithfulness: to seek elsewhere when the Giver is always there. Man is like a child who would leave his generous parents to beg among strangers.

records.section_title: Munājāt — Intimate prayers
records.french_translation: O He who makes His beloved ones taste the nectar of His intimacy so that they stand in front of Him with admiration, O He who clothes His saints in the attire of reverence to Him so that they stand exulting in His magnificence.
records.english_text_draft: O He who makes His beloved ones taste the nectar of His intimacy so that they stand in front of Him with admiration, O He who clothes His saints in the attire of reverence to Him so that they stand exulting in His magnificence.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Praise to God who gives His beloved ones to taste the nectar of His intimacy, leaving them in wonder before Him. Who clothes His saints in the garment of reverence, making them exult in His magnificence.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: Intimacy (uns) and awe (hayba) are two states of the saints.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: The love of God alternates between intimate sweetness (tender nearness) and reverential awe (majestic magnificence). Both are needful.

records.section_title: Munājāt — Intimate prayers
records.french_translation: You are the invoker before all invokers (You remember them before they remember You); You are benevolent before servants turn to You; You are the most munificent giver before seekers beseech You; and You are the great benefactor who, in exchange of what You have given, seeks a loan from us.
records.english_text_draft: You are the invoker before all invokers (You remember them before they remember You); You are benevolent before servants turn to You; You are the most munificent giver before seekers beseech You; and You are the great benefactor who, in exchange of what You have given, seeks a loan from us.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: God always goes first: He remembers before we invoke. He is benevolent before we turn towards Him. He gives before we ask. And He asks of us a loan (Quran 57:11) upon what He has given us!
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: The loan to God (qarḍ ḥasan): God asks us to spend on the poor, as though it were a loan to Him. A superabundant generosity.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: God is always ahead. He outstrips us in giving. The charity we offer is to repay Him a loan upon His own gift. An astonishing reversal.

records.section_title: Munājāt — Intimate prayers
records.french_translation: My God, seek me with Your grace so that I will join You and draw me (to You) with Your blessings so that I will draw closer to You.
records.english_text_draft: My God, seek me with Your grace so that I will join You and draw me (to You) with Your blessings so that I will draw closer to You.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: A request: that God seek him out by His grace, so that he may reach Him. That God draw him by His blessings, so that he may draw near.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: The divine initiative always precedes. Even man's search for God is a response to the divine call.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Do not believe that you seek God first. It is He who seeks us, draws us, goes before us. Prayer simply asks God to fulfil what He has already promised.

records.section_title: Munājāt — Intimate prayers
records.french_translation: My God, my hope will not be separated from You even if I disobey You, just as my fear will not leave me even if I obey You.
records.english_text_draft: My God, my hope will not be separated from You even if I disobey You, just as my fear will not leave me even if I obey You.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Even while disobeying, hope remains (for God forgives). Even while obeying, fear remains (for the act may not be accepted).
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: A permanent balance: neither despair, nor false security.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Hold both: hope within the fault, fear within the obedience. This is the path of sincerity.

records.section_title: Munājāt — Intimate prayers
records.french_translation: My God, the world pushed me to You, and it is my knowledge about Your benevolence that made me stand in front of You.
records.english_text_draft: My God, the world pushed me to You, and it is my knowledge about Your benevolence that made me stand in front of You.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: The world (its hardships) drove me towards You. The knowledge of Your benevolence made me stay before You.
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: The world repels, God attracts. Knowledge stabilises.
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: Trials have a positive function: they drive us towards God. But only the knowledge of His goodness makes us stay. Without that knowledge, we leave again.

records.section_title: Munājāt — Intimate prayers
records.french_translation: My God, how can I attach glory to myself while You have positioned me in lowliness, or why should I not attach glory to myself if You have associated me with You? Why should I not be in need (of You) if it is You who placed me in poverty, or why should I be in need if You have enriched me with Your generosity? There is no God but You.
records.english_text_draft: My God, how can I attach glory to myself while You have positioned me in lowliness, or why should I not attach glory to myself if You have associated me with You? Why should I not be in need (of You) if it is You who placed me in poverty, or why should I be in need if You have enriched me with Your generosity? There is no God but You.

commentary_draft.explanation_fr: Questions on honour: how can I claim glory for myself when You have set me in abasement? Yet why should I have no glory if You associate me with Yourself? Questions on need: why should I not be in need if You have set me in poverty? Yet why should I be in need if You have enriched me?
commentary_draft.classical_sources_note: The servant's condition is paradoxical: lowered by himself (sin), raised by God (grace).
commentary_draft.modern_insight_fr: The human being is a paradox: base in himself, noble through God. Do not absolutise either. See yourself as dust, yet loved by God.

_note_en: Aphorisms shown in English (english_text_draft). Pedagogical commentary still to be translated (next batch).
